<a id="visualizer-dashboards"></a>

## Visualizer Dashboards

> 📄 Source: [Book_VS_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_VS_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm)

### Visualizer Dashboards


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="visualizer-dashboards-overview"></a>

### Visualizer Dashboards Overview

> 📄 Source: [VS_Visualizer_Dashboards_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Visualizer_Dashboards_Overview.htm)

#### Visualizer Dashboards Overview


Visualizer Dashboards provide a visual perspective over large volumes of EDI (X12, HIPAA) and non-EDI (HL7, Positional Flat Files, and so on) data without any special ETL tools, expensive database, and reporting infrastructure. When you visualize thousands of related items at once, you can see trends and patterns that would otherwise not be apparent when you look at one item at a time. The Visualizer dashboards display full, high-resolution, highly customizable graphical charts with short load times, while the animations and natural transitions provide context and prevent you from being overwhelmed by large quantities of information. As an analyst, there are several business reasons to visualize your data in this way. For example, you can analyze data to detect abnormal activity with claim amounts. To do this, you can gather all the EDI claims (837 Professional, Dental, or Institutional) made on a particular day or coming from a certain provider to confirm the claim amounts are legitimate. You can also analyze trends in the enrollment of partners over a month or more to determine a pattern, if one exists.


Technically, you can work either with the raw EDI data or visualize LDNS reports, if you need to preserve custom configuration like Guideline edits, severity configuration, and so on. This makes it easy for you to interact with massive amounts of data in ways that are powerful and informative.


SpecBuilder uses Kibana 4.x to aggregate data and highlight the required information in the form of dashboards. Kibana is an open source analytics and search dashboard that operates using Elasticsearch. With Kibana Dashboards, you can combine multiple visualizations on a single page and filter them using your custom criteria. For more information on Kibana, see [Kibana documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/kibana/4.0/index.html).


SpecBuilder allows you to visualize LDNS reports in XML, CSV (DFF) files - text files that contain fields and records separated by certain delimiters, and raw data - data files used to generate LDNS reports internally before uploading. You can accumulate statistics on data validation results and important business data, and then view them in the form of informative graphs.


The following diagram shows you the visualization work flow.



Visualizer Server supports the upload of data from multiple instances of SpecBuilder and XEServer.



To view the Visualizer dashboards:

1. Click File, in the Backstage view that appears, click New, and then click Visualizer Dashboard.


Note You must have a connection to Visualizer Server. For more information, see [Manage Configurations](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Manage_Configurations.htm).


Note By default, Visualizer Server is installed locally during the SpecBuilder installation. You can also work with standalone Visualizer Server installed on a network workstation.

---

<a id="manage-configurations"></a>

### Manage Configurations

> 📄 Source: [VS_Manage_Configurations.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Manage_Configurations.htm)

#### Manage Configurations


To access Visualizer Dashboards, you can configure SpecBuilder by providing a Visualizer Server URL and a security token. For more information, see [Security Tokens](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Security_Tokens.htm).


You can configure SpecBuilder using the Import Connection Configuration File option in the Connection Manager dialog available on the Advanced tab of the ribbon. To do this, acquire a connection configuration file generated during the installation of Visualizer Server. If Visualizer Server is not installed locally, get the connection configuration from the Visualizer Server administrator.


With the Connection Manager dialog, you can import and edit connection settings, add and delete connections.


To configure connections to Visualizer Servers:

1. On the Advanced tab of the ribbon, click Connection Manager.
2. In the Connection Manager dialog, the following options are available:


To import connection settings:

1. In the Connection Manager dialog, click Import connection from Connection Configuration file.
2. Navigate to an archive that contains the connection configuration.
3. Click Open.


To add a connection:

1. In the Connection Manager dialog, click Add a new connection.
2. Fill in the Connection Name, Server URL and SecurityToken fields.
3. Select Validate Certificate to identify a server using a certificate when you establish an SSL connection. In Certificate Thumbprint, navigate to the .pem certificate located in the Connection Configuration archive. Note that you must extract the file from the archive first.
4. Click OK.


Note The name of the connection you use is displayed in the name of the Visualizer tab.

---

<a id="security-tokens"></a>

### Security Tokens

> 📄 Source: [VS_Security_Tokens.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Security_Tokens.htm)

#### Security Tokens


A security token is a unique character string required to authorize access to Visualizer Server. There are two types of security tokens:

- For full access: Allows you to upload data to Visualizer Server, configure, and view dashboards.
- For read-only access: Allows you to view existing dashboards.


You can find security tokens in the Connection Configuration archive that includes a .pem certificate and a connection.properties file that contains a hostname, port number, and an actual security token. A token archive is generated during the installation of Visualizer Server. However, you can overwrite the default token by generating a new one. The directory with default security tokens is specified during the installation process. By default, this directory location is the following:C:\Users\<YourUsername>\Documents\Edifecs\VisualizerServer\tokens.

---

<a id="before-you-begin"></a>

### Before You Begin

> 📄 Source: [VS_Before_You_Begin.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Before_You_Begin.htm)

#### Before You Begin


On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, you can create new dashboards, launch them in Kibana, and export dashboards as packages. With a dashboard, you can combine multiple visualizations on a single page. Using visualizations, you can explore your data; create charts to see trends and patterns of your data items. Dashboards allow you to get an overview of your data and make correlations among various visualizations.


Before you begin working with Visualizer Dashboards on the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, follow the steps in the presented order:

1. On the Templates page, select Base Visualizer Package. For more information, see [Manage Configuration Packages](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Manage_Configuration_Packages.htm).
2. On the Your Dashboards page, create a new dashboard. For more information, see [Create Visualizer Dashboards](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm).
3. In the Upload Data Wizard step, upload your data. For more information, see [Upload Data](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Upload_Data.htm).
4. Start analyzing your data. On the Dashboard tab of Kibana, you can see the dashboard you created. For more information, see [Create Configuration Packages](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Configuration_Packages.htm).


Note You can work with Visualizer Server only using SpecBuilder and/or [XESManager](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Book_Visualizer_Server.htm) user interfaces.

---

<a id="quick-start"></a>

### Quick Start

> 📄 Source: [VS_Quick_Start_Guide.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Quick_Start_Guide.htm)

#### Quick Start


The following procedure describes a common case of dashboard creation by using the Base Visualizer Package that is distributed with Visualizer Server.


To create a new dashboard:

1. On the Templates page, click the Base Visualizer Package folder, and then in Dashboard Templates, select the dashboard template you need. For example, EDI Error Troubleshooting.
2. Click Create Dashboard.
3. In the Create new Data Store dialog, enter a name and description for a new data store, and then click Create Data Store.
4. Click Next.
5. In the Create new Dashboard dialog, enter a name and description for a new dashboard, and then click Finish.
6. In the Upload Data Wizard step, click Add files (), navigate to the data file you want to analyze, and then click Open.
7. Click Process. In the Visualizer Uploads dialog, you can see your data being processed.
8. Click Hide. On the Dashboard tab of Kibana, you can see the dashboard you created.


The following sample dashboard graphs are created by using the EDI Error Troubleshooting dashboard template. The pie chart and two histograms display errors categorized by severity, SNIP type, and IDs.



For more information about dashboard creation, see [Work with Visualizer Dashboards](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_VS_Work_with_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm).

---

<a id="work-with-visualizer-dashboards"></a>

### Work with Visualizer Dashboards

> 📄 Source: [Book_VS_Work_with_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_VS_Work_with_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm)

#### Work with Visualizer Dashboards


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="work-with-data-stores"></a>

#### Work with Data Stores

> 📄 Source: [Book_VS_Work_with_Data_Stores.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_VS_Work_with_Data_Stores.htm)

##### Work with Data Stores


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="create-data-stores"></a>

##### Create Data Stores

> 📄 Source: [VS_Create_Data_Stores.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Data_Stores.htm)

###### Create Data Stores


Visualizer dashboards show you aggregations against your data. You can upload your data in buckets to simplify the data management. For example, you can choose to upload today’s data and yesterday’s data separately to analyze it together and then separately to compare the results. In any case, all your data is in the same storage and in the same format - Data Store, as if it is stored in a database table. Depending on your business needs, you can create several Data Stores.


Before you begin uploading data for visualization, create a data store to upload your data to. Technically, a data store is a place in the repository for storing data extracted from an LDNS report. Multiple pieces of data of the same format can be loaded into the same data store.


The following is an extract of a sample LDNS report:


Example


...


<GroupNode Name="PatientEvent" GUID="{EB5ECBB6-3553-4F75-85A7-36803B8AFA83}" ChildNodeReceived="3" StatusCode="R" ChildNodeAccepted="0" ChildNodeRejected="3" ChildNodePartial="0">


<DataPointer Offset="443" Size="560"/>


<Fields>


<Field Type="Property" Name="PatientEventHLIndex">4</Field>


<Field Type="Property" Name="RequestCategoryCode">HS</Field>


<Field Type="Property" Name="ProcedureCodes"> A0428</Field>


<Field Type="Property" Name="CertificationTypeCode">R</Field>


<Field Type="Property" Name="EventDate">20050510</Field>


<Field Type="Property" Name="PatientEventDate">20050510</Field>


<Field Type="Property" Name="DiagnosisCodes"/>


<Field Type="Property" Name="ServiceTypeCodes">56</Field>


</Fields>


...


On the Data Stores page, you can add new, edit, delete, or optimize existing data stores. You can use existing data store templates for data store creation. A data store template is a JSON configuration that includes an Elasticsearch field's declaration and configurations for XML/DFF parsers. Data Store templates allow you to define settings that are automatically applied when you create new Data Stores. Templates provide instructions for data parsing, describe how to save data to the Elasticsearch and display it in Kibana. The templates include both settings and mappings, and a simple pattern template that controls whether the template should be applied to the new index.


Note Templates are only applied when you create a Data Store. If you change a template, it does not have an impact on existing Data Stores.


The following is a sample extract from the Data Store template that provides instructions for Elasticsearch and Kibana.


Example


"fields": {


"ClaimTraceNumber": {


"type": "string",


"analyzed": false


},


"ClaimChargeAmount": {


"type": "double",


"analyzed": false,


"formatter": {


"id": "number",


"pattern": "0,0.[00]"


}},


"StatusDate": {


"type": "date",


"analyzed": false,


"formatter": {


"id": "date",


"pattern": "MMMM Do YYYY"


}},…


For more information, see [Configuration Package Structure](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Configuration_Package_Structure.htm).


To create a new data store using a template:

1. On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, on the Data Stores page, click Create New, and then click Choose a Template. A list of data store templates is displayed.
2. Select a template you want to use, and then click Next.
3. On the Create new Data Store page, in Name New Data Store, enter a name for a new data store, and then in Describe New Data Store, enter a description.
4. Click Finish. A new data store is created.


To create a data store from the beginning, you must define the mapping conditions for the fields extracted by a parser. Mapping is the process that defines how data and the fields it contains are stored. For example, mappings can define which string fields are treated as full text fields, which fields contain names, numbers, or dates, the format of date values.


This means that you must define a way to store data that was extracted from the LDNS report to the Elasticsearch storage. For every piece of data, extracted by the parser, you must create a data store field with the corresponding data type to store it in the Elasticsearch storage. An Elasticsearch field is a cell in the Elasticsearch storage that keeps a single piece of business data extracted from an LDNS report. Each field has a data type that can be simple (text, keyword, date, long, boolean, and so on), a type that supports the hierarchical format of JSON (object or nested), or a specialized type (geo_point, geo_shape, or completion).


To create a new data store from the beginning:

1. On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, on the Data Stores page, click Create New.
2. Click Start From Blank. The Configure Data Store mapping page is displayed.
3. Add as many fields as you need to match the structure of the parser you use. For every field, provide a name, and select the corresponding data type, and then click Next. The Configure parsers page is displayed.
4. Depending on the data type, add the corresponding parser (LDNS or DFF) in the JSON format, and then click Next. The Name Data Store template page is displayed.
5. In Name New Data Store Template enter a name for the newly created template, and then in Describe New Data Store Template, add a description.
6. Click Finish. The new template is available in the list of data store templates.
7. On the Create new Data Store page, in Name New Data Store enter a name for the newly created data store, and then in Describe New Data Store, add a description.
8. Click Finish. A new data store has been created.

---

<a id="data-store-mapping-parameters"></a>

##### Data Store Mapping Parameters

> 📄 Source: [VS_Data_Store_Mapping_Parameters.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Data_Store_Mapping_Parameters.htm)

###### Data Store Mapping Parameters


When creating a data store from the beginning or when editing an existing data store template, you can define optional field parameters: Analyzed field and Kibana format.



The Analyzed field option is available only for fields of the data type String. If you select this option, the current field is "split" by a white space character during parsing. When performing a search, such split substrings are compared independently, and so search results may contain partial matches. If you clear this option, the search result contains only strict matches.


Example


Data store field value:


"John Smith"


Possible search queries:


"John Smith",


"Jacob Smith"*,


"John A"*, and so on.


*On condition that the marked results are also analyzed fields.


With the Kibana format option, you can control the way in which data store field values are displayed in Kibana graphs. The following format options are available in the Configure Data Store Mapping dialog:


Format type: String

- To upper case: Displays the current field name in upper case.
- To lower case: Displays the current field name in lower case.
- From Base 64: Displays the current field name after it is converted from the Base64 format.


There are additional formatting parameters specific to limited data types. For example, the date formatter for the date data type.

- Formatter pattern: Enter a date pattern for displaying date fields in Kibana graphs.


###### Date pattern samples:


| MMMM Do YYYY, HH:mm:ss.SSS (Default pattern) | January 1st 1970, 00:00:00.000 |
| --- | --- |
| YYYY MM DD | 1970 01 01 |
| YYYY MMMM DD | 1970 January 01 |
| HH:mm:ss | 14:27:31 |



 


Example


The following example demonstrates how to set the data format for the visualization. Date fields are displayed according to the pattern YYYY MMMM DD.


Kibana format:


Format type: "date"


Formatter pattern: "YYYY MMMM DD"


 


Kibana graph:



For more information on Kibana, see [Kibana documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/kibana/4.0/index.html).

---

<a id="data-store-optimization"></a>

##### Data Store Optimization

> 📄 Source: [VS_Data_Store_Optimization.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Data_Store_Optimization.htm)

###### Data Store Optimization


All data in the Data Store are distributed among segments. A segment is a fragment of a Data Store, and the more segments there are in the storage, the less efficient the search engine performance is. Excessive storage segmentation impairs the storing process rate. To avoid this, you can optimize your data stores in the Elasticsearch storage by merging available segments.


Caution The optimization process is resource-intensive. Although it is performed in the background, it affects other processes and slows down the system. Therefore, take measures to plan the optimization of the Elasticsearch storage to avoid an adverse impact on your business environment.


To optimize a data store:

1. On the Data Stores page, select a data store you want to optimize, and then click .
2. Click Confirm to initialize the optimization process.

---

<a id="create-visualizer-dashboards"></a>

#### Create Visualizer Dashboards

> 📄 Source: [VS_Create_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm)

##### Create Visualizer Dashboards


On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, you can create new dashboards, launch them in Kibana, and export dashboards as packages. With a dashboard, you can combine multiple visualizations on a single page. You can filter them using a search query or filters by clicking elements in the visualization. Dashboards allow you to get an overview of your logs and make correlations among various visualizations and logs. Visualizations are based on Elasticsearch queries. With a series of Elasticsearch aggregations, you can extract and process your data to create charts that show trends, dips, and spikes that you need to know about. For more information on using dashboards, see [Create Configuration Packages](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Configuration_Packages.htm).


Your Dashboards page allows you to manage your dashboards, perform searches and sorting.


###### Dashboard Toolbar


You can perform the following operations using the toolbar:


| Option | Description |
| --- | --- |
| | Click to refresh the list of available dashboards. |
| | Click to export several dashboards as one package. |
| | Click to launch several dashboards in Kibana. |
| | Click to create a new dashboard. A dashboard is created using a template. A dashboard template is a JSON configuration used as a base for new dashboards. A template includes configurations for several visualizations. |



Select a dashboard to view details. To edit a dashboard, click . You can rename dashboards, change dashboard descriptions, and upload images () to change the dashboard template icon.


To combine multiple visualizations on a single page, create a new dashboard:

1. On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, on the Your Dashboards page, click Create New.
2. From the template list, provided with the package, select the dashboard you want to use as a base, and then click Next. You can use one of the pre-built configuration templates distributed with SpecBuilder or import a new configuration to use a pre-defined collection of visualizations in a new dashboard. For more information, see [Manage Configuration Packages](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Manage_Configuration_Packages.htm).
3. From the data store template list, select a data store to be used by the dashboard or create a new data store and click Next. For more information, see [Create Data Stores](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Data_Stores.htm).
4. Enter a name and a description for the new dashboard, and then click Finish.
5. After you have created a new dashboard, in the Upload Data pop-up dialog box, you can upload data to view on the dashboard or skip this dialog to upload data to a data store later. For more information, see [Upload Data](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Upload_Data.htm).


You can access recently created or open dashboards. For this, on the Advanced tab of the ribbon, in the Visualizer group, click Dashboards and select a dashboard you want to display from the list of the last ten dashboards you worked on.

---

<a id="upload-data"></a>

#### Upload Data

> 📄 Source: [VS_Upload_Data.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Upload_Data.htm)

##### Upload Data


After you have created the Data Store, and there is a structured container to store and upload the data you want to analyze by using Visualizer Server, you can upload data of one of the following source types, to analyze using Visualizer dashboards:

- LDNS report: LDNS reports generated by SpecBuilder or XEServer in the .xml format.
- CSV / DFF: Data in the DFF format.
- Raw data: EDI data files. Visualizer Server does not process this data, and so XEngine processes your data and generates an LDNS report implicitly. Visualizer Server extracts the data from the resulting LDNS report.


Note SpecBuilder supports processing of the following raw data types: HIPAA 4010A1, 5010, 5010A1 - all transactions; X12 4010 – transactions 810, 850, 856, 204, 214.


Note If you process raw data of the HIPAA standard, make sure you have the HIPAA Standards Database 8.8 or later installed for accurate LDNS report generation.


To upload data:

1. On the Advanced tab of the ribbon, in the Visualizer group, click Upload Data. The Upload Data wizard is displayed.
2. In the Upload Data dialog box, select one of the following options:
3. On the toolbar, click Add files () or Add folder ( ) to add a file or a file folder to be uploaded to a selected data store. You can add several files or folders.
4. Click Process.


If you select the CSV/DFF source data type, the Configure option becomes enabled and you can provide additional DFF settings in the DFF Settings dialog:



In the DFF Settings dialog, you can optionally set the following options:

- Field separator: Select the field separator used in your DFF data file - Comma, Pipe or Tab, or type your custom separator.
- HEX representation: Enable to view a filed separator as hexadecimals.
- Skip first record: Enable to ignore the header record during parsing.
- Field indexes: Select the data file fields that you want to extract, and in Index, enter the position of the field in the source file.


Note A record terminator is detected automatically.


Records of your upload processes are displayed in the Visualizer Uploads dialog. For more information, see [Manage Uploads](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Manage_Uploads.htm).

---

<a id="manage-uploads"></a>

#### Manage Uploads

> 📄 Source: [VS_Manage_Uploads.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Manage_Uploads.htm)

##### Manage Uploads


The Visualizer Uploads dialog displays your records of upload processes, statuses of your uploads, and the number of received, accepted, and rejected records. You can select an upload to view a list of files being uploaded within a selected upload. By using a toolbar, you can delete an upload record by clicking Cancel () or by clicking Stop () to interrupt the upload. In this case, only those files that have been completely uploaded are added to the data store.


The Visualizer Uploads dialog pops up after you complete the Upload Data wizard. To skip this dialog, disable the Pop up the dialog when uploading process starts option. To open this dialog, on the Advanced tab of the ribbon, in the Visualizer group, click Uploads.



Note The upload process is not persistent and gets interrupted when SpecBuilder is closed. The upload is not resumed automatically when SpecBuilder is launched next. The Visualizer Uploads dialog also gets reset when SpecBuilder is re-launched.


Use , to save a log of the selected upload. A log is saved as an XML file that includes details on the number of received, accepted, rejected records. If there are errors, they too are reflected in the log.


Your records or whole files can be rejected for reasons described below:

1. When data cannot be parsed, the reasons can be the following:

- Your LDNS report is not a valid .xml file, or is not an LDNS report (it does not have the <DataReport> root element).
- Your DFF/CSV data file does not have the expected formatting.
- The Date format in your LDNS report, DFF/CSV, or raw data file does not correspond to the expected format. For example, if the Data Store definition defines the Date type for the ErrorDate field, and the parser is configured to process the date in the MMDDYYYYformat, and if your data contains date in the MMDDYY, it cannot be parsed.

1. When data cannot be stored in the Data Store, the reasons can be the following: Data extracted from your source file is not compatible with the Data Store you save this data to. For example, a Data Store has the ClaimChargeAmount field with the Double type, and a source data file contains a string in this field. The whole record cannot be saved in Data Store due to the incompatible field format. Note: The parsing error does not appear in this case since it does not check data, except for dates, because they are extracted according to the format.

1. XEngine cannot generate an LDNS report from the raw data file you uploaded because of XEngine failures caused by an incompatible Java version.


If none of the above-mentioned errors occurred, but no records were made to the Data Store, SpecBuilder marks such files with "warning" in the Visualizer Uploads dialog to inform you that you need to check them. The potential issues can be the following:

- If you upload an LDNS report or raw data file that is not compatible with the current Data Store. For example, the Data Store is used for storing information on claims of the 837 Professional transaction. If the LDNS report you upload is generated based on data of the 835 transaction, then there is a mismatch because it does not contain the needed data.
- If the LDNS report or raw data file you upload is not complete because of envelope segments errors. For example, you upload 837 Professional claims, and the source data contain an error on the interchange level. In this case, data from the transaction level cannot be added to the LDNS report, since XEngine does not process transactions with envelope segments errors.
- If you upload raw data and the generated LDNS report is not complete because the guideline it uses is not supported, or the HIPAA Standards Database is not available.
- If you ignore the warning when you upload a file that does not contain a payload to extract. For example, if Data Store contains information about transaction errors, but the uploaded raw data file does not contain any errors. The LDNS report generated for this raw data file will not have any errors to extract.

---

<a id="manage-configuration-packages"></a>

#### Manage Configuration Packages

> 📄 Source: [VS_Manage_Configuration_Packages.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Manage_Configuration_Packages.htm)

##### Manage Configuration Packages


On the Templates page, you can view all imported packages and custom data store templates. You can also import and export packages. SpecBuilder includes configuration packages for the most common business tasks. You can use the pre-created out-of-the-box templates and pre-built dashboards.


###### Manage Packages


To import a new package:

1. Open the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, click Templates and then click Import New.
2. At the bottom of the page, click Choose File, and then navigate to the package archive.
3. Click Import. The package is now imported and available in the template list.


To export a package:

1. On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, click Templates.
2. Select a package you want to export. You can also export your custom data store templates as one package.
3. Click . The package is exported to the file system as a .zip archive.


For more information on packages, see [Work with Configuration Packages](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_VS_Work_with_Configuration_Packages.htm).

---

<a id="work-with-kibana"></a>

#### Work with Kibana

> 📄 Source: [Book_VS_Work_with_Kibana.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_VS_Work_with_Kibana.htm)

##### Work with Kibana


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="kibana-discover-tab"></a>

##### Kibana Discover Tab

> 📄 Source: [VS_Kibana_Discover_Tab.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Kibana_Discover_Tab.htm)

###### Kibana Discover Tab


Kibana is an open source platform for analytics and visualization designed to work with the Elasticsearch repository. Kibana allows you to view, search, and interact with data stored in the Data Store and visualize large volumes of data in a variety of charts, tables, and maps.


With the Discover tab of Kibana, you can interactively explore your data, perform search queries, filter the results, and find a data file in indexes that fit the selected index pattern.


Note An index is a logical namespace in an Elasticsearch storage for storing data extracted from an LDNS report. In Visualizer Server, indexes are called Data Stores.


You can also view the number of data files that match the search query and receive field value statistics. The current index pattern is displayed in the left pane of Kibana. To use the Discover tab, on the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, click Launch Kibana.


To search for the indexes that match the current index pattern, submit a search on the Discover tab. To submit a search, use simple query strings, the [Lucene query syntax](https://lucene.apache.org/core/2_9_4/queryparsersyntax.html) for simple requests or [Elasticsearch Query DSL](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/1.7/query-dsl.html) for more complex conditions.


After submitting a query, the Available Fields pane, the documents table update automatically to show the search results. You can find the number of matching documents (hits) in the upper right corner below the toolbar. By default, the documents table reflects the first five hundred query results in the reverse sort order, with the most recent documents at the top of the table. Also you can sort documents by the values of any field.


To submit a search query:

1. Enter a query in the search field.
2. Click to view the results.


To start a new search:


In the Discover toolbar, click New Search ().


To load a saved search:

1. In the Discover toolbar, click Load Saved Search ().
2. Click the needed search query.


Note If the selected search does not match the current index pattern, the index pattern automatically changes according to the search query.


To narrow the search results:


In the Kibana left pane, in the Available Fields pane, select the needed fields and click .


You can see the specified fields in the Selected Fields pane.


To change the searched indexes:

1. At the top of the Kibana left pane, below the search field, click the current index pattern name. The list of available index patterns expands.
2. Click a different index pattern. The Available fields pane displays new indexes that match the selected index pattern.


With the Discover tab, you can also perform the following actions:

- Filter by the field: You can configure positive or negative filters to include or exclude data with a particular field value. You can add a filter from the Selected Fields, Available Fields lists, and the documents pane. After you create a filter, the filter conditions toolbar is displayed below the search field.
- Work with filters: You can disable (you disable the filter without removing it and you can enable it again), pin (the pinned filter is available across all Kibana tabs), toggle (the inclusion filter turns to the exclusion one), and remove filters (you can remove a filter completely).
- View document data: You can configure view settings of the documents that meet the query's demands, and expand their field data in the table and JSON formats. You can add fields to the documents pane from the left Kibana pane, remove fields, and sort data by any selected indexed field.
- View field data statistics: The fields list enables you to see the number of documents containing a particular field, with the top five values and the percentage of documents including each value. You can also create a separate visualization based on a particular field.


For more information about the Discover tab of Kibana, see [Kibana official documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/kibana/4.1/discover.html).

---

<a id="kibana-visualize-tab"></a>

##### Kibana Visualize Tab

> 📄 Source: [VS_Kibana_Visualize_Tab.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Kibana_Visualize_Tab.htm)

###### Kibana Visualize Tab


With the Visualize tab, you can create data visualizations, which you can combine into a dashboard or use separately. To use the Visualize tab, on the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, click Launch Kibana and then click Visualize.


By using the aggregation that extracts and processes your data, you can build charts and tables that show you the information you need. You can open a saved visualization template, or start building a new visualization.


To create a new visualization:

1. On the Visualize tab, choose the type of visualization you want to create.
2. Select a new or saved search source.


To manage saved visualizations:

1. On the Settings tab, click Objects.
2. On the Edit Saved Objects page, click the Visualizations tab, and then select the visualization you want to modify.


For more information on the Visualize tab, see [Kibana official documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/kibana/4.1/visualize.html).

---

<a id="work-with-filters"></a>

##### Work with Filters

> 📄 Source: [VS_Work_with_Filters.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Work_with_Filters.htm)

###### Work with Filters


If you want to display or exclude documents containing a particular field value, you can filter your search results.


To add filters on the Dashboard tab:

1. Open the required dashboard or [create a new dashboard](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm).
2. Click any interactive part of a visualization (for example, a slice in a “Pie” visualization, or a row in a table).


To add filters on the Discover tab:

1. On the Discover tab of Kibana, in the left pane, click the required field name. Next to the required field value, click to add a positive filter and filter out documents without the specified value in the field, or to add a negative filter and exclude documents with the specified value in the field.
2. On the Discover tab of Kibana, in the right pane with the documents table, click to expand the document. Next to the required field name, click to add a positive filter, or to add a negative filter.


After you create a filter, the filter conditions toolbar is displayed below the Search field.


To work with a filter, hover over the filter to see the available options:

1. : Disables the filter without removing the filter. Enabled by default.
2. : Pins the filter and makes the filter available across all Kibana tabs.
3. : Turns the inclusion filter to the exclusion filter.
4. : Removes the filter completely.
5. : Opens a dialog to customize the filter.


You can also set a time filter to get search results of a specific time period.


To set a time filter:

1. Open the required dashboard or [create a new dashboard](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm).
2. On the tab bar, click to expand the time filter options.
3. Click Quick and choose the available time period.

---

<a id="kibana-usage-tips"></a>

##### Kibana Usage Tips

> 📄 Source: [VS_Kibana_Usage_Tips.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Kibana_Usage_Tips.htm)

###### Kibana Usage Tips


The following are the frequently asked questions while working with Kibana.

 What is Kibana?


Kibana is an open source platform that allows you to visualize data in the form of tables, charts, and maps, and also to perform advanced data analysis. Kibana is integrated with Visualizer Server, which allows you to apply all its capabilities to the data stored in the Data Stores of Visualizer Server.





 How do I look for my data?


To look for your data, in the Search field, enter a query string. For more information on possible search queries, view the Help topic [Discover Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Kibana_Discover_Tab.htm#To).





 How do I save and load my search parameters?


To save your search parameters, on the Discover tab, on the toolbar, click . Enter a search name, and then click Save.


To load the search parameters that you saved, on the Discover tab, on the toolbar, click , and then choose the search to load.





 How do I filter search results?


If you want to display or exclude documents containing a particular field value, you can filter your search results. For instructions, view the Help topic [Work with Filters](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Work_with_Filters.htm).





 How do I work with filters?


To work with a filter, hover over the filter to see the available options. You can enable/disable, pin, toggle, and remove filters, as well as customize them.


For more information, view the Help topic [Discover Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Kibana_Discover_Tab.htm#Work_with_Filters).





 What is a time filter?


A time filter allows you to get the search results of a specific time period. For instructions, view the Help topic [Work with Filters](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Work_with_Filters.htm).





 How do I create a visualization?


You can create visualizations of several types on the Visualize tab of Kibana. For instructions, view the Help topic [Visualize Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Kibana_Visualize_Tab.htm).





 How do I create a dashboard?


You can create a dashboard to combine multiple visualizations on a single page. For instructions, view the Help topic [Create Visualizer Dashboards](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Visualizer_Dashboards.htm).





 Can I save and load my dashboards?


Yes, you can. To save your dashboard, click on the toolbar. In the Save As field, enter a name for your dashboard, and then click Save.


To load a saved dashboard, click on the toolbar, and then select the required dashboard from the list of available dashboards.





 Can I customize existing dashboards?


Yes, you can. You can move dashboard elements (or containers) by using the drag-and-drop operation, re-size them by dragging the bottom right corner, or remove dashboard elements by clicking .





 Where do I find more information on Kibana capabilities?


You can get detailed information on all the features of the Kibana platform from the official Kibana documentation portal. Just enter "Kibana 4.5 User Guide" in the Web search engine you prefer.

<a id="work-with-configuration-packages"></a>

##### Work with Configuration Packages

> 📄 Source: [Book_VS_Work_with_Configuration_Packages.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_VS_Work_with_Configuration_Packages.htm)

<a id="configuration-packages-overview"></a>

##### Configuration Packages Overview

> 📄 Source: [VS_Configuration_Packages_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Configuration_Packages_Overview.htm)

###### Configuration Packages Overview


A combination of all configurations required to use Visualizer Server such as Data Stores, Dashboards, and so on can be gathered into a single package to simplify distribution and delivery. This section contains advanced information on how to create new and/or modify existing distribution packages.


Note You do not need to create your own configuration package to use Visualizer dashboards. Skip this section unless you need to create your custom package.


SpecBuilder handles incoming source files and builds graphs based on imported packages. A package is a set of JSON configurations that define which parts of LDNS reports should be stored in the Elasticsearch storage and how they should be visualized. A package includes configurations for data stores and dashboards.


Out of the box SpecBuilder provides packages for major Healthcare transactions as well as for some general industry X12 transactions. If you need an additional package that best satisfies your business needs, contact [Edifecs Support](https://support.edifecs.com).


A typical package includes the following folders:

- dashboards: Contains dashboard templates.
- indexes: Contains data store templates.
- visualizations: Contains configurations for Kibana graphs.
- package-definition.json: Describes the contents of the current package and the relationship between data stores and dashboards.


Download the distribution package:

1. After you receive a package, import it to SpecBuilder. For more information, see [Manage Configuration Packages](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Manage_Configuration_Packages.htm).
2. After you import a package, create a new data store. A data store is an address in the Elasticsearch storage for storing data extracted from an LDNS report. You must create a data store for each Visualizer dashboard. For more information, see [Create Data Stores](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Data_Stores.htm). Typically, packages are distributed with data store templates so that you can create a new data store using a template.
3. Once you create a data store, select a dashboard for visualizing. A dashboard is a set of visualizations (graphs) to be displayed in Kibana. A package may contain several dashboards for grouping relevant graphs. For more information on Kibana, see [Kibana documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/kibana/4.0/index.html).


Visualizer Server is distributed with the Base Visualizer package. The package includes a set preconfigured data stores and dashboard templates to handle basic transaction types, such as:

- Health Care Eligibility 270 Inquiry/271 Response
- Health Care Claim Status 276 Inquiry/276 Response
- HIPAA 820 Payment Order/Remittance Advice
- HIPAA 834 Benefit Enrollment and Maintenance
- HIPAA 837 Health Care Claim, and so on.


You can see the full list of templates on the Templates page of the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder.

---

<a id="configuration-package-structure"></a>

##### Configuration Package Structure

> 📄 Source: [VS_Configuration_Package_Structure.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Configuration_Package_Structure.htm)

###### Configuration Package Structure


A basic configuration package consists of the following set of artifacts: dashboards, data stores, visualizations, and a package-definition.json file that is the registry that reflects the contents of a package. Note: Data stores are defined in the files of the indexes folder.


The package-definition.json file includes the following:

- a version. For example, "version": 1

- an array of indexes (data stores), that contains identifiers and names.
- an array of dashboards that contains identifiers, names, and a list of visualizations that are included in this dashboard.
- an array of visualizations that contain an ID, a name, and an ID of an index that includes data represented in this visualization.


Indexes, dashboards, and visualizations folders contain definition files named based on the IDs of included entities. For example, in the indexes folder, you can find a file name that corresponds to a data store with a certain ID that describes an Error Troubleshooting Report. In this file, you will find a data store description, the list of fields it comprises, field types, details on whether it has been analyzed or not.


So, files in the indexes folder list fields the corresponding data stores include and define a set of parsers. SpecBuilder supports two types of parsers: LDNS and DFF. A parser is a set of instructions used to extract information from LDNS reports and DFF files to be recorded at data stores. For example, a data store contains a field called ErrorID of the type string. By using this field, you can figure out what rules are being used to extract this field from the LDNS report. The XPath language is used to access data in XML files.


The following is a fragment of a sample instruction for the LDNS parser. It allows you to create configurations for extracting data from the source LDNS reports to be recorded at a data store.


Example


In the following example, the group node Transaction includes all elements that contain the Error element if an error occurred. Each error has an ID and the details on the failure. The Errors element that contains the Error and ErrorID elements is extracted from the attribute of a given type with the ID name. The Brief element contains a description of an error from the transaction data.


"parsers": [


{


"type": "LDNS",


"version": 1,


"params": {},


"config": {


"xpath": "//GroupNode[@Name='Transaction']",


"dataItems": [


{


"xpath": "descendant::Errors/Error",


"mappings": [


{


"xpath": "@ID",


"name": "ErrorID"


},


{


"xpath": "Brief",


"name": "Brief"


}, ....


The following is a fragment of a sample instruction for the DFF parser.


Example


In the following example, ErrorID is located in the '0' position. Information to be extracted using the XPath rules and added to the index is in this position.


"type": "DFF",


"version": 1,


"params": {


"fieldDelimiter": "0x2C",


"recordSeparator": "0x0D0x0A"


},


"config": {


"mappings": [


{


"index": 0,


"name": "ErrorID"


},


{


"index": 1,


"name": "Severity"


},....


Note When you upload data, the Raw data source type is also accepted. However, it uses the LDNS parser since raw data is internally transformed into the LDNS report.


Visualization is a separate graph that contains information on what is represented on this graph in the Kibana format. Kibana entities define the values on the axes of the graph, and also determine what fields are used for data representation. An internal format of Kibana defines what information from a data store is included into the graph and the way it is displayed. Kibana Dashboard is a set of saved visualizations in groups that you can arrange. For more information on Kibana, see [Kibana documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/kibana/4.0/index.html).

---

<a id="create-configuration-packages"></a>

##### Create Configuration Packages

> 📄 Source: [VS_Create_Configuration_Packages.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Create_Configuration_Packages.htm)

###### Create Configuration Packages


With the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, you can create your own configuration packages to create dashboards. The following is a case study to create a custom configuration package. This package configures a dashboard that displays the average number of claims for a certain user.


To create a new configuration package:

1. On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, click Data Stores, and then click Create New.
2. In the Create new Data Store dialog, click Start from Blank.
3. In the Configure Data Store mapping dialog, click Add New Field.
4. Enter Name as a field name.
5. Click Add New Field, enter a field name (example: Claim Number).
6. In the field type, select integer, and then click Next.
7. In the Configure parsers dialog, click Add DFF Parser.
8. In the DFF Parser Configuration field, type the following code, and then click Next:
9. In the Name Data Store template dialog, enter a name (example claim_info_template) and a description (example:template_description) for the data store template, and then click Finish.
10. In the Create new Data Store dialog, enter a name (example: claim_info_data store) and a description (example: data store_description) for the created data store, and then click Finish.
11. On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, click Templates. In the Custom Templates folder, in the Data Store Templates section, the claim_info_template template you created is displayed.
12. On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, click Data Stores. In the Custom Templates section, the claim_info_store data store template you created is displayed.
13. On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, click Launch Kibana. In the left pane, the claim_info_data store template is displayed.
14. On the Visualize tab, in the Create a new visualization dialog, click Vertical bar chart.
15. In the Select a search source dialog, click From a new search.
16. In the left pane, enter metrics for a visualization as shown in the following image, and click Apply changes ():
17. On the toolbar, click Save Visualization (), enter a name (example: AverageClaimNumber) for the visualization, and then click Save.
18. On the Dashboard tab, click New Dashboard () to create a new dashboard.
19. Click and add the AverageClaimNumber visualization you created.
20. On the Dashboard tab of Kibana, click Save Dashboard () and in the Save As field enter a name (example: AverageClaimNumberDashboard) for your dashboard, and then click Save.
21. On the Visualizer tab of SpecBuilder, click Your Dashboards. The AverageClaimNumberDashboard dashboard you created is displayed.
22. On the toolbar, click Export as a Package (), and then select the dashboard you created.
23. At the bottom of the page, enter a name (example: AverageClaimNumberPackage) for your package.
24. Click EXPORT AS PACKAGE.


The package is now available in the Downloads folder as a .zip file.


To import a new package:

1. Open the Visualizer tab, click Templates, and then click Import New ().
2. At the bottom of the page, click Choose File, navigate to the AverageClaimNumberPackage package archive, and then click Open.
3. Click Import. The package is now available in the templates list.
4. Click Your Dashboards, click Create New (), select the AverageClaimNumberDashboard dashboard, and then click Next.
5. In the Create new Dashboard dialog, click the claim_info_datastore data store you created, and then click Next.
6. In the Create new Dashboard dialog, enter a name (example: ClaimNumberDashboard) and a description (example: DashboardDescription) for a dashboard, and then click Finish.
7. In the Upload Data Wizard step, click Add files (), navigate to the data file that contains information on the claims number of certain patients, and then click Open. The following is an example of a .txt data file which contains the names and claims quantity.
8. Click Process. In the Visualizer Uploads dialog, you see your data being processed.
9. Click Hide. On the Dashboard tab of Kibana, you see the ClaimNumberDashboard dashboard you created.



The bar chart displays an average number of claims for a certain patient.

---

<a id="upload-reports"></a>

### Upload Reports

> 📄 Source: [VS_Upload_Reports.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Upload_Reports.htm)

#### Upload Reports


On the Upload Reports page, you can view information about items uploaded to Visualizer Dashboards. You can find details on the number of received, accepted, rejected files, error details. You can search for reports by tag, index, and by date.


You may encounter a situation, when the SpecBuilder submits an LDNS report to Visualizer Server, but the reports fails to be stored in the Elasticsearch storage. The reasons for such behavior can be the following:

- The submitted LDNS report has invalid XML/DFF syntax
- Elasticsearch Windows service stops unexpectedly during the upload of the report.


So, this page can be especially useful for tracking the items that were uploaded to Visualizer Server but failed to be stored in the Elasticsearch storage.


Note The Search by upload ID option can be used when Edifecs XEServer is used to upload source files to Visualizer Server.

---

<a id="visualizer-dashboards-glossary"></a>

### Visualizer Dashboards Glossary

> 📄 Source: [VS_Visualizer_Dashboards_Glossary.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/VS_Visualizer_Dashboards_Glossary.htm)

#### Visualizer Dashboards Glossary


###### Configuration Package


A set of JSON configurations that define which parts of LDNS reports should be stored in the Elasticsearch storage and how they should be visualized. The package also includes configurations for data stores and dashboards.


###### Dashboard


A set of saved visualizations in groups that you can arrange.


###### Dashboard template


A JSON configuration used as a base for a new dashboard. It includes configurations for several visualizations.


###### Data Store


An address in an Elasticsearch storage for storing data extracted from an LDNS report. A single Data Store is required for one dashboard. Multiple pieces of data of the same format can be loaded into the same data store.


###### Data Store template


A JSON configuration used as a template for creating a new data store. A template includes Elasticsearch fields declaration and configurations for XML/DFF parsers.


###### Elasticsearch field


A cell in the Elasticsearch storage that keeps a single piece of business data extracted from an LDNS report. Each field has a data type that can be a simple data type (text, keyword, date, long, boolean, and so on), a type that supports the hierarchical format of JSON (object or nested), or a specialized type (geo_point, geo_shape, or completion).


###### Elasticsearch storage


A storage that is used by Visualizer Server to store data extracted from LDNS reports. The storage is partitioned into data stores, which are abstract storage parts for grouping information in storage.


###### Kibana


An open source platform for analytics and visualization designed to work with the Elasticsearch repository. Kibana allows you to view, search, and interact with data stored in the Data Store and to visualize large volumes of data in a variety of charts, tables, and maps.


###### Security token


A unique character string required to authorize access to Visualizer Server.


###### Tag


A string identifier that is used to mark a particular upload to Visualizer Server. This tag can be used to search for uploaded data and to remove data that belongs to the current upload from the data store.


###### Token archive


An archive with connection parameters required to authorize the connection to Visualizer Server. Includes a .pem certificate and a connection.properties file that contains a host name, port number, and actual security token. A token archive is generated during the installation of Visualizer Server.


###### Upload ID


An ID that is generated if an LDNS report fails to get stored in the Elasticsearch storage during the upload process. The ID can be used when searching for failed uploads.


###### Visualization


A separate graph that contains information on what is represented on this graph in the Kibana format.


###### Visualizer Server


A server application designed for storing and visualizing LDNS reports in XML and DFF formats. With Visualizer Server, you can accumulate statistics on XEngine validation results, summarize valuable business data, and then view it in the form of informative graphs in XESManager or SpecBuilder.

<a id="work-with-rules"></a>

### Work with Rules

> 📄 Source: [Book_Work_with_Rules.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Work_with_Rules.htm)

#### Work with Rules


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="rules-overview"></a>

#### Rules Overview

> 📄 Source: [Rules_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Rules_Overview.htm)

##### Rules Overview


Using rules, you can handle special business requirements by executing Java scripts or methods at specific times during the processing of the guideline. You can check for specific data and perform functions based on the value of that data.


For example, in a purchase order, you can create a rule that states that "If the line item quantity is greater than 50, an item description is required."


The types of rules that you can create include:


| Rules | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Natural Language | Used to describe a business rule in plain language such as English. This rule is used for documentation purposes and to convey the proper business usage to technical staff. |
| Regular Expressions | Allows you to specify a pattern by using the UNIX regular expression language. The following are the examples of regular expressions: [0-9] - indicates the data value must be between 0 and 9. [A-Z][a-z]+ - indicates any alphabetic word starting with an upper case character. Several references on how to build regular expressions are available on the Internet. For example, [http://www.regular-expressions.info/](http://www.regular-expressions.info/). Best Practices: It is better to use [Ec_PatternChecker](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/EC_PatternChecker_Object.htm) to find patterns in the data. Regular expressions only check for a pattern one time. |
| Lexical Format | Like regular expressions, the lexical format also allows you to specify a pattern which the data must match. While there is some functionality overlap between regular expression rules and lexical format rules, the lexical format rule is easier to use when the pattern is not complex. For example, date validation can easily be done by setting the lexical format to something like: YYYYMMDD. |
| OnSystemNode Script | This rule is used to provide pre-built JavaScripts that are executed when this node is encountered during processing. The script cannot be modified through the user interface but the rule can be excluded. Note Excluded system rules are not executed during validation so, if the variable defined in this rule is used later it may cause unexpected script error messages. |
| OnSystemEndNode Script | This rule is used to provide pre-built JavaScripts that are executed when all child nodes for the current node have been processed. The script cannot be modified through the user interface but the rule can be excluded. |
| OnUserNode Script | You can add your own custom JavaScript by using this rule. This rule is executed when the node is encountered during processing. |
| OnUserEndNode Script | By using this rule, you can add your own custom JavaScript to be executed at the end of this node. End of node means that the JavaScript is executed after all child nodes have been processed. This rule is useful on group nodes such as Segments and Loops in EDI. |
| OnUserNodeContent Script | By using this rule, you can add your own custom JavaScript to be executed on leaf nodes data, attributes data, or on mixed content data if mixed content is allowed in the corresponding guideline schema. |
| System Global Declaration | System Global Declarations are used in conjunction with System JavaScripts. |
| User Global Declaration Script | Variables or objects that are used in more than one rule can be declared in the Global Declaration Script. This allows the variables and objects to be used in multiple places. |
| XEngine Commit | This rule is only for customers who are using XEngine. This allows the current node data to be captured so that it can be used for auditing and tracking purposes. |
| Java Classes Declaration | Any custom Java classes that are used with OnUserNode Java and OnUserEndNode Java must be declared here. Java classes or the JAR files must also be pre-defined in the CLASSPATH environment variable. The CLASSPATH is checked for a class entry when a class is registered.    |
| OnUserNode Java | You can execute your own Java class by using this rule. This rule is executed when the node is encountered during processing. Multiple OnUserNode Java rules can be defined. This rule can be added to any guideline node except for code list node. Note that in the JSON guideline this rule can be added to the object, property, and array nodes only. |
| OnUserEndNode Java | You can execute your own Java class at the end of the node by using this rule. End of node means that the Java rule is executed after all child nodes have been processed. This rule can be used with group nodes such as segments, loops, and transactions in EDI. Note that in the JSON guideline this rule can be added to the object, property, and array nodes only. |
| OnUserNodeContent Java | You can execute your own Java class on leaf nodes data, attributes data, or on mixed content data if mixed content is allowed in the corresponding guideline schema. |
| .NET Classes Declaration | Any custom .NET classes that are used with OnUserNode .NET and OnUserEndNode .NET must be declared here. .NET assembly DLL files must be registered in Java/.NET Workspace Editor dialog box. |
| OnUserNode .NET | You can execute your own .NET class by using this rule. This rule is executed when the node is encountered during processing. Multiple OnUserNode .NET rules can be defined. This rule can be added to any guideline node except for code list node. Note that in the JSON guideline this rule can be added to the object, property, and array nodes only. |
| OnUserEndNode .NET | You can execute your own .NET class at the end of the node by using this rule. End of node means that the .NET rule is executed after all child nodes have been processed. This rule can be used with group nodes such as segments, loops, and transactions in EDI. Note that in the JSON guideline this rule can be added to the object, property, and array nodes only. |
| OnUserNodeContent .NET | You can execute your own .NET class on leaf nodes data, attributes data, or on mixed content data if mixed content is allowed in the corresponding guideline schema. |

---

<a id="add-rules"></a>

#### Add Rules

> 📄 Source: [Add_Rules.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_Rules.htm)

##### Add Rules


You can add rules to a selected node.


To add a rule to a node:

1. Click a node in the tree.
2. Click the Rules pane.
3. On the Rules toolbar, click New Rule ().
4. Click the type of rule to add, and then click OK.
5. In the right pane, create the parameters for the rule or, in the case of a script, provide the script.
6. Click Save to save the updated guideline.

---

<a id="customize-rules"></a>

#### Customize Rules

> 📄 Source: [Customize_Rules.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Customize_Rules.htm)

##### Customize Rules


To delete an existing rule:


Note Only User Rules can be deleted. Systems rules can only be included or excluded.

1. In the left pane of the Rules tab, click the rule to delete.
2. On the Rules toolbar, click Delete ().


To resequence a rule:


The order in which the rules are executed is determined by the order that they appear in the user interface.

1. In the left pane of the Rules tab, click the rule to move.
2. On the Rules toolbar, click the Move Rule up () or Move Rule Down ().


To modify rule properties:

1. In the left pane of the Rules tab, click the rule to modify.
2. In the right pane of the Rules tab, modify any properties for the rule.


To include/exclude a rule:

1. In the left pane of the Rules tab, click the rule to include or exclude.
2. On the Rules toolbar, click Exclude rule () or Include rule ().


Note In some cases, the guidelines contain System Actions. These actions cannot be modified, but they can be included, excluded and/or moved.


To select a node to be used in Lookahead method:


Note You can [insert a lookup path](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Insert_a_Lookup_Upath.htm) only for nodes of JavaScript rules.

1. In the left pane of the Rules tab, select the rule with a node to be used in Lookahead method.
2. In the right pane of the Rules tab, click Insert Lookup UPath ().
3. In the Lookup node selector dialog box, select the needed node, and then click OK.

---

<a id="script-rules-designer"></a>

#### Script Rules Designer

> 📄 Source: [Book_Script_Rules_Designer.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Script_Rules_Designer.htm)

##### Script Rules Designer


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="script-rules-designer-overview"></a>

##### Script Rules Designer Overview

> 📄 Source: [Script_Rules_Designer_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Script_Rules_Designer_Overview.htm)

###### Script Rules Designer Overview


The Script Rules Designer makes it easy for advanced users to write scripts in a fast and easy manner.


###### Features and Benefits

- View and edit all scripts simultaneously
- Open a script from an external file
- Save a script to a file
- Search for and replace text in the script
- Repeat or undo changes made to the script
- Click a node in the tree and view associated scripts

---

<a id="script-rules-designer"></a>

##### Script Rules Designer

> 📄 Source: [Script_Rules_Designer.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Script_Rules_Designer.htm)

###### Script Rules Designer


The Script Rules Designer is a double-pane window. The guideline tree is displayed in the left pane and the script is displayed in the right pane.


To launch the Script Rules Designer:

- On the Home tab, in the Rules group, click Script Rules.


To add a new function:

- The function name must be formatted as follows:

- To finish adding the function, do one of the following:
- Click Synchronize.
- Click Close.
- Click Yes to confirm replacing matching functions and deleting any missing functions.


To delete and modify functions:

- To delete a function, delete the text, and then click Synchronize () on the toolbar.
- To modify a function, edit the text of the function within the start and end curly brackets and do one of the following:
- Click Close.
- On the toolbar, click Synchronize ().
- Confirm the deletion or modification.


Warning: Any changes to functions modify all occurrences of the function. Deleted functions is no longer available for any nodes.


To find or replace text in the script:


See [Find and Replace Script Text](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Find_and_Replace_Script_Text.htm).


To select a node to be used in Lookahead method:

1. Click the correspondent place in right pane area, and on the Script Rules Designer toolbar, click Insert Lookup UPath ().
2. In the Lookup node selector dialog box, select the needed node, and then click OK.

---

In the Script Rules Designer, you can search for text and replace it if desired.


To search for text:

2. In the What box, type the text to find or select it from the list.
3. Choose from the following options:
4. Click the Direction.
5. Click Find.


To find and replace text:

1. On the toolbar, click Replace ().
2. In the Find box, type the text to find or select it from the list.
3. In the Replace with box, type the text to find or select it from the list.
4. Choose from the following options:
5. In the Replace in group, click one of the following:
6. Click one of the following:

<a id="javascript-implementation"></a>

##### JavaScript Implementation

> 📄 Source: [JavaScript_Implementation.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/JavaScript_Implementation.htm)

###### JavaScript Implementation


With JavaScript, you can customize and extend the guideline further to meet their business needs. While many JavaScript features are supported, JavaScript implementation is limited such that it can work effectively within the SpecBuilder framework. JavaScript features used in client-side HTML scripting are not supported.

---

<a id="insert-a-lookup-upath"></a>

##### Insert a Lookup Upath

> 📄 Source: [Insert_a_Lookup_Upath.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Insert_a_Lookup_Upath.htm)

###### Insert a Lookup UPath


When working with JavaScript rules, you can insert a lookup path for a node to be used in the Lookahead method.


To insert a lookup path for a node:

- Add a JavaScript rule. For more information, see [Add Rules](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_Rules.htm).
- In the Rules pane, on the toolbar, click Insert Lookup UPath ().
- On the Lookup node selector, select the node path, and then click OK.


You can also use a UPath to refer data instances, for more information, see [Reference Data Instances](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Reference_Data_Instances.htm).

---

<a id="ec-objects"></a>

##### EC Objects
