<a id="x12-companion-guide-packages"></a>

## X12 Companion Guide Packages

> 📄 Source: [Book_X12_Companion_Guide_Packages.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_X12_Companion_Guide_Packages.htm)

### X12 Companion Guide Packages


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="x12-companion-guide-package-overview"></a>

### X12 Companion Guide Package Overview

> 📄 Source: [X12_Companion_Guide_Package_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/X12_Companion_Guide_Package_Overview.htm)

#### X12 Companion Guide Package Overview


SpecBuilder publishing was empowered with the capability of creating the Companion Guides compliant to new WEDI/X12 regulations, mandated specific and very strict template for the published documents. SpecBuilder UI allows modification of the templates to match the exact customer needs and provides a set of macros to automate future document publications. A publication template is needed to minimize the effort on building printable documents out of multiple Companion Guide guidelines. Since they all have the same template mandated by X12/CORE with only difference in few key properties and custom nodes on the updated fields, the typical way of solving the publication task is to use the template that has different guidelines applied to it.


A package includes the following items:

- Guidelines - A set of guidelines associated with the template. The guidelines are the main source of information. You can add references to guidelines to the package or remove them.
- Document template - A template is a Microsoft Word document containing main body of the publication and special macros that are replaced with the information from the guideline. This template is used to publish information about the guidelines associated with it.
- Macro Dictionary - A list of all available macros that can be used for the companion guide template creation.


An X12 Companion Guide Package with the added guidelines can be published. For more information about how to publish a package, see [Publish X12 Companion Guide Package](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publish_X12_Companion_Guide_Package.htm). For more information about how to add guidelines to the package, see [Add a Guideline to the Companion Guide Package](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_a_Guideline_to_the_Companion_Guide_Package.htm).


You can publish an existing X12 guideline to the X12 Companion Guide document. For more information about how to publish a guideline, see [Publish to X12 Companion Guide Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publish_to_X12_Companion_Guide_Overview.htm).


According to DISA, no structures related to X12 guidelines may be published or saved except for internal purpose. Such guidelines or publications cannot be sent out to the external trading partners.


Due to that, every attempt to save the HIPAA or X12N – Healthcare guideline is associated with the special confirmation dialog. If this information is acknowledged and accepted, user may choose not to show this again, which means automatic acceptance and compliance with DISA Copyright regulations.

---

<a id="work-with-x12-companion-guide-packages"></a>

### Work with X12 Companion Guide Packages

> 📄 Source: [Book_Work_with_X12_Companion_Guide_Packages.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Work_with_X12_Companion_Guide_Packages.htm)

#### Work with X12 Companion Guide Packages


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="create-new-x12-companion-guide-package"></a>

#### Create New X12 Companion Guide Package

> 📄 Source: [Create_New_X12_Companion_Guide_Package.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_New_X12_Companion_Guide_Package.htm)

##### Create New X12 Companion Guide Package


X12 Companion Guide package contains the following items:

- Guidelines - A set of guidelines associated with the template. The guidelines are the main source of information. You can add references to guidelines to the package or remove them.
- Document template - A template is a Microsoft Word document containing main body of the publication and special macros that are replaced with the information from the guideline. This template is used to publish information about the guidelines associated with it.
- Macro Dictionary - A list of all available macros that can be used for the companion guide template creation.


To create a new X12 Companion Guide Package:

- On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click New.
- Click X12 Companion Guide.
- On the Companion Guide Template Selection wizard page, choose from the following options:
- Click Next.
- The following options are available on the Select Guidelines for Publishing wizard toolbar:
- Click Finish.

---

<a id="add-a-guideline-to-the-companion-guide-package"></a>

#### Add a Guideline to the Companion Guide Package

> 📄 Source: [Add_a_Guideline_to_the_Companion_Guide_Package.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_a_Guideline_to_the_Companion_Guide_Package.htm)

##### Add a Guideline to the Companion Guide Package


Companion Guide template may contain multiple guidelines and the template itself may assume iteration through these guidelines and extraction of the information from them in form of a list (see the standard templates, for example). You can add guidelines to the X12 Companion Guide Package. All the guidelines in the package have one of the following types:

- Content
- Envelope
- Acknowledgment


Guideline types set the way macros are resolved within the template. For example 'Content' specific macros are not resolved for the guidelines with the type set to 'Acknowledgment'.


Note One guideline with the same data source type cannot be added to the package twice.


To add a guideline:

1. Create an X12 Companion Guide package or open an existing one.
2. In the Guidelines pane, click Add Guideline. The Add Guideline dialog box is displayed.
3. Type a path and a guideline name, or click Browse to navigate to the location of the guideline, and then click the name of the guideline to add.
4. In the Data Source Type field, select the guideline type to be used. You can add one and the same guideline with the different data source type.
5. Click OK.


Note You can change a type of added guidelines or guideline paths by using Modify button on the guideline toolbar.

---

<a id="work-with-companion-guide-macros"></a>

#### Work with Companion Guide Macros

> 📄 Source: [Work_with_Companion_Guide_Macros.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Work_with_Companion_Guide_Macros.htm)

##### Work with Companion Guide Macros


Companion Package macros are used to set the outlook and content of the published document. You can simply drag a macro from the Macro Dictionary pane and drop to the Editor. Special macros may be used for different content types: Content (main transaction content), Ack (Acknowledgement-type guidelines such as 999, TA1, and the rest), Envelope (guidelines with ISA, GS content).


The following content macros are supported:


| Macros | Description | | --- | --- | | Exclusion Table | Inserts the table with all changes made to the Companion Guide guideline. This macro is resolved for the guidelines that contain CG user notes. | | CORE Exclusion Table | Inserts a table with all changes made to the CORE Companion Guide guideline. This macro is only resolved for the guidelines that contain CORE user notes. These guidelines are available in the Operating Rules package. | | Group Version | Inserts a version field from the Transaction node. | | Transaction Name | Inserts a name field from the Transaction node. | | Transaction ID | Inserts an ID field from the Transaction node. | | Implementation Guide Name | Inserts the Name of Implementation Guide. This macro is resolved in the IG: Name user note. For more information, see [*Publish to X12 Companion Guide Overview*.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publish_to_X12_Companion_Guide_Overview.htm) | The following service macros are supported:


| Macros | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Content: Begin Scope | Opens the scope with the repeating guideline information from the content guideline. This macro is only resolved for the guidelines of the "Content" type. |
| Content: End Scope | Closes the content scope. This macro is only resolved for the guidelines of the "Content" type. |
| Envelope: Begin Scope | Opens the scope with the repeating guideline information from the envelope guideline. This macro is only resolved for the guidelines of the "Envelope" type. |
| Envelope: End Scope | Closes the envelope scope. This macro is only resolved for the guidelines of the "Envelope" type. |
| Acknowledgment: Begin Scope | Opens the scope with the repeating guideline information from the acknowledgment guideline. This macro is only resolved for the guidelines of the "Acknowledgment" type. |
| Acknowledgment: End Scope | Closes the acknowledgment scope. This macro is only resolved for the guidelines of the "Acknowledgment" type. |
| User Name | Inserts the author field from the guideline document properties information. |
| Time | Inserts current time. |
| Date | Inserts a current date. |
| Counter | Inserts an auto-increment counter which allows numbering the repeating rows. |
| Year | Inserts a current year in the CCYY format. |
| Company Name | Inserts the company name from the SpecBuilder registration information. |



Note Date, Time, Year, and Company Name are the only macros, which can be resolved in headers and footers.


If your template contains embedded scopes, these scopes are resolved as plain text within an external scope. In the following example:


Example


`[Content:BeginScope]` `[Transaction ID][Transaction Name]` `[Envelope:BeginScope]` `[User Name]` `[Envelope:EndScope]` `[Content:EndScope]`


`[Envelope:BeginScope]` and `[Envelope:EndScope]` are resolved as plain text and [User Name] is resolved for the 'Content' guideline type.


When the template is saved, you can choose that template from the list and reuse it for all further Companion Guide guidelines you’ll be working with.

---

<a id="work-with-companion-guide-templates"></a>

#### Work with Companion Guide Templates

> 📄 Source: [Work_with_Companion_Guide_Templates.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Work_with_Companion_Guide_Templates.htm)

##### Work with Companion Guide Templates


While working with templates you can save a template to be used later. You can also change a template to a different one while working with packages.


To change companion guide template:

1. On the Home tab, click change companion guide template.
2. Choose one of the following templates:
3. Click OK.


To save a template:

1. On the Home tab, click Save or click Save as.
2. Provide a Title and an Author name in the Template Properties dialog box.
3. Click OK.


Use Save as Template option to save your template with a different title or author's name.

---

<a id="publish-x12-companion-guide-package"></a>

#### Publish X12 Companion Guide Package

> 📄 Source: [Publish_X12_Companion_Guide_Package.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publish_X12_Companion_Guide_Package.htm)

##### Publish X12 Companion Guide Package


An X12 Companion Guide Package with the added guidelines can be published. Information about all the guidelines in the package is published to the one document of the selected format.


To publish an X12 Companion Guide Package:

1. Open the X12 Companion Guide package you want to publish.
2. Make sure that needed guidelines are added. For more information, see [Add a Guideline to the Companion Guide Package](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_a_Guideline_to_the_Companion_Guide_Package.htm).
3. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click Publish.
4. Click Publish.
5. Type a name of the document you want to use as an output for publishing.
6. Click Next.
7. When asked if you want to view the published document, do one of the following:


Note You can choose an output file type while publishing. The following types are available: PDF (.pdf), Rich Text Format (.rtf), HTML (.htm), Microsoft Word (.doc, .docx). When publishing to a specific file format, make sure that saving to this format is not blocked in Microsoft® Word. To verify that, go to File > Options >Trust Center Settings > File Block Settings.


You can also publish existing guideline currently open in the editor. For more information about publishing guidelines, see [Publish to X12 Companion Guide Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publish_to_X12_Companion_Guide_Overview.htm).

---

<a id="schematron"></a>

## Schematron

> 📄 Source: [Book_Schematron.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Schematron.htm)

### Schematron


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="schematron-overview"></a>

### Schematron Overview

> 📄 Source: [Schematron_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schematron_Overview.htm)

#### Schematron Overview


Schematron is a rule-based validation language, which allows creating business edits for extra validation on top of XML-based documents, such as CCD, CDA, and others. It allows you to make assertions about the presence or absence of patterns in XML trees. It is a structural schema language, expressed in XML by using a small number of elements and XPath. With Schematron, you can express constraints in ways that other XML schema languages like XML Schema and DTD don't allow. For example, you can require that the content of an element be controlled by one of its siblings, or the root element must have specific attributes. Schematron can specify required relationships between multiple XML files. Constraints and content rules can be associated with free form validation error messages, that allows translation of numeric Schematron error codes into meaningful user error messages.


For more information about working with Schematron, see [Schematron Syntax](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schematron_Syntax.htm).


When an XML Schema guideline is open, the SpecBuilder Schematron editor is available on the Schematron tab. With the Schematron editor, you can edit Schematron rules and check whether the Schematron file structure is correct. For more information, see [Schematron Pane](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schematron_Pane.htm).

---

<a id="schematron-syntax"></a>

### Schematron Syntax

> 📄 Source: [Schematron_Syntax.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schematron_Syntax.htm)

#### Schematron Syntax


A Schematron schema must be a well-formed XML document, according to the version of XML used. The following is an example of the Schematron rules:


Example


<pattern id="p-1.3.6.1.4.1.19376.1.5.3.1.1.5.3.1-errors">


<title>IHE PCC Hazardous Working Conditions - errors validation phase</title>


<rule context='*[cda:templateId/@root="1.3.6.1.4.1.19376.1.5.3.1.1.5.3.1"]'>


<assert test='../cda:section'>


Error: In IHE PCC Hazardous Working Conditions Section (1.3.6.1.4.1.19376.1.5.3.1.1.5.3.1), the Hazardous


Working Conditions can only be used on sections.


</assert>


<assert test='cda:code[@code = "10161-8"]'>


Error: In IHE PCC Hazardous Working Conditions Section (1.3.6.1.4.1.19376.1.5.3.1.1.5.3.1), the section type


code of a Hazardous Working Conditions must be 10161-8


</assert>


<assert test='cda:code[@codeSystem = "2.16.840.1.113883.6.1"]'>


Error: In IHE PCC Hazardous Working Conditions Section (1.3.6.1.4.1.19376.1.5.3.1.1.5.3.1), the section type


code must come from the LOINC code system (2.16.840.1.113883.6.1).


</assert>


</rule>


</pattern>


<pattern id="p-1.3.6.1.4.1.19376.1.5.3.1.1.5.3.1-warnings">


<title>IHE PCC Hazardous Working Conditions - warning validation phase</title>


</pattern>


The following table shows the elements used to create Schematron schemas:


| Element | Description |
| --- | --- |
| active | Refers to a pattern that is active in the current phase. |
| assert | An assertion that is made about the context nodes. |
| extends | Abstract rules are lists of assertions without a context expression. The required rule attribute references an abstract rule. The current rule uses all the assertions from the abstract rule it extends. |
| include | You can insert an external document in place of the include element. The href attribute (required) references an external well-formed XML document whose document element is a Schematron element at the current position in the schema. |
| let | Declares a named variable. You can make the let element a child of a rule element for the variable to calculate and scope to the current rule and context. The name attribute (required) is the name of the variable. The value attribute (required) is an expression evaluated in the current context. |
| name | Names nodes from the instance document to allow clear assertions and diagnostics. The path attribute (optional) is an expression evaluated in the current context that returns a string that is the name of a node. |
| ns (namespace) | Specifies a namespace prefix and URI. The prefix attribute (required) is an XML name with no colon character. The uri attribute (required) is a namespace URI. |
| param | A name-value pair that provides parameters for an abstract pattern. The name attribute (required) is an XML name with no colon. The value attribute (required) is a fragment of a query. |
| pattern | A structure, simple or complex. A set of rules which give constraints that are in some way related. The id attribute provides a unique name for the pattern and is required for abstract patterns. The title and p elements allow rich documentation. The icon, see and fpi attributes allow rich interfaces and documentation. When a pattern element has the attribute abstract with a value true, then the pattern defines an abstract pattern. Abstract patterns allow a common definition mechanism for structures which use different names and paths, but which are at heart the same. |
| phase | Groups patterns to name and declare variations in schemas to support progressive validation. The id attribute (required) is the name of the phase. The implementation determines which phase to use to validate documents, for example, by user command. |
| report | Asserts context nodes. The data content is a natural-language assertion. The test attribute (required) is an assertion test evaluated in the current context. If the test evaluates positive, the report succeeds. The diagnostics attribute (optional) refers to further diagnostic information. The natural-language assertion is a positive statement of a found pattern or a negative statement of a constraint. |
| rule | A list of assertions tested within the context specified by the required context attribute. The context attribute specifies the rule context expression. |
| schema | The top-level element of a Schematron schema. The schemaVersion attribute (optional) provides the version of the schema. The queryBinding attribute (optional) provides the short name of the query language binding in use. If this attribute is specified, it cannot have a value that the current implementation does not support. The defaultPhase attribute may be used to indicate the phase to use in the absence of explicit, user-supplied information. |
| value-of | Finds or calculates values from the instance document to allow clearer assertions and diagnostics. |
| flag | You can declare a flag by using an assertion or rule with a flag attribute with that name. By using flags, you can indicate state or severity information to a subsequent process. |
| diagnostic | An English language message giving specific details concerning a failed assertion, such as found versus expected values and repair hints. |
| diagnostics | A section that contains individual diagnostic elements. |
| dir | A section of English language text with a direction specified by the value attributes. The value ltr indicates left-to-right text; the value rtl indicates right-to-left text. |
| emph | Lays emphasis on a portion of text that is rendered. |
| flag | A Boolean flag variable is implicitly declared by an assertion or rule having a flag attribute with that name. The purpose of flags is to convey state or severity information to a subsequent process. |
| p | A paragraph of English text that encases maintainer and user information about the parent element. |
| span | A portion of text that is rendered in a distinct way, used with the class attribute. |
| path | A path that allows a precise specification of nodes. |
| title | A summary of the purpose of the schema or pattern. |



 


Syntax for Attributes


 


| Attributes  | Descriptions  |
| --- | --- |
| fpi | A formal public identifier for the schema, phase, or other element. This attribute allows rich interfaces and documentation. This attribute is applicable for assert, pattern, phase, report, rule, and schema elements. |
| flag | The flag attribute allows more detailed outcomes. It is the name of a Boolean flag variable. A flag is implicitly declared by an assertion or rule having a flag attribute with that name. The value of a flag becomes true when an assertion with that flag fails or a rule with that flag is applied. The purpose of flags is to convey state or severity information to a subsequent process. This attribute is applicable for assert, report, and rule elements. |
| icon | Indicates the location of a graphics file containing some visible representation of the severity, significance, or other grouping of the associated element. This attribute is applicable for assert, pattern, phase, report, rule, and schema elements. |
| role | Describes the function of the assertion or context node in the pattern. If the assertion has a subject attribute, then the role labels the arc between the context node and any nodes that match the path expression given by the subject attribute. The role attributes allow explicit identification of some part of a pattern. |
| see | The path/URI of external information of interest to maintainers and users of the schema. This attribute is applicable for assert, pattern, phase, report, rule, and schema elements. |
| subject | A path that allows additional, precise specification of nodes. The subject attributes allows explicit identification of some part of a pattern. This attribute is applicable for assert, report, and rule elements. |



For more information about using Schematron and its syntax, see the Schematron part in the Rule-based validation section of the Document Schema Definition Language (DSDL), ISO/IEC 19757-3:2006 Information technology.

---

<a id="the-schematron-business-use-cases"></a>

### The Schematron Business Use Cases

> 📄 Source: [Schematron_Business_Use_Cases.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schematron_Business_Use_Cases.htm)

#### The Schematron Business Use Cases


With Health Level Seven (HL7) version 3 formally adopting XML as its format for representing documents, there is a need to process business rules, which is different than the structural validation, with an XML schema.


HL7 version 3 describes the abundance of clinical cases, where some may not be significantly different structurally, but may have different clinical business restrictions. One example provided by HL7 is the CDA (Clinical Document Architecture), which defines a common schema, with the ability to stricken it for specific clinical documents. In many cases, when CDA-derived documents are created, specific validation is needed. Schematron, which describes the rules of a business domain, can be used to distinguish these cases.


The Schematron rules library could be created independent of the documents /xml payload, and different Schematron files could be used to validate documents with certain requirements specific to those categories.


Note Clinical Document Architecture (CDA) is a XML-based markup standard intended to specify the encoding, structure and semantics of clinical documents for exchange. It is one of the base structures, which Schematron rules use.


Schematron can be used for the following and many other business cases:

- HL7v3: Contains Schematron rules, which verify various business requirements such as presence of proper attribute values for certain usage templates (ClinicalDocument must have an extension attribute equal to POCD_HD000040), sequence of elements, co-occurrence constraints.
- CCD: Continuity of Care Document (CCD) uses XML-based markup standard to specify the encoding, structure, and semantics of a patient summary clinical document for exchange of information. The Continuity of Care Document has sections on allergies, medications, problems, and laboratory results in addition to patient header information. Schematron rules verify specific constraints, related to CCD. For example, a procedure activity should contain exactly one [Act | Observation | Procedure] / effectiveTime.
- HITSP C32: The HITSP Summary Documents by using HL7 CCD Component describes the content of the document summarizing a user's medical status for information exchange. HITSP/C83 Clinical Document, the Payer section conforms to the IHE Payers Section template, and contains a template Id element whose root attribute is “1.3.6.1.4.1.19376.1.5.3.1.1.5.3.7”.
- IHE Laboratory Document: Integrates information systems that support healthcare institutions. The objective is to ensure that in the care of patients all data required for medical decisions is correct and available to healthcare professionals. Laboratory Specialty Section (1.3.6.1.4.1.19376.1.3.3.2.1) shall be a leaf section with a single entry and non-empty text derived from that entry, or it shall have no text and one or more component subsections.
- IHE Patient Care Coordination and Quality Reporting Public Health: This domain deals with general clinical care aspects such as document exchange, order processing, and coordination with other domains. It also addresses work flows that are common to multiple specialty areas and the integration needs of areas that do not have a separate domain within IHE. In IHE PCC Estimated Blood Loss Section (1.3.6.1.4.1.19376.1.5.3.1.1.9.2), the title element should be present in each section and should be valued with an appropriate text string representing the clinical data in that section.


The following is an example of Verifications for Hazardous Working Conditions Schematron rule that can be edited in SpecBuilder.


*[Image: Business Use Case]*

<a id="work-with-schematron"></a>

### Work with Schematron

> 📄 Source: [Work_with_Schematron.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Work_with_Schematron.htm)

#### Work with Schematron


With Schematron, you can validate XML files against Schematron rules. Validation is performed after the guideline validation is complete.


Note To validate a data file against Schematron rules, the Java runtime environment 11 is required. You should also ensure that the JAVA_HOME variable is set correctly. For example, C:\Program Files (x86)\Java\jre11.


To validate XML files against Schematron rules:

1. Open the XMLSchema guideline you want to use for the Schematron validation. For more information, see [Open an XML Schema Guideline](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Open_an_XML_Schema_Guideline.htm).
2. Click the Schematron pane to display the Schematron editor. For more information about Schematron editor, see [Schematron Pane.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schematron_Pane.htm)
3. On the Editor tab of the ribbon, click Open Schematron.
4. Navigate to the file you want to use for data validation, and then click Open. If the Schematron file contains references to external entities, the following dialog box is displayed:
5. Click Yes to display the entities content in the original Schematron file for further editing. In this case, the Schematron editor includes the content of the external entity file instead of its reference.
6. In the Schematron pane, you can edit Schematron rules. On the Editor tab of the ribbon, click Validate Syntax to make sure rules don't have syntax errors. The syntax validation results are displayed in the Schematron Design Errors pane. For more information, see [Schematron Design Errors Pane.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schematron_Design_Errors_Pane.htm)
7. In the Analyzer/Data pane, click New Data ( ) to start the Data Wizard.
8. Click Load an existing data file and browse to the file location.
9. Check the Show Advanced Options check box, and then click Next three times.
10. In the Select Additional Validation Options wizard page, provide the Schematron validation options. For more information, see [Select Additional Validation Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Analyzer_Additional_Validation_Options.htm).
11. Click Next.
12. Click the Schematron Runtime Messages pane to view the list of validation errors. For more information, see [Schematron Runtime Messages Pane](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schematron_Runtime_Messages_Pane.htm).
13. In the Schematron Runtime Messages pane, click the error you want to analyze. The failed rule in the XML file is highlighted in the Analyzer/Data pane.
14. On the Analyzer tab of the ribbon, click Details. The Details dialog box is displayed.


The following fields are available:

- Active Pattern: Displays the pattern belonging to the active phase.
- Fired Rule: Specifies the context in which the rule was used. The Schematron rules can have “phase” elements defined with IDs. In your Schematron file these “phases” can have IDs that display as Errors, Warning, and Manual. When a rule for a specific phase is set off, it has a flag that is equal to the ID of corresponding phase.
- Assert or Report: Depends on the message type. Displays a description of the nature of the message.

---

<a id="link-schematron-files-to-a-guideline"></a>

### Link Schematron Files to a Guideline

> 📄 Source: [Link_Schematron_files_to_a_guideline.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Link_Schematron_files_to_a_guideline.htm)

#### Link Schematron Files to a Guideline


Guidelines can be stored with related Schematron rules. In this case, you get an analyzer package which includes a guideline and linked Schematron rules to validate this guideline against. Linked Schematron rules are saved to any sub-folder or folder of the same or outer level, depending on what internal structure you use.


To link Schematron rules to a guideline:

1. Open a guideline.
2. Open Schematron file you want to link to a guideline.
3. On the Home tab, in the Schematron group, click Validation Options, and then click Use currently open rules.


You can provide a Schematron file to validate a guideline against even if you do not have any Schematron rules open, you do not want to open them, or you want to validate a guideline not against open rules. In this case, on the Home tab, in the Schematron group, click Use specified Schematron and provide a path to the Schematron files you need. Or in the Select Additional Validation Options page of the Analyzer wizardin the Schematron validation options, click Use specified Schematron, and then click to navigate to the Schematron file location.


*[Image: Select Additional Validation Options]*


You can review or edit Schematron rules in the Schematron pane in the appeared editor. For more information, see [Schematron Pane](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schematron_Pane.htm).


Note A path to a Schematron should be valid when sharing a guideline with the related Schematron file.


You can change related Schematron files to another one by performing the same procedure and choosing another Schematron file.


The Edifecs Standard Database contains guidelines with linked Schematron rules which are ready to be used.

---

<a id="tag-schemas"></a>

## Tag Schemas

> 📄 Source: [Book_Tagging_Schemas.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Tagging_Schemas.htm)

### Tag Schemas


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="tagging-overview"></a>

### Tagging Overview

> 📄 Source: [Tagging_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Tagging_Overview.htm)

#### Tagging Overview


Tagging allows you to define logical markers to specific raw data elements to transform raw data, which is not human-friendly, to a business-friendly extract that can be easily understood by humans and downstream business systems. Tagging simplifies access to your data. A tag schema is a configuration for tagging that includes location of a nodes to be tagged in a data node tree and criteria when these nodes must be tagged. To create a tagging schema, you can use the Tag Schema editor, that allows you to open a guideline as a reference to simplify tags creation.


You can set as tags the following:

- text values
- values from data nodes using UPath to access the required element.


You can assign tags to both leaf and non-leaf nodes. You can use attributes as tag destinations for XData, key/value pairs - for JData, and properties on logical lever - for LDNS XML Report correspondingly and for DFF Tag report.


You can have multiple tag schemas in separate files, and all tags from multiple files are applied if they match the corresponding criteria. You can specify which tag schema file must be applied to the required transaction. For example, Key="X12:*:V5010X223A1:837".


With the Tag Schema editor, you can open and edit tag schemas with or without a guideline. You can use a guideline to drag and drop UPaths of the node you want to tag or use the guideline context menu to create tags from guideline nodes when creating a tag schema. It allows you to avoid typing UPaths manually in tag settings. For more information, see [Add and Edit Tags](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_and_Edit_Tags.htm).

---

<a id="work-with-tag-schemas"></a>

### Work with Tag Schemas

> 📄 Source: [Book_Work_with_Tag_Schemas.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Work_with_Tag_Schemas.htm)

#### Work with Tag Schemas


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="add-and-edit-tags"></a>

#### Add and Edit Tags

> 📄 Source: [Add_and_Edit_Tags.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_and_Edit_Tags.htm)

##### Add and Edit Tags


With tag schemas, you can add a marker to the XData element.


To add a tag:

1. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click New, and then click Tag Schema. The Tag Schema Editor is displayed.
2. On the Home tab of the ribbon, in the Editing group, click Insert or Insert Child to add a child tag to the selected parent tag. A new tag is displayed in the tags tree.
3. In the General pane, in Name, enter a name for the new tag.
4. In Apply To, enter the UPath of the node you want to apply a tag to in the run-time format. Note that the UPath should not contain nodes that have already been included to “Apply To” of the parent tag node. When looking for a matching data node, the scope of the search is limited by the children sub tree of a node to which the parent tag is applied. For more information, see [Reference Data Instances](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Reference_Data_Instances.htm).
5. In Value, enter a value for a tag if you want to use a constant value or click Complex to provide a complex value definition. On the Tag Rules tab of the ribbon, in the General group, select a function to use, and then drag and drop it to the Value Definition box. For more information, see [Create Tagging Rules](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_Tagging_Rules.htm).
6. In Usage Condition, click to add a tag rule. On the Tag Rules tab of the ribbon, in the General group, select a function to use and drag and drop it to the Usage Condition box. For more information, see [Create Tagging Rules](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_Tagging_Rules.htm). Note that this step is optional.
7. On the Home tab of the ribbon, in the Editing group, click Apply to save the changes you made to the current tag.
8. On the File tab, click Save, or Save As and enter a name for a tag schema to save the changes.


To edit an existing tag, open an existing tag schema, click the tag in the tags tree you want to edit, and perform steps from 3 to 8.

---

<a id="create-tagging-rules"></a>

#### Create Tagging Rules

> 📄 Source: [Create_Tagging_Rules.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_Tagging_Rules.htm)

##### Create Tagging Rules


With the Tag Schema Editor, you can provide a complex value definition for a tag.


To provide a complex value definition for a tag:

1. Click an existing tag in the tags tree or create a new one. For more information, see [Add and Edit Tags](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_and_Edit_Tags.htm).
2. In Value, click Complex to provide a complex value definition.
3. On the Tag Rules tab of the ribbon, in the General group, select a function to use and drag and drop it to the Value Definition box. You can create complex rules using the following functions:
4. On the Home tab of the ribbon, click Apply.
5. On the File menu, click Save to save your tag schema.


You can also apply a complex usage condition.


To use a complex usage condition:

1. In Usage Condition, click to add a tag rule.
2. On the Tag Rules tab of the ribbon, in the General group, select a function to use and drag and drop it to the Usage Condition box. The following functions are available:
3. On the Home tab of the ribbon, click Apply.
4. On the File menu, click Save to save your tag schema.


You can use a code list value in a conditional criterion when creating a tagging rule. For example, you need to check if the QTY.01 element in the 834 transaction is DT (Dependent total) or ET (Employee Total). In this case, you can create an expression where the code list value is used: IF /834/QTY/673 equals to [DT, ET].


To use a code list value:

1. On the Rules Editor Tools tab of the ribbon, click Set. Drag and drop the Set function to the conditional criterion, and then click . The Select Set dialog is displayed.
2. Click , and in the right pane click . The Select Codes dialog is displayed.
3. In the Select Codes dialog, in Source, click External Code Lists, and from the External Code Lists drop-down menu, select the code list you want to use.
4. In the following table, click a code list value you want to use, and then click Select.

---

<a id="parser-schemas"></a>

## Parser Schemas

> 📄 Source: [Book_Parser_Schemas.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Parser_Schemas.htm)

### Parser Schemas


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="parser-schema-overview"></a>

### Parser Schema Overview

> 📄 Source: [Parser_Schema_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Parser_Schema_Overview.htm)

#### Parser Schema Overview


When processing native files, parser schema files are used to identify standards and build a basic data node structure that is common to the specific standard. The parser schema files are generated and editable by SpecBuilder and are saved in .ecs file format.


Each node of the parser schema is color-coded and displayed with a folder icon to represent its function within the parser schema.


The parser schema files perform the following functions:

- Define the data that identifies the file type.

- Define delimiters used by the parser.
- Define the common structure.  
- Define all properties and lookups, which are added to the data node.

---

<a id="parser-schema-guideline-view"></a>

### Parser Schema Guideline View

> 📄 Source: [Parser_Schema_Guideline_View.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Parser_Schema_Guideline_View.htm)

#### Parser Schema Guideline View


The Guideline pane presents a parser schema structure, allowing you to navigate through the different parent/child nodes contained in the currently open parser schema.


Each node of the parser schema is color-coded and displayed with a folder icon to represent its function within the parser schema.


The icons are presented here:

- is used for Decimal Indicator nodes.

- is used for Initial File Detect nodes.

- is used for Detect Information.

- is used for EDI Delimiter Information node.

- is used for EDI Delimiter Settings nodes.

- is used for Element Delimiter nodes.

- is used for Group nodes.

- is used for Leaf nodes.

- is used for Nodes Fields nodes.

- is used for Leaf nodes.

- is used for Release Character nodes.

- is used for Repeat Delimiter nodes.

- is used for Segment Delimiter nodes.

- is used for Static nodes.

- is used for Subelement Delimiter nodes.

- is used for Tag delimiter nodes.


To view properties of each node, select the node in the Guideline pane, and then click the Properties Pane.

---

<a id="generate-parser-schema"></a>

### Generate Parser Schema

> 📄 Source: [Generate_Parser_Schema.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Generate_Parser_Schema.htm)

#### Generate Parser Schema


XEngine doesn't contain parser schemas for Delimited Flat Files and Positional Flat Files. With the Parser Schema Tool, you can generate parser schemas for delimited records.


To generate parser schema:

1. Open a DFF or PFF guideline.
2. On the Home tab of the ribbon, click Generate Parser Schema. The Create Parser Schema Tool dialog box is displayed.
3. In the Create Parser Schema Tool dialog box, in the Edit delimiters table, provide the Record Terminator you want to use.
4. Check the Create a new message if a data record matches the first record in a guideline to create a new message if a data record of a DFF/PFF file matches the first record in a guideline.
5. Click Browse and provide a file path and a parser schema name.
6. Click Save.


The parser schema is generated and saved. You can open the generated parser schema for further editing and use in data processing.

---

<a id="error-severity-configuration-files"></a>

## Error Severity Configuration Files

> 📄 Source: [Book_Severity_Configuration_Files.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Severity_Configuration_Files.htm)

### Error Severity Configuration Files


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="error-severity-configuration-file-overview"></a>

### Error Severity Configuration File Overview

> 📄 Source: [Severity_Configuration_File_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Severity_Configuration_File_Overview.htm)

#### Error Severity Configuration File Overview


With error severity configuration files, you can customize the way errors are handled by relaxing or tightening validation rules. User-defined error severity settings can be configured to either override the validation rules defined in guidelines (.ecs files) or to extend those rules with the additional criteria.


SpecBuilder generates XML-formatted error severity configuration files with the .esf extension that you can register in the XEngine registry settings file, or pass the name of the severity settings file to XEngine through a Selector object, which allows you to use a different error severity setting for each of your trading partners.


User-defined errors configured in an error severity configuration file are handled same way as standard validation errors. Their descriptions appear in XML and HTML error reports and error object collections. Acknowledgments display a code describing an error. Error severity settings are typically only applicable to HIPAA transactions, although they can be used for other data types in limited cases.


You can use the error severity configuration in addition to system settings. If the matching severity entry is found in the current configuration, it is used. Otherwise, an appropriate entry from system severity configuration is used. For this, on the Home tab of the ribbon, in the Configuration group, click Extension . Or use the error severity configuration to override system settings. Settings specified in this configuration are only used. For this, on the Home tab of the ribbon, in the Configuration group, click Override.


Note If the mode is not specified explicitly in the error severity configuration file, the Override mode is used by default. But note that if you use SpecBuilder to update the severity configuration, then the severity configuration file will always have a mode specified. If you update your configuration file manually, you need to use the Mode="extension" (or Mode="override") attribute in the SeverityConfigFile element since the default severity configuration file does not specify any mode. For example, <SeverityConfigFile Version="2.0" Mode="extension" …


Error severities are noted on reports and acknowledgments and divided into two main categories: rejecting and non-rejecting. The following error severities are rejecting:

- Critical: considered to be in violation of a guideline rule and causes XEngine to stop validation when encountered.
- Normal: considered to be in violation of a guideline rule but does not stop validation when encountered.


Warning, Ignore, and Information error severities are non-rejecting and do not stop validation.


Note The Information error severity is noted on reports only.


Error severities are divided into the following categories:


| Category | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Error | Rejecting | When severity is set to this type, it indicates that there is a data error and it should be fixed prior to further processing. XEngine does not pass data to the next step in the work flow and a negative acknowledgment is sent to the sender of the data. |
| Warning | Non-rejecting | When severity is set to this type, it indicates that there is something wrong with the data but processing may continue. XEngine notes the error in the acknowledgment document, but data gets passed to the next step in the work flow. |
| Information   | Non-rejecting | When severity is set to this type, it indicates that the data check reported a message that should be noted. XEngine does not note the error in the acknowledgment document and the data is passed to the next step in the work flow. The Information category includes the "Ignore" type that indicates that any data check message is suppressed and it is treated as clean data. XEngine makes a note of the error or suspect data and it is passed to the next step in the work flow document. "Information" is non-rejecting severity, which is shown in all error reports and which is not referenced in acknowledgments. But "Ignore" is severity, which leads to hiding the error from all reports and thus from acknowledgments as well. |



These three categories are standard and cannot be modified. There are default system error severities defined for each of the categories. You can also add your own severities based on these categories.

---

<a id="work-with-error-severity-configuration-files"></a>

### Work with Error Severity Configuration Files

> 📄 Source: [Book_Work_with_Error_Severity_Configuration_Files.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Work_with_Error_Severity_Configuration_Files.htm)

#### Work with Error Severity Configuration Files


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="error-severity-overview"></a>

#### Error Severity Overview

> 📄 Source: [Error_Severity_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Error_Severity_Overview.htm)

##### Error Severity Overview


Errors reported by [Analyzer](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Analyzer_Overview.htm) have an associated severity level. The following system-defined error severity types are available in SpecBuilder:


| Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Critical | Indicates that the data has errors and was not processed during data validation. In a production environment such as XEngine, it indicates that XEngine stops processing this particular session. |
| Normal | Indicates that the data has errors, and should be fixed for further processing. In a production environment such as XEngine, it indicates that data is not passed to the next step in the workflow and a negative acknowledgment is sent to the data sender. |
| Warning | Indicates that there are problems with the data, but the data can be processed. In a production environment such as XEngine, it indicates that an error is recorded in the acknowledgment document, but the data gets passed to the next step in the workflow. |
| Information | Indicates that the data check reported a message that should be recorded. In a production environment such as XEngine, it indicates that no error is recorded in the acknowledgment document, and the data gets passed to the next step in the workflow. |
| Ignore | Indicates that data check messages are suppressed, and the data is treated in the same manner as clean data. In a production environment such as XEngine, it indicates that no error is recorded in the error report or acknowledgment document and the data gets passed to the next step in the workflow. Note These errors are never printed. |

---

<a id="insert-and-edit-error-severity"></a>

#### Insert and Edit Error Severity

> 📄 Source: [Insert_Error_Severity.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Insert_Error_Severity.htm)

With Error severity configuration files, you can customize the way errors are handled by relaxing or tightening validation rules.


To insert Error Severity:

1. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click New, and then click Error Severity Configuration.
2. On the Home tab of the ribbon, in the Editing group, click Insert Error Severity. The Insert Error Severity dialog box is displayed.
3. Provide the following information:
4. Click OK.

1. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click New, and then click Error Severity Configuration.
2. Click the Error Severity you want to edit.
3. On the Home tab of the ribbon, in the Editing group, click Properties. The Error Severity Properties dialog box is displayed.

<a id="add-error-severity-from-a-guideline-map-and-data-errors"></a>

#### Add Error Severity from a Guideline, Map, and Data Errors

> 📄 Source: [Add_Error_Severity_from_a_Guideline_Map_Data_File.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_Error_Severity_from_a_Guideline_Map_Data_File.htm)

##### Add Error Severity from a Guideline, Map, and Data Errors


Once you have defined new error severities, you need to associate them to the Analyzer data checks. For instructions on defining new error severities, see [Insert and Edit Error Severity](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Insert_Error_Severity.htm).


To add an error severity from a guideline or a map:

1. Open a guideline or map document.
2. On the Home tab of the ribbon, in the Error Severity group, in the Current drop-down list, click the error severity configuration file you want to use for the open document.
3. In the guideline tree, right-click a node you want to add to the error severity, and then click Add Error Severity.


After Analyzer validates the data, validation errors can be added to the error severity configuration file.


To add an error severity from a data error:

1. Open a data file and run Analyzer.
2. In the Data Errors pane, right-click an error you want to add to the error severity configuration file, and then click:
3. Click the severity type you want to use for the selected error. For more information about severity types, see [Error Severity Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Error_Severity_Overview.htm).


The current error severity configuration file opens and displays the added entries.

<a id="manage-severities"></a>

1. On the Home tab of the ribbon, in the Manage group, click Severities. The Manage Severities dialog box is displayed.
2. Click Add to add a user defined severity.
3. In the created row, enter a severity name, type, and description.


To delete a user severity that you created, select the severity, and then click Delete.


Note: System severities cannot be edited or removed.

<a id="handle-snip-error-types"></a>

#### Handle SNIP Error Types

> 📄 Source: [Analyzer_Handle_SNIP_Error_Types.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Analyzer_Handle_SNIP_Error_Types.htm)

##### Handle SNIP Error Types


The following SNIP error types are available:


| SNIP Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| System | These are errors related to execution of the validation engine, such as a JavaScript file containing the invalid script |
| Type 1 | EDI Syntax integrity testing |
| Type 2 | HIPAA syntactical requirement testing |
| Type 3 | Balancing |
| Type 4 | Situation testing |
| Type 5 | External code set testing |
| Type 6 | A product type or line of services testing |
| Type 7 | Implementation Guide-Specific Trading Partners |
| Others | A custom category |



To hide/show SNIP error types:

1. In the Data Errors pane, click Customize ().
2. On the General tab of the **Data Error View Options** dialog box, select the error type to show and clear the error types to hide.
3. Click OK.


Note You can change the color of the selected error type by clicking Change color () or pressing Ctrl+L in the Data Error View Options dialog box.

---

<a id="error-severity-rules"></a>

#### Error Severity Rules

> 📄 Source: [Error_Severity_Rules.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Error_Severity_Rules.htm)

##### Error Severity Rules


In order to ensure that an error severity is applied to the correct validation messages, it is important to understand the match algorithm.  


First, ensure that the proper error severity file is used, and that the option to apply the error severity file as an add-on against the default is set correctly. If the add-on option is set, the analyzer checks the specified error severity file first, and only after that the system file. If the default option is set, then the analyzer simply uses the specified error severity file.


In either case, the Analyzer engine tries to determine the best match based on the most information and, if the analyzer finds a single match that error severity is used. If the analyzer finds no match, a broader search is done and if the analyzer finds a match, that error severity is used. If the analyzer finds no match, the default severity of Normal is applied. If the analyzer finds more than one match, the first error severity in the list is used.


Example


Analyzer finds an error caused by a non-matching data value for the code list 51, inside the CLM (Loop 2300). The SNIP Type for this error is 5.


###### Case 1:


Error severity rules:

- Apply the severity Warning when SNIP Type is 2.


###### Results:


The analyzer reports the previous error as severity type Normal when the analyzer finds no match based on the defined usage.


###### Case 2:


Error severity rules:

- Apply the severity Warning when SNIP Type is 2.
- Apply the severity Information when SNIP Type is 5.


###### Results:


The analyzer reports the previous error as severity type Information when the analyzer finds a match based on the defined usage of SNIP Type 5.


###### Case 3:


Error severity rules:

- Apply the severity Warning when SNIP Type is 2.
- Apply the severity Information when SNIP Type is 5.
- Apply the severity Warning when CodeListID is 51.


###### Results:


The analyzer reports the previous error as severity type Information when the analyzer finds two matches - first, based on the defined usage of SNIP Type is 5 and second, based on the code list being 51.  However, the analyzer selects the first match is based on the list order.


###### Case 4:


Error severity rules:

- Apply the severity Warning when SNIP Type is 2.
- Apply the severity Warning when CodeListID is 51.
- Apply the severity Information when SNIP Type is 5.


###### Results:


The analyzer reports the previous error as severity type Warning when the analyzer finds two matches - first, based on the defined CodeListID being 51 and second, based on SNIP type being 5. However the analyzer selects the first match is based on the list order.


###### Case 5:


Error severity rules:

- Apply the severity Warning when SNIP Type is 2.
- Apply the severity Information when SNIP Type is 5.
- Apply the severity Warning when CodeListID is 51 and SNIP Type is 5.


###### Results:


The analyzer reports the previous error as severity type Warning when the analyzer finds a more specific match based on the code list ID and the SNIP Type.  


###### Case 6:


Error severity rules:

- Apply the severity Warning when SNIP Type is 2.
- Apply the severity Information when SNIP Type is 5.
- Apply the severity Warning when CodeListID is 51 and SNIP Type is 5.
- Apply the severity Normal when the CodeListID is 51, SNIP Type is 5, and the Scope is Claim.


###### Results:


The analyzer reports the previous error as severity type Normal when the analyzer finds a more specific match based on the code list ID, the SNIP Type and the error is inside the scope of a claim loop.


###### Summary

1. Severity definitions are defined based on pre-defined types which are Error, Warning, Information, and Ignore.
2. Pre-defined severity definitions are provided, but you can create you own custom severity.
3. Severity definitions are then applied to the data checks based on Scope (Transaction or Claim), SNIP Type, Error ID, Node Selection, and Compound Criteria.
4. Multiple error severity configuration files can be used to apply different error severities based on the trading partner and transaction. Use the add-on option to apply both system and trading partner/transaction type specific files or use the default to only use the specified file.

---

<a id="work-with-avro-schema-registry"></a>

## Work with Avro Schema Registry

> 📄 Source: [Work_with_Avro_Schema_Registry.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Work_with_Avro_Schema_Registry.htm)

### Work with Avro Schema Registry


Edifecs SpecBuilder comes with a tool that is intended to work with Avro schema registry and allows you to:

- retrieve the list of subjects;
- check schema compatibility;
- upload or download schema for a specific subject.


To start working with the tool, navigate to the SpecBuilder root folder and run schema.registry.bat.


The following parameters are available:

- -url : The Avro schema registry URL (mandatory).
- -subject : the name of the registry subject. The Avro schema registry subject name consists of the Kafka topic's name and -value. For example, for the topic with the name 101, use 101-value as the subject.
- -schema : the path to .jar file with the Avro schema to upload or validate.
- -do : the action to perform on the schema (upload, download, or validate). When you download a schema, it is saved to the current folder with the file extension .avsc. The file name is the same as the name of the subject.


###### Tool Usage Examples

1. Get the list of available subjects from the Avro schema registry: -url http://localhost:8081
2. Check to see if the schema is compatible with the subject's last version: -url http://localhost:8081 -subject 101-value -schema 101.jar -do validate
3. Register the schema for the specified subject: -url http://localhost:8081 -subject 101-value -schema 101.jar -do upload
4. Download the schema from the specified subject: -url http://localhost:8081 -subject 101-value -do download

---

<a id="maps"></a>

## Maps

> 📄 Source: [Book_Maps.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Maps.htm)

### Maps


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="guideline-mapping-overview"></a>

### Guideline Mapping Overview

> 📄 Source: [Guideline_Mapping_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Guideline_Mapping_Overview.htm)

#### Guideline Mapping Overview


Guideline mapping visually represents the data translation between source and target specifications. These specifications are created by using SpecBuilder or come from the Standards Library. Where SpecBuilder allows you to create and validate B2B file formats, with guideline mapping, you can see how one guideline structure is transformed into another.


You can easily create map documents between two specifications, showing the transformation of data from one specification to another. Functions performed on data - for example, elements that are concatenated to form a single element - during the transformation can also be defined in the map document for documentation purposes.


Map documents are stored as an .ems extension and supports map packages as .empkg files. For more information about saving map packages, see [Save a Map Document](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Save_a_Map_Document.htm).


With guideline mapping, you can perform the following tasks:

- [Create a new map document](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_a_Map_Document.htm) from the Standards Library or from a guideline file.
- [Save a map document](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Save_a_Map_Document.htm) as an .ems file.
- Edit an existing map document.
- Customize a map document by:
- [Add functions](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_or_Remove_a_Function.htm) to your map document.
- [Connect guideline nodes](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_or_Remove_a_Connector.htm) with functions.
- [Publish new map document to MS Excel](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publish_to_Excel_XML_Wizard_Overview.htm).

---

<a id="supported-standards"></a>

### Supported Standards

> 📄 Source: [MB_Supported_Standards.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/MB_Supported_Standards.htm)

#### Supported Standards


The following combinations of standards are supported in guideline mapping:


|   | X12 | EDIFACT | HL7 | PFF | NCPDP Telecom | DFF | XML Schema | JSON |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| X12 | | | | | | | | |
| EDIFACT | | |   | |   | | | |
| HL7 | |   | | |   | | | |
| PFF | | | | | | | | |
| DFF | | | | | | | | |
| NCPDP Telecom | |   |   | | | | | |
| XML Schema | | | | | | | | |
| JSON | | | | | | | | |

---

<a id="work-with-map-documents"></a>

### Work with Map Documents

> 📄 Source: [Book_Work_with_Map_Documents.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Work_with_Map_Documents.htm)

#### Work with Map Documents


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="map-document-overview"></a>

#### Map Document Overview

> 📄 Source: [Map_Document_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Map_Document_Overview.htm)

##### Map Document Overview


A map document shows the relationship between a source and a target guideline along with any transformation that occurs. Mapping is shown by a [connector](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Connectors_Overview.htm) that links two [hot points](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Hot_Points.htm). The data transformation is performed by [functions](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Functions_Overview.htm) that transform input values to the output result.

---

<a id="create-a-map-document"></a>

#### Create a Map Document

> 📄 Source: [Create_a_Map_Document.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_a_Map_Document.htm)

##### Create a Map Document


When creating a map document, you can use files in .ecs or XSD format for source and target guidelines.


To create a new map document:

1. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click New, and then click Map.
2. In the New document creation dialog box, do one of the following:
3. Repeat step 2 for the target guideline.


Use Existing source option, to pick up the same guideline that was chosen as a source in the previous step. By selecting the same guideline you make the Auto Link Children Implicitly option available. For more information about this option, see [Create Implicit Connectors](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_Implicit_Connectors.htm).


Note If you are going to execute this map by using the XEngine Translator, you must select a guideline from a file or use the File Save As function on the File tab to save the map and guidelines once the map is complete.

---

<a id="save-a-map-document"></a>

#### Save a Map Document

> 📄 Source: [Save_a_Map_Document.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Save_a_Map_Document.htm)

##### Save a Map Document


Once you have created a map document, you can save it as:

- A Map (*.ems): a map without guidelines is saved.
- A Package (*.empkg): target guideline, source guideline and a map are saved in the same package as one file. You may choose what files to include into the package.
- A Map with Guidelines in the Same Folder (*.ems, *.ecs, *.ecs): all source guidelines, target guideline, and a map are saved in the same folder.


To save a map document:

1. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click Save, or click Save on the quick access toolbar.
2. In Save as type combo box, select how you want your document to be saved.

---

<a id="mapping-options"></a>

#### Mapping Options

> 📄 Source: [Mapping_Options.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Mapping_Options.htm)

##### Mapping Options


Use the Options dialog box to define Map Document settings.


To launch this dialog box:

- On the Tools tab of the ribbon, click Options, and then click Map tab.


The following settings are available:

- Use wizard for a new document: When selected, a wizard walks you through the creation of a new map document. If cleared, a blank map is displayed where you can add source and target guidelines, and functions.
- User notes for a new document: Use this option to edit global user notes. Add, delete or edit user note labels in the list.
- Publish empty user notes: Click to include empty user notes into the published document. For more information about Publishing to Excel, see [Publish to Excel XML Wizard Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publish_to_Excel_XML_Wizard_Overview.htm).
- Remember state of guideline views: Select this check box to remember the state of guideline nodes from the last session. If you clear the box, both source and target guidelines are shown as collapsed when opening a map document.
- Auto-linking: Use this option to implement the auto-link children functionality.
- Keep unmapped target nodes: Check the box to display all unmapped target nodes in the mismatched nodes folder on the sources side.
- Use 'always' data filter for auto-linked connectors: Check the box to use 'always' data filter for all connectors that were auto-linked.
- Force creation of group structures explicitly during auto-linking: Select this check box to enable force creation of group structures explicitly during auto-linking.
- Create a backup copy of a map document when map changes: Select this check box to automatically create an emergency backup copy of open map documents to prevent data loss in case of an error or power failure. The backup copy of the map document is saved to the same location the original file is saved. The backup file name starts with the ~ character followed by the original name of the file.
- Show map validation errors message box: Select this check box to get pop-up message when map validation errors are present.
- Show translator wizard completion message box: Select this check box to get a pop-up message when Translator finishes its job.

---

<a id="map-document-options"></a>

#### Map Document Options

> 📄 Source: [Map_Document_Options.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Map_Document_Options.htm)

##### Map Document Options


Use Map Document Options dialog box to edit user notes labels.


To edit map document options:

1. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click Document Options. The Map Document Options dialog box is displayed.
2. In the When emergency backup creation takes much time drop-down box, select one of the following:
3. In the Target creation order drop-down box, select one of the following:


To edit global user notes: 

1. In the Map Document Options dialog box click Global User Notes, and then click Edit.
2. Add, delete, or edit values in the list.
3. Click OK to go back to the Map Document Options.


You can only modify global user notes with this procedure. For information about editing local user notes, see [Function Properties User Notes Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Function_Properties_User_Notes_Tab.htm).

---

<a id="set-map-document-properties"></a>

#### Set Map Document Properties

> 📄 Source: [Set_Map_Document_Properties.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Set_Map_Document_Properties.htm)

##### Set Map Document Properties


When working with map documents, you can set their properties by using Map Document Properties dialog box.


The Map Document Properties allow to set general parameters for a map. These parameters are also used when [publishing map documents to MS Excel](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Publish_Map_Document_to_MS_Excel.htm).


To set the properties for the document:

1. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click Document Properties.
2. Type information into any of the following optional fields: - Title: The user-defined title of the implementation map. - Subject: The user-defined area where a subject can be entered. - Author: The name of the party who created the document. - Version: The version of the map. - Company: The company creating the map. - Trading Partner: The name of the trading partner. - Keywords: User-specific keywords that describe the map. - Specify if it's a **Draft** or **Final**. - **Location**: The location of the map. - **Created By**: SpecBuilder version, date and time when the map was created. - Status: The status of the guideline - Draft or Final. - **Notes**: Add any notes or remarks associated with the map.
3. Click OK.

---

<a id="auto-linking-overview"></a>

#### Auto-Linking Overview

> 📄 Source: [Auto_Linking_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Auto_Linking_Overview.htm)

##### Auto-Linking Overview


When you perform mapping and you might need to connect all child nodes of one parent node in the source guideline with all child nodes of another parent node in the target guideline. To perform this you can use Auto-Linking function. In this case, you do not have to do it manually for every individual node.


To use Auto-Linking:

1. Right-click parent node in source guideline, and then click Pick.
2. Right-click parent node in target guideline, and then click **Auto-Link Children**.
3. Select linking type you want to use.


*[Image: Auto-Linking]*


You can auto-link group nodes explicitly by using drag and drop option. Drag a node from a source and drop to a target guideline or vice versa, and select Auto-link Children Explicitly on the context menu.


*[Image: AutoLinking]*

---

<a id="variables"></a>

#### Variables

> 📄 Source: [Variables.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Variables.htm)

##### Variables


When working with the map documents you can use variables to implement your business needs. Variables can be set by using the Variables tab or by means of JavaScript, Java or .NET programming languages. You can view and manage variables by using the Variables tab in the Details pane. Use Change Variable function to set variable values according to the business needs.


In the debug mode Variable tab also displays the variables with their values and RecordSets created by means of JavaScript, Java or .NET programming languages.


When adding a new variable, make sure that its name starts with a letter or an underscore and contains only alphanumeric characters or underscores.


###### Variable Names Compatibility


Variable naming conventions have changed in SpecBuilder 8.3. With the current product behavior variable name must start with a letter or an underscore and must contain only alphanumeric characters or underscores. In case a variable is set by using JavaScript or any other programming language and its name contains invalid characters, the Translator engine is stopped with an error.


Example


"{Var123}" is an invalid variable name. Variable name must start with a letter and cannot contain spaces or non alpha-numeric characters. For example: Var123.


However, you can still use maps that contain variables and were created before SpecBuilder 8.3. Keep in mind that the behavior for variables resolution for such maps is different.


For example {Var123} and Var123 are considered to be two different variables. Thus if you run Translator in SpecBuilder 8.3 by using a map created before 8.3 and it contains a script that sets a variable with the name in braces and at the same time you have a variable in the map that has the same name but doesn't have braces, these variables are treated as two different variables. The Translator is not stopped in this case but a warning is triggered. We recommend you either rename such variables identically if they are the same, or provide unique names if the different variables are meant.

---

<a id="publish-to-excel-xml-wizard-overview"></a>

###### Publish to Excel XML Wizard Overview


You can use the Publish to Excel XML wizard to define the format and content of the exported document. The exported document has the Excel XML (*.xml) format.


The Publish to Excel wizard walks you through the following tasks:


[Define an output file name and select the settings to use](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/MB_Welcome_to_Publishing_to_Excel_Wizard.htm)


[Create a Title](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Title_Setup.htm)


[Set Publishing Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publishing_Options.htm)


[Define Source Section](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Setup_Source_and_Target_Sections.htm)


[Save Settings](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Save_Settings.htm)

<a id="welcome-to-publishing-to-excel-wizard"></a>

##### Welcome to Publishing to Excel Wizard

> 📄 Source: [MB_Welcome_to_Publishing_to_Excel_Wizard.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/MB_Welcome_to_Publishing_to_Excel_Wizard.htm)

###### Publish to Excel Wizard


To publish a map document to MS Excel:

1. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click Publish, and then click Excel.
2. Select the settings to be used for the export:
3. Click Next. If you choose to use default settings or settings from the external file not by using Advanced mode, you are asked if you want to view the exported document:


If you want to define new settings or to run the wizard in the advanced mode, go to [Template File Selection](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Template_File_Selection.htm).

<a id="template-file-selection"></a>

##### Template File Selection

> 📄 Source: [Template_File_Selection.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Template_File_Selection.htm)

###### Template File Selection


Use the Template File Selection page of the wizard to define the layout of the output document. This template defines only the look of the exported document and has an Excel XML (*.xml) file format. You can change the format of the template (for example, change the fonts or colors), but the structure is always the same. See the following example of the template:


*[Image: Output template]*

- Left Section - used for the lead guideline information.
- Middle Cell Section - used for map instructions.
- Right Section - used for the derived guideline information.


To provide a template file:

1. In the Template Excel file name box, type a name for the template file or click Browse to navigate to the location of the file.
2. Click Next to go to [Publishing Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publishing_Options.htm).

---

<a id="publishing-options"></a>

##### Publishing Options

> 📄 Source: [Publishing_Options.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publishing_Options.htm)

###### Publishing Options


Use the Publishing Options page to define how your map document should be displayed in the output. You can select the publishing order and choose what items to include.


To set publishing options:

1. Under Select publishing order, choose how you want your document to be published. The first guideline in this order is called the leading guideline.
2. Click Publish function settings to include settings information in your published document.
3. Click Limit publishing content for the leading guideline to define what you want to be published in the leading guideline, and then click one of the following options:
4. Click Generate document properties worksheet, to include Title, Subject, Author, Company, and Publish Date information to your document.
5. Click Publish connector properties to include the properties of each connector. See later for a description of how properties are published.
6. Click Next to go to the [Title Setup](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Title_Setup.htm) wizard page.


###### Publishing Connector Properties


| Filter Type | Filter Name | Excel Filter Name |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Guideline Filters | on end node | E |
|   | on last instance end | LE |
| Data Filters | not exists | - |
|   | always | * |
| Target-side manipulation | default value | D |
|   | force creation | F |



Note Default values of filters are not published.
