> 📄 Source: [Export_to_XML_Schema_XSD.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Export_to_XML_Schema_XSD.htm)

###### Export to XML Schema (XSD)


This function exports XML Schema guidelines to the XML Schema format.


To export to XML Schema:

1. Open your guideline file.
2. On the File menu, click Export to a Data Format.
3. In Choose an export to perform, click XML Schema (XSD), and then click Next.
4. In the Save resulting files to the folder field, enter a path to save exported file or click Browse to navigate to the location.
5. Click Save.
6. Select Save guideline before exporting to save a guideline that matches the exported document, and then click Next.
7. On the Options page of the wizard, select a schema node, which you want to export.
8. Click Next.
9. Check the Flatten Xml Schema option in case you want to consolidate a group of XML Schemas into the fewest possible set of files. Components of every included schema are added as global components of the flattened schema.
10. Click Finish to close the Export wizard.


Note While the file exports, you can click Close on Finish to automatically close the wizard when the export finishes.

<a id="export-to-jdata-schema-json"></a>

##### Export to JData Schema (JSON)

> 📄 Source: [Export_to_JData_Schema.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Export_to_JData_Schema.htm)

###### Export to JData Schema (JSON)


JData Schema (JSON) is an analogue of XData XML Schema and converts from the EDI, DFF, PFF, HL7, NCPDP, and DFF guidelines.


To export a guideline to JData Schema (JSON):

1. Open your guideline file.
2. On the File menu, click Export to a Data Format.
3. In Choose an export to perform, click JData Schema (JSON), and then click Next.
4. On the Export destination page, in Save exported file as enter a path and a file name for the exported document or click Browse to navigate to the location, and then enter a name for the file.
5. Click Save.
6. Select Save guideline before exporting to save a guideline that matches the exported document.
7. Select Show advanced options to manually configure the export templates, and then click Next.
8. Select the type of schema to be exported: **Light JSON Schema**, performing only the top-level validation or **Full JSON Schema**, performing validation and verifying elements using the available values, and click Next.
9. If you selected **Full JSON Schema**, the Options page is displayed.
10. On the Customize element and type name templates wizard page, you can choose to generate the default node name or select Use abbreviated element name prefixes to reduce the default node name to a single character, for example, S for Segment. Both element names are compatible with XEngine. Click *[Image: Use Custom Type]* to display the additional type name option. Select **Use abbreviated type name prefixes** to reduce type name to a prefix, for example, T for Transaction.
11. For each node type in the guideline, click to define the name as a combination of various properties from the specification. The Template editor dialog box is displayed.
12. Click OK, and then click Next to export the document.
13. Click Finish to close the Export Wizard page.
14. Note While the file is exporting, you can click Close on Finish to automatically close the wizard once the export is complete.

---

<a id="export-to-json-schema"></a>

##### Export to JSON Schema

> 📄 Source: [Export_to_JSON_Schema.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Export_to_JSON_Schema.htm)

###### Export to JSON Schema


You can export a JSON guideline to the JSON Schema Draft 4 format.


To export a guideline to JSON Schema:

1. Open your JSON guideline file.
2. On the File menu, click Export to a Data Format.
3. In Choose an export to perform, click JSON Schema, and then click Next.
4. On the Export destination page, in Save exported file as, enter a path and a file name for the exported document or click Browse to navigate to the location, and then enter a name for the file.
5. Click Save.
6. Select Save guideline before exporting to save a guideline that matches the exported document, click Next, and then in the Save As dialog box, navigate to the destination folder, and provide a file name, or click Save to save an existing file without a file name.
7. Click Finish to close the Export Wizard page.

---

<a id="export-to-avro-data-schema"></a>

##### Export to Avro Data Schema

> 📄 Source: [Export_to_Avro_Data_Schema.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Export_to_Avro_Data_Schema.htm)

###### Export to Avro Data Schema


You can export EDI, HL7, NCPDP, DFF, PFF, and XML Schema guidelines to Avro Data Schema (JAR and Avro formats) if you need to generate Avro Java Model of a guideline.


Avro is a data framework that is convenient and widely used for data serialization. A key feature of Avro is powerful support for all the data schemas that change with times – that is often referred to as schema evolution. Avro handles the following schema changes: missing fields, added fields, and changed fields, which helps old programs read new data and in reverse, helps the new programs read the old data.


To export a guideline to Avro Data Schema:

1. Open your guideline file.
2. On the File menu, in Export, click Export to a Data Format.
3. In Choose an export to perform, click Avro Data Schema, and then click Next.
4. On the Export destination page, in Save exported file as, enter a path and a file name for the exported .jar file or click Browse and navigate to the location where the exported guideline must be saved, enter a name for the file, and then click Save.
5. If you need to generate Avro schema with this export, select Show advanced options, click Next, and make sure that the option Enable schema generation in the Avro format in the .avsc file is selected. The .avsc file will be saved with the same name to the same location that you specified for the .jar file at the previous step.
6. Click Next to start the conversion.
7. Click Finish to close the Export Wizard page.

---

<a id="compare-and-migrate-guidelines"></a>

### Compare and Migrate Guidelines

> 📄 Source: [Book_Compare_and_Migrate_Guidelines.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Compare_and_Migrate_Guidelines.htm)

#### Compare and Migrate Guidelines


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="migrator-overview"></a>

#### Migrator Overview

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Overview.htm)

| # Migrator Overview | |
| --- | --- |



Migrator is used to compare and change guideline content, including user, system rules, and custom matching rules. The following are some of the most common uses for Migrator:

- Update a previously-created document from its current version to a newer version
- Compare different versions of a standard to visualize the changes
- Compare a document to a newer version of the standard to determine if you need to upgrade
- Troubleshoot problems with guidelines by identifying changes
- [Incorporate new system rules](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrate_New_System_Rules.htm)
- Create custom rules for matching on ID or Name


Migrator shows differences between guidelines in a visual way and allows you to decide what if any of the differences should be applied.


Note Migrator only supports .ecs files. Use SpecBuilder to open or import a file not in .ecs format, and then create a new document by using the Save As command. You can then open the document in Migrator to compare it to another file and apply any necessary changes.


###### Features and Benefits

- User Interface: State-of-the-art, intuitive, and easy-to-use interface for viewing and navigation through guidelines.
- Standards: Support for X12 (including subsets such as VICS, UCS, X12N, HIPAA), EDIFACT (including subsets), TRADACOMS, Delimited Flat Files, Positional Flat Files, RosettaNet, HL7 (including version 3.0) and NCPDP (Telecom and Batch) and XML Schema.
- Cost Savings: Reduces the time spent in migrating to newer versions of standards.


###### Migrator Architecture


The following diagram shows the migration process.


*[Image: SpecBuilder Migrator]*

---

<a id="migrator-interface"></a>

#### Migrator Interface

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Interface.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Interface.htm)

##### Migrator Interface


The Migrator user interface is displayed in a double-pane format for each guideline, with a tree view in the top pane. The top Guideline Pane displays the guideline nodes for both source and destination guidelines. The status of these nodes depends on the [Comparison Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Comparison_Options.htm). Properties Pane (at the bottom) displays the properties of the node selected in the Guideline Pane and shows actual property differences.


*[Image: Migrator]*


Use the following toolbar options to work with guidelines:


| To | Do this |
| --- | --- |
| Only migrate the selected node | Click Migrate Current Node (). |
| Migrate a selected node with all children | Click Migrate the subtree (). |
| Browse through the different nodes skipping all other nodes | Click Previous or Next Difference (). |
| Select source and target guidelines | Click Open (). |
| Save destination document | Click Save Destination Document (). |
| Preview the document | Click Preview (). |
| Print out the document | Click Print (). |
| Remove selected destination node | Click Delete Destination Node (). |
| Exclude selected destination node | Click Exclude Destination Node (). |



Use Guideline pane and Properties pane toolbar (in the middle) to work with differences found during the comparison process.

- shows that the destination node is missing.

- shows that some attributes of the node are different.

- shows that the source node is missing.

- shows that some attributes and child nodes are different.

- shows that some child nodes are different.


For more information about migrating process, see [Migrating Nodes and Properties](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrate_Nodes_and_Properties.htm).

---

<a id="the-migrator-wizard"></a>

#### The Migrator Wizard

> 📄 Source: [Book_The_Migrator_Wizard.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_The_Migrator_Wizard.htm)

##### The Migrator Wizard


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="migrator-wizard-overview"></a>

##### Migrator Wizard Overview

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Wizard_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Wizard_Overview.htm)

###### Migrator Wizard Overview


The Migrator wizard steps you through the migrating and comparing of the guidelines. 


 There are several scenarios of how Migrator can be used:

- Compare an older standard to a newer standard (most common use of Migrator)
- Compare an older standard to a newer guideline document
- Compare an older guideline to a newer guideline document
- Migrate an older guideline to a newer standard
- Troubleshoot problems with guidelines by identifying changes
- [Incorporate new system rules](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrate_New_System_Rules.htm)


Note You can only use .ecs files in the Migrator. You must open and save older .eds guidelines as .ecs files before using the Migrator.


Please click the following links to get detailed Help on the wizard screen you are viewing: 

- [Select source and destination guidelines](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Select_Source_or_Destination_Guideline.htm)
- [Comparison Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Comparison_Options.htm)
- [Migration Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Migration_Options.htm)
- [Match Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Match_Options.htm)
- [Complete Migration/Comparison wizard](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Complete_Comparison_Migration.htm)


Note You can also open guidelines without going through the wizard. Drag two guideline files from a folder and drop them on the Migrator window to open for comparing and migrating.


After performing the drag-and-drop operation, you can swap the two guidelines to start comparison. To swap guideline, on the Tools menu, click Swap source with destination.

---

<a id="select-source-or-destination-guideline"></a>

##### Select Source or Destination Guideline

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Select_Source_or_Destination_Guideline.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Select_Source_or_Destination_Guideline.htm)

###### Select Source or Destination Guideline


In the Guideline Selection dialog box, you can select the guidelines to be migrated.


To select a guideline:

1. Do one of the following:
2. Click Next.
3. Repeat the same steps for selecting a destination guideline.
4. Click Next to go to [Comparison Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Comparison_Options.htm).

---

<a id="comparison-options"></a>

##### Comparison Options

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Comparison_Options.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Comparison_Options.htm)

###### Comparison Options


Use the Comparison Options dialog box to set the options that are used in the comparison process.


*[Image: Comparison Options]*

- Ignore Whitespace: Select this check box to ignore whitespace.
- Ignore Nodes that are different
- Never: Click to always show the differences between nodes.
- If both source and destination are excluded or missing: Click to skip different nodes if they are excluded or missing in both source and destination guidelines.
- If either source or destination is excluded or missing: Click to skip different nodes if they are excluded or missing in one of the guidelines.
- Document Options Comparison Settings: Check the properties to be compared.


Check all the node properties you want to compare, and then click Next to go to the [Migration Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Migration_Options.htm).

---

<a id="migration-options"></a>

##### Migration Options

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Migration_Options.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Migration_Options.htm)

###### Migration Options


Use the Migration Options dialog box to set the options that is used in the migration process.


*[Image: Migration Options]*

1. Check Validate Java Action when saving destination guideline box to validate Java actions automatically when you save a destination guideline.
2. In the Document Options Migration Settings group, check the document options you want to be migrated.
3. Check all the nodes and properties that should be migrated from the source to the destination. For example, if you know that certain properties are different and the destination property value is the correct one, those properties can be cleared in this screen and the destination property does not get overridden during the migration of that node. You can also check the User Rules check box to migrate
4. Click Next to go to the [Match Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Match_Options.htm).

---

<a id="match-options"></a>

##### Match Options

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Match_Options.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Match_Options.htm)

###### Match Options


Custom Rules Matching User Note Matching Cross Linking within group of segments of same ID


Use the Match Options dialog box to select the key attributes that Migrator uses to find matches.


*[Image: Match Options dialog box]*

1. Select the Case Sensitive check box to match text by case.
2. Select node attributes that are used as key attributes to find the match. For example, if you want Migrator to establish a match when loops have the same ID, check the box marked ID under the Loop folder as it is shown in the preceding picture. If you want Migrator to match by name as well as ID, check both.
3. Click Next to proceed to the [Complete the Comparison/Migration wizard](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Complete_Comparison_Migration.htm).


###### Additional Matching Options


###### Custom Rules Matching


Migrator provides the ability to create custom rules for matching. Normally, Migrator would match on ID and/or Name exactly, among other matching options. Starting with version 6.7, you have the ability to create custom rules to match items with the same business information, regardless of ID or name.


Custom options for migration of HIPAA 4010A1 to 5010 are provided with version 6.7. They are stored in %ECRootPath%\STANDARDS\Shared\X12N\MigratorOptions\HIPAA\4010A1-5010 and can be loaded when setting migration options.


Here is an example of custom rules based on ID:


*[Image: Custom rules based on ID]*


An example of custom rules based on Name:


*[Image: Custom rules based on Name]*


Prior to using custom rules, this would be shown in Migrator:


*[Image: Migrator]*


To add a custom rule:

1. On the Match Options screen, under the appropriate node type, click the box in front of ID or Name depending on what you want to match by using custom rules.
2. Click Custom Rules, and then click Edit.
3. Do one of the following:
4. Click OK.


Note the following issues:

- Spaces in the rule are ignored.
- Before you can create a rule matching an ID to a different ID, you must break the rule matching it to the same ID. For example, in the previous example, before the rule matching 2310D to 2310C, the match between 2310C and 2310C needed to be broken.
- Rules on names are not case sensitive.


###### User Note Matching


In some cases the user notes may have been moved between the source and destination guidelines. What may have been user note 3 may now be user note 4. If this is the case, you can adjust the comparison so user note 3 is compared to user note 4.


To change the match algorithm for user notes:

1. Go the appropriate guideline node type such as Segment, Element, and the rest.
2. Click User Notes match rules, you see a customize button appear next to the text in the tree.
3. Click Customize and following dialog box is displayed:
4. Use the Move Up or Move Down arrows on the toolbar to change the Destination User Note to compare.


Note To adjust the user note comparison start by setting the appropriate comparison for Source User Note 1, then Source User Note 2 and so forth. If you set the Source User Note 2 comparison first, and then Source User Note 1, the User Note 2 gets reset.


###### Cross Linking within group of segments of same ID


If your guideline uses the old Key Type property to resolve ambiguity when the same segment occurs more than once within the scope of the same parent node such as a Loop, it can also be used in the Migrator to match segments properly by setting this option. For example, if your guideline structure is as follows:


| Source Structure | Destination Structure |
| --- | --- |
| Loop 2100 - Claim Payment | Loop 2100 - Claim Payment |
| REF - Other Claim ID | REF - Rendering Provider ID |
| REF - Rendering Provider ID | REF - Other Claim ID |



Without setting this option, REF - Other Claim ID matches to Rendering Provider ID, but when this option is set - cross linking is performed and each source REF segment is compared to it's corresponding destination REF segment.


Note Migrator's performance degrades if you choose to customize User Notes match rules for codes.

---

<a id="printing-options"></a>

##### Printing Options

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Printing_Options.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Printing_Options.htm)

###### Printing Options


Use the Printing Options wizard to set printing options and the key attribute of each node type to be used for printing.


*[Image: Printing Options wizard]*

- Print Cover Page: Select this check box to include the cover page to the printed document.
- Print Difference Only: Select this check box to print only the nodes which are different.
- Print code difference only: Select this check box to print only the code lists which are different.
- Print detail attributes even source or destination is missing: Select this check box to print the detail attributes even if source or destination is missing.
- Print rich user note as HTML source: Select this check box to print a rich user note as HTML source.
- Page Layout:
- Margins: Set Left, right, top and bottom margin step.
- Header/Footer start position: Set header or footer position in the printed document.
- Header content: Set the content for the header and its location.
- Footer content: Set the content for the footer and its location.
- Document Options Printing Settings: Click the node labels which you want to print.


Click the properties you want to print for each node.

---

<a id="complete-comparisonmigration"></a>

##### Complete Comparison/Migration

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Complete_Comparison_Migration.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Complete_Comparison_Migration.htm)

###### Complete Comparison/Migration


Use this wizard to verify the summary information about source and destination guidelines and set the start-up options.


*[Image: Complete the Comparison/Migration Wizard]*

- Start Migration Automatically: Select this check box to automatically migrate source properties to the destination when they are different.

- Compare Codes at Start: Select this check box to compare each member of the code lists before displaying differences. If this is checked, if the lists contain all the same codes (even if they are in a different sequence), the lists show as matching.

<a id="migrate-nodes-and-properties"></a>

#### Migrate Nodes and Properties

> 📄 Source: [Migrate_Nodes_and_Properties.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrate_Nodes_and_Properties.htm)

##### Migrate Nodes and Properties


You can migrate either nodes or individual node properties.


To migrate nodes:


Do one of the following:

- On the toolbar, click Migrate current node () or Migrate the subtree ().
- Right-click the node and choose Migrate Node or Migrate node with Children.
- On the Tools menu, click Migrate Node or Migrate Node with Children.


To migrate node properties:


On the Properties pane, right-click the property you want to migrate and choose Migrate Property.


*[Image: Migrator properties pane]*


Note After two guidelines are opened inside the Migrator window, you can swap two guidelines without relinking/recomparing by clicking Swap Source with Destination on the Tools menu.

---

<a id="view-properties-dialog-box"></a>

#### View Properties Dialog Box

> 📄 Source: [View_Properties_Dialog_box.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/View_Properties_Dialog_box.htm)

##### View Properties Dialog Box


Merging Function


You can use the View Properties dialog box to compare a node's property information in both the source and destination document side-by-side. It is useful to see this information when the Properties pane cannot completely show all information due to space limitation.


To launch Properties dialog box:

- In the Properties pane, double-click the property you want to view.
- Use the Properties drop-down list to select the property to display.
- Use the Previous Difference and Next Difference () at the bottom of the dialog box to move to the previous or next difference.
- Use the Migrate Property () at the bottom of this dialog to migrate the property from the source to the destination. This operation is not controlled by the [Migration Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Migration_Options.htm).


###### Merging Function


The merging function enables you to compare and merge a node's property information from the source to the destination document. The merging function is available for such text types as:

- User Notes
- User Action
- Semantics
- Set Notes
- Set Comment


If the merging function is available for the node, then there is no ReadOnly indicator in the Destination Document pane in the View Properties dialog box as it is shown in the following picture:


*[Image: Migrator merging function]*


To merge properties information:

1. Do one of the following:
2. In the Source Document pane, right-click the line you want to merge.
3. Choose Copy line to right.

---

<a id="publish-reports"></a>

#### Publish Reports

> 📄 Source: [Select_the_Report_Style.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Select_the_Report_Style.htm)

##### Publish Reports


After you have compared and migrated your documents and the destination guideline is ready you can print it to paper or publish to RTF, PDF, HTML, Word XML or Excel XML.


To publish a guideline to RTF, PDF, HTML, Word XML or Excel XML:

1. On the File tab, in the Backstage view, click Publish to HTML, RTF, PDF, WORD, EXCEL or X12 Companion Guide.
2. Enter a file name for the output.
3. Select from three different types of report layouts:


The following option is available for Word XML publishing:

- Update page references if possible: Click to update the page references in the published document. This option is only available if you have Word 2003 or later installed at the time of the publishing.


For information about publishing to Excel, see [Publish to Excel XML Wizard Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Publishing_to_Excel_XML_Wizard_Overview.htm).

<a id="migrator-settings"></a>

#### Migrator Settings

> 📄 Source: [Migrator_Settings.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migrator_Settings.htm)

##### Migrator Settings


When setting Migrator options, you can save them to the file to use them later or set your options as default. You can do it in both ways when going through the wizard or setting options through the Tools menu. The following commands are available for [Comparison options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Comparison_Options_Tab.htm), [Migration options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Migration_Options_Tab.htm), [Printing options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Print_Options_Tab.htm), and [Matching options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Match_Options_Tab.htm):

- Click Load settings from file (), to navigate to the location of the file with the settings.
- Click Save settings to file (), to save your setting to the file.
- Click Clear All (), to clear all the boxes on the settings page.
- Click Select All (), to check all the boxes on the settings page.
- Click Reset (), to go back to the last saved settings.
- Click Set as default (), to set your settings as default.


For the HIPAA standard, basic Migrator options are provided in the read-me mode. You cannot modify them. In order to set your own Migrator options for HIPAA guidelines, you have to save the settings file with another name.

In some cases, you may only want to incorporate new system rules into a current document. To do that, use the following process:

<a id="flat-files"></a>

### Flat Files

> 📄 Source: [Book_Flat_Files.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Flat_Files.htm)

#### Flat Files


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="flat-files-overview"></a>

#### Flat Files Overview

> 📄 Source: [Flat_Files_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Flat_Files_Overview.htm)

##### Flat Files Overview


Delimited Flat File Format Positional Flat File Format


Enterprise applications use hierarchically-organized record structures that have been traditionally presented in files where this hierarchy has been flattened by the use of delimiters or record/field size indicators. The structure of the file is usually defined by a meta-schema that is separate from the data file itself.


Flat files consist of the following three core component types:

- Fields: Atomic pieces of information such as the last name of a person.
- Records (Segments): Groups of fields that are considered to be a set of logical information.
- Group: A set of records.


Flat files are of two common types and are supported fully by SpecBuilder:

- Delimited Flat Files: The component structure is indicated by delimiters that separate various components within the file.
- Positional Flat Files: The component structure is indicated by the position (location) of the component within the file.


###### Delimited Flat File Format


A Delimited Flat File is very similar to an EDI structure and is composed of variable- or fixed-length fields separated by a field delimiter. Unlike EDI formats that are defined by various standards organizations, Delimited Flat Files are usually defined by individual companies for their internal use. They are sometimes also exchanged among trading partners.


Delimited Flat Files are composed of fields, which are variable-length and separated by a specific field delimiter. Fields are then grouped into records and records have a common end-of-record terminator. Records can be further grouped into group records. Group records do not appear in the data and are used in the guideline to present the logical structure.


The following is an example of a Delimited Flat File with a description of the structure of the data file:


010,John Adams,123 First St,Redmond,WA,98052,123-456-7890


010,Abraham Lincoln,1 Main Way,Baton Rouge,LA,70805,111-222-3333


Field Delimiter value is: ","


Record Delimiter value is: CRLF (Carriage return and Line Feed)


###### Positional Flat File Format


A Positional Flat File is composed of fields that are the same fixed length and records that have a common end-of-record terminator. The structure of an incoming file must be represented in the records and fields of the source specification so the positional nature of the incoming file is preserved. Therefore, before defining the document structure of a source specification, obtain a layout of the necessary records and fields.


The following table shows an example of a fixed format:


| Name | Address | City | State | ZipCode | Phone |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| John Adams          | 123 First St.                 | Redmond | WA | 98052 | 123-456-7890 |
| Abraham Lincoln    | 1 Main Ave.                     | Baton Rouge          | LA | 70805 | 111-222-3333 |



The Name field is fixed at a maximum of 18 characters, the Address field maximum is 30 characters, the City field maximum is 20 characters, the State field is 2 characters, the ZipCode field is 5 characters and the Phone field maximum is 12 characters. The end-of-record terminator is a carriage return and/or line feed character(s).

---

<a id="set-tag-values-in-positional-flat-files"></a>

#### Set Tag Values in Positional Flat Files

> 📄 Source: [Set_Tag_Values_in_Positional_Flat_Files.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Set_Tag_Values_in_Positional_Flat_Files.htm)

##### Set Tag Values in Positional Flat Files


When processing a Positional Flat File, the processing engine needs to know what type of record is being processed in order to match the data to the guideline. Normally, the first field of the record is the record identifier, but any field could be used. Tag values are used to create the identifying information. The tag value is set to the record type.


There are two ways to set the tag value - in the Properties pane, or by using the Home tab.


*[Image: Set the tag value]*


To set tag values in the Properties pane:

1. Open a Positional Flat File guideline.
2. Click the field that identifies the record.
3. In the Properties pane, scroll down to the Tag Settings section.
4. In the Tag Field box, choose the type of tag.
5. In the Tag Value box, type the value that matches the record.


In this example, this is an AA0 record, so the tag type is Value and the value is EDI_DC40.


To set tag values by using the Home tab:


You can set multiple tag values quickly by using the Set Tag Values dialog box.

1. Open a Positional Flat File guideline.
2. On the Home tab, click Set Tag Values.
3. In the Set Tag Values dialog box, in the Records pane, click a record.
4. In the Fields pane, click the field that identifies the record.
5. Click the corresponding button to perform the following tasks:

- Set Current - Sets the record type as the tag value for the selected field.
- Set All - Sets the record type as the tag value for all fields with the same ID as the selected field.
- Set All Nth - Sets the record type as the tag value in all records to a field in a given position. For example, to set the tag value of the third field in each record, enter "3" in the box, and then clickSet All Nth.
- Clear - Clears all tag values.

---

<a id="set-data-types-in-positional-flat-files"></a>

#### Set Data Types in Positional Flat Files

> 📄 Source: [Set_data_type_for_PFF.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Set_data_type_for_PFF.htm)

##### Set Data Types in Positional Flat Files


Each field in a record belongs to a data type. To select a data type for a field:

1. Open a positional flat file guideline in SpecBuilder.
2. Click the pane Properties in the data pane.
3. Click Type to open the drop-down menu of a field. The drop-down displays the options available, as shown on the following image:


###### PICTURE clauses


A PICTURE clause is an element used to describe a datum, by using sample characters that indicate the item characteristics and size.


A PICTURE clause comprises various format characters, each of which represents a certain portion of the data item. You can repeat each format character or follow it up by a repeat number, which specifies the number of times the format item occurs in the data item. Some examples (from COBOL) are:


| Format Example | Sample Values |
| --- | --- |
| 999 or 9(3) | 123, 005, and 087 - any number from 000 through 999 |
| AAAAAAAA or A(8) | Fredrick, Fred , and Fred Jr, any string of 8 alphabetic letters (may include spaces) |



###### Type Property


The node Type property, which works in conjunction with the Format properties, describes a data type. The following data types are available for a COBOL Copy Book Positional Flat File (PFF).


| Type | Data | Available PICTURE Clause for Format Property |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Binary | COBOL binary numeric format | 0, 9, S |
| Computational | COBOL binary numeric format | 0, 9, S |
| Computational-1 | COBOL floating point numeric format. Specified for internal floating-point items (single precision). The leftmost bit contains the sign and the next 7 bits contain the exponent; the remaining 3 bytes contain the mantissa. | no format is specified |
| Computational-2 | COBOL floating point numeric format. Specified for internal floating-point items (double precision). COMP-2 items are 8 bytes long. The leftmost bit contains the sign and the next 7 bits contain the exponent; the remaining 7 bytes contain the mantissa. | no format is specified |
| Computational -3 | COBOL Computation numeric type. Binary field type that puts ("packs") two digits into each byte, by using a notation called Binary Coded Decimal. This halves the storage requirements compared to a character, or COBOL "display" field. Packed-decimal items occupy 1 byte of storage for every two decimal digits you code in the PICTURE description, except that the rightmost byte contains only one digit and the sign. | 9, S, V, P |
| Computational-5 | COBOL Computation numeric type. The data items can contain values up to the capacity of the native binary representation (2, 4 or 8 bytes), rather than being limited to the value implied by the number of nines in the PICTURE for the item. When you move or store numeric data into a COMP-5 item, truncation occurs at the binary field size rather than at the COBOL PICTURE size limit. When a COMP-5 item is referenced, the full binary field size is used. | 9, S, V, P |
| Computational-6 | COBOL Computation numeric type. To maintain the size and capacity of the data items, the COBOL system treats COMP-6 data items as COBOL's COMP format fields, and pads the field with binary zeros where necessary. | 9, S, V, P |
| Date | Date Time | A specific calendar date. Possible formats: • YY A windowed (two-digit) year • YYYY An expanded (four-digit) year • X A single character; for example, a digit representing a semester or quarter (1-4) • XX Two characters; for example, digits representing a month (01-12) • XXX Three characters; for example, digits representing a day of the year (001-366) • XXXX Four characters; for example, two digits representing a month (01-12) and two digits representing a day of the month (01-31) |
| Display | COBOL Computation numeric type | P, S, V, Z, comma, ., E, $, 0, 9, +, -, *, CR, DB, / |
| Display Sign Leading | COBOL Computation numeric type | P, S, V, Z, comma, ., E, $, 0, 9, +, -, *, CR, DB, / |
| Display Sign Leading Separate | COBOL Computation numeric type | P, S, V, Z, comma, ., E, $, 0, 9, +, -, *, CR, DB, / |
| Display Sign Trailing Separate | COBOL Computation numeric type | P, S, V, Z, comma, ., E, $, 0, 9, +, -, *, CR, DB, / |
| Dollar | Dollar (over punch character) | $, c |
| Explicit Sign Number | Denotes positive or negative numbers | 9, P, S, V |
| Identifier | Identifier | A, B, X, /, 9, 0 |
| National | COBOL Computation numeric type | P, S, V, Z, comma, ., E, $, 0, 9, +, -, *, CR, DB, N, G, / |
| National Sign Leading Separate | COBOL Computation numeric type | P, S, V, Z, comma, ., E, $, 0, 9, +, -, *, CR, DB, N, G, / |
| National Sign Trailing Separate | COBOL Computation numeric type | P, S, V, Z, comma, ., E, $, 0, 9, +, -, *, CR, DB, N, G, / |
| Overpunch Signed Number | Over Punch characters | Over punch characters {, }, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I J, K, L, M, N, O, P, Q, R |
| String | Alpha-numeric | A, B, X, /, 9, 0 |
| Time | Time | 9(4) allows two digits for the month and two digits for the year. Also accepts a 9(8) PICTURE format: • Two digits for hour of day • Two digits for minute of hour • Two digits for second of minute • Two digits for hundredths of second |



**Note** DISPLAY/ DISPLAY SIGN LEADING/DISPLAY SIGN LEADING SEPARATE/DISPLAY SIGN TRAILING SEPARATE: Each position (byte) of storage contains one decimal digit. This means the items are stored in displayable form. External decimal items that have DISPLAY are referred to as zoned decimal data items. When DISPLAY is in effect for a floating-point data item, each PICTURE character position (except for v, an implied decimal point, if used) takes 1 byte of storage. External floating-point items that have DISPLAY are referred to as display floating-point data items when necessary to distinguish them from external floating-point items that have NATIONAL.


**Note** NATIONAL/NATIONAL LINE LEADING SEPARATE/NATIONAL LINE TRAILING SEPARATE: When NATIONAL is in effect for a category numeric data item, 2 bytes of storage are required for each decimal digit. The items are stored as national characters in UTF-16 format. External decimal items that have NATIONAL are referred to as national decimal data items. National decimal data items, if signed, must have the SIGN SEPARATE clause in effect. All other rules for zoned decimal items apply to national decimal items. You can use national decimal items anywhere that other category numeric data items can be used. When NATIONAL is in effect for a floating-point data item, each PICTURE character position (except for v, if used) takes 2 bytes of storage. External floating-point items that have NATIONAL are referred to as national floating-point data items. The existing rules for display floating-point items apply to national floating-point items.


For more information, see COBOL Language Manual > Data Types.


###### Format Property


The Format property accepts a COBOL PICTURE clause that corresponds to the Type property. Refer to the Enterprise COBOL for z/OS, Version 3 Release 3 Language Reference Manual for restrictions and additional information. The following values are available:


| PICTURE Clause | General Meaning | Size |
| --- | --- | --- |
| asterisk (*) | A check project symbol. | 1 byte for each asterisk |
| comma (,) | The character is a comma. | 1 byte for each comma |
| cent sign ( c ) | The character is a cent sign. | 1 byte for each cent symbol |
| dollar sign ($) | The character is a dollar sign. | 1 byte for each dollar sign |
| minus (-) | The minus character marks a negative number. | 1 byte |
| period (.) | The period marks a decimal point. | 1 byte for each period |
| plus (+) | The plus character marks a negative number. | 1 byte |
| slash (/) | The character is a forward slash. | 2 bytes |
| zero (0) | The character is a zero value. | 1 byte |
| nine (9) | The character is a numeric value. | 1 byte |
| B | The character is a space. | For non-DBCS data- 1 byte. For DBCS data 2 bytes |
| CR | The characters are CR for a credit value. | 1 byte |
| DB | The characters are DB for a debit value. | 1 byte |
| E | The start of the exponent. | 1 byte |
| G | A DBCS character position. | 2 bytes |
| N | The 16-bit Unicode Transformation Format (UTF-16) representation. | 2 bytes |
| P | The location of the decimal point in a number. | Not counted in the size of the data item |
| S | The location of the positive or negative sign in a number. | Not counted in the size of the data item |
| V | An implied decimal point, for example, 9V9(9)E-99. | 1 byte |
| X | Any alpha-numeric or special character. | 1 byte |
| Z | The leading numeric character such as a space or a zero character. | 1 byte for each Z |

---

<a id="set-data-types-in-delimited-flat-files"></a>

#### Set Data Types in Delimited Flat Files

> 📄 Source: [Set_Data_Types_in_DFF.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Set_Data_Types_in_DFF.htm)

##### Set Data Types in Delimited Flat Files


Each field in a record belongs to a data type. To select a data type for a field:

1. Open a delimited flat file guideline in SpecBuilder.
2. Click the Properties pane in the data pane. To display the Properties pane, on the View tab of the ribbon click Properties.
3. Click Type to open the drop-down menu of a field. The drop-down displays the options available, as displayed on the following image:


###### Type Property


The node Type property is a data type. The following data types are available for a delimited flat file (DFF).


| Type | Data | Possible value |
| --- | --- | --- |
| String | Alpha-numeric | Charset symbols. |
| Number | Denotes positive or negative numbers. Check for '0' at the beginning and at the end is performed. If a value is not valid according to the defined rules, then the verification against the charset is performed. | '+', '-', '0'...'9', <decimal point>, 'E', 'e'. For example, "7", "+7", "-7", "7.", "7.3", "7e2", "7e-2", "+765.43e-21". |
| Positive Integer | A number without a sign and without a decimal point. Check for '0' at the beginning is performed. | '0'...'9' |
| Positive Real | A number without a sign but with a decimal point. Check for '0' at the beginning and at the end is performed. | '0'...'9', <decimal point>. For example, "7", "7.3", ".3". |
| Date | A specific calendar date | If<Min Length> = 6 and <Max Length> = 6, then the date format is YYMMDD. If <Min Length> = 8 and <Max Length> = 8, then the date format is CCYYMMDD. If <Min Length> and <Max Length> have other values, validation cannot be performed. |
| Time | A time format | The HHMM or HHMMSS format. HH is two digits for hour of day, MM is two digits for minute of hour, SS is two digits for second of minute. |
| Binary | A binary data format | Validation is not performed. |
| Identifier | Identifier | If a field contains a code list, then validation against this code list is performed. If a field does not contain a code list, a value should include symbols that are allowed by the charset and have one of the following value: the white space character, '0'...'9', or an upper case letter. |



Check for '0' at the beginning is performed the following way. If the length of a value does not equal <Min Length>, and the value begins with '0' (plus and minus are ignored), and the option Actions.Settings.ValidateLeadingZeros=true in the XERegistry.htm, an error is thrown. In this case, the length is a number of digits in the value. A decimal point, plus, minus, and the exponent sign are ignored.


Check for '0' at the end is performed the following way. If a mantissa value ends with '0', and it is not the only symbol after the decimal point, and the option Actions.Settings.ValidateTrailingZeros=true in the XERegistry.htm, an error is thrown.

---

<a id="xml-schema-guidelines"></a>

### XML Schema Guidelines

> 📄 Source: [Book_XML_Schema_Guidelines.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_XML_Schema_Guidelines.htm)

#### XML Schema Guidelines


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="xml-schema-overview"></a>

#### XML Schema Overview

> 📄 Source: [XML_Schema_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/XML_Schema_Overview.htm)

##### XML Schema Overview


The World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) defines many of the standards that are used on the Web. One of these is the XML set of standards that specifies the format of documents to be exchanged between businesses on the Web by using Internet protocols. While the B2B transactions have traditionally used EDI and other proprietary document formats, more and more businesses are moving towards using the XML format for exchanging transactions. The W3C XML Schema specifications defines a language to describe an XML schema. An XML Schema is like an EDI guideline that specifies the structure and format of a class of document. For example, the schema stored in a file po.xsd defines the structure and format of any purchase order that is sent or received between businesses. Businesses have to agree to the XML Schema before starting the exchange of XML documents.


This section provides you with an overview of W3C XML Schema guidelines supported in SpecBuilder. It does not intend to describe the W3C XML Schema itself. That is described in great detail by W3C among other Web sites.


You may find the following references useful to get more information:

- XML 1.0 Specification: [http://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-20040204/](http://www.w3.org/TR/2004/REC-xml-20040204)
- Namespaces in XML: [http://www.w3.org/TR/xml-names11/](http://www.w3.org/TR/xml-names11)
- W3C XML Schema Primer: [http://www.w3.org/TR/xmlschema-0/](http://www.w3.org/TR/xmlschema-0)
- W3C XML Schema Specifications Part 1: Structures: [http://www.w3.org/TR/2001/REC-xmlschema-1-20010502/](http://www.w3.org/TR/2001/REC-xmlschema-1-20010502)
- W3C XML Schema Specifications Part 2: Datatypes: [http://www.w3.org/TR/2001/REC-xmlschema-2-20010502/](http://www.w3.org/TR/2001/REC-xmlschema-2-20010502)
- O'Reilly XML.com: [http://www.xml.com/schemas/](http://www.xml.com/)


SpecBuilder provides you with a tool to define the XML Schema guidelines.


There are two parts to the XML Schema guideline:

- Structure: Defines the element types that can occur in an XML instance document and their relationships.
- Format: Defines how the structure is represented in a file so it can be used to interpret and validate the XML instance document.


SpecBuilder provides you with two format options to save XML Schema guidelines:

- XSD file format: The standard XML text format as defined by W3C.
- .ecs file format: A binary format used internally for efficient editing and validation of the schema.


We recommend that you use the .ecs format for XML Schema guidelines when you are updating the guideline. You should always save it in the XSD file format when you want to move the XML Schema guideline to production systems that require XSD format. You can always review the XSD format in the Browser tab.


The XML Schema guideline supports the full W3C XML Schema specifications. However, the following limitation exists:

- XML comments (for example, <!-- my comment -->) are not fully supported. When you import an XSD file the XML comments are skipped and cannot be edited.


Instead, XML Schema specifications provide for an <annotation> as a child of most schema elements. This element provides for storage of human-readable text data or XML-formatted structured data to be associated for documentation purposes. Thus, this mechanism is more powerful and flexible for documentation purposes.

---

<a id="xml-schema-guideline-overview"></a>

#### XML Schema Guideline Overview

> 📄 Source: [XML_Schema_Guideline_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/XML_Schema_Guideline_Overview.htm)

##### XML Schema Guideline Overview


An XML Schema guideline is a document with a .ecs extension that can contain one or more schema structures. Each schema structure is a representation of an XML Schema document. With an XML Schema guideline, you can combine multiple schema files in a single document. You can also create individual documents that refer to each other, creating a reference tree. This may be more useful in the case of library-oriented XML Schema documents, where base schema, containing common types and definitions, are read-only and custom schema include the common information.


An XML Schema guideline supports external references to XML Schema files as well as XML Schema guidelines, providing a useful solution for many business tasks.


Note HL7 version 3 guidelines are open as XML Schema. Old HL7 version 3 guidelines are converted to XML Schema automatically.

---

<a id="open-an-xml-schema-guideline"></a>

#### Open an XML Schema Guideline

> 📄 Source: [Open_an_XML_Schema_Guideline.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Open_an_XML_Schema_Guideline.htm)

##### Open an XML Schema Guideline


XML Schema guidelines can be saved on the disk as .ecs files or the standard .xsd file. Opening an .ecs formatted XML Schema Guideline is similar to other file types supported by SpecBuilder.


Often you may need to open and edit an XML Schema file that is in .xsd format. You can directly open these files with SpecBuilder.


For efficient editing and schema validation, the .xsd file is converted to the internal (.ecs) format. Therefore, this is considered to be an import operation. You can save the file back to .xsd format after editing. You can also save the guideline in .ecs format until you are done with all the changes and export it to .xsd format when you roll it out into production.


To open (import) an existing .xsd file:

1. From the File menu, click Open.
2. Select the .xsd file to open, and then click OK.


When opening an .xsd file any non-standard attributes of the schema nodes are ignored when opening the .xsd file.


The .xsd file must be a well-formed XML Schema to open successfully. If the file cannot be opened, the file is opened in a text editor, where you can make necessary changes, and then reopen the file. Depending on the [XML Schema Global Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Set_Global_Options.htm) setting, you may see the following prompt:


*[Image: Error]*


If you choose No, in this dialog box the operation is canceled. If you choose Yes, the .xsd file is opened in the XSD File Editor as shown on the following image:


*[Image: XSD File Editor]*


You can use the commands on the toolbar to perform the following functions:

- Click Indent text (), to auto-indent the .xsd file content.
- Click Cut (), Copy (), or Paste () to change the .xsd file content.
- Click Undo () or Redo (), to undo or redo the last edit.
- Click Find (), to find text in the .xsd file.
- Click Replace, () to find and replace text in the .xsd file.
- Click Retry, to validate and re-open the corrected file, without saving changes to the original file.
- Click Save, to overwrite the .xsd file with the changes.
- Click Cancel, to cancel editing and also cancel the .xsd file open operation.

---

<a id="edit-an-xml-schema-guideline"></a>

#### Edit an XML Schema Guideline

> 📄 Source: [Edit_an_XML_Schema_Guideline.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Edit_an_XML_Schema_Guideline.htm)

##### Edit an XML Schema Guideline


You can perform the following operations when editing an XML Schema guideline:

- Insert a node
- Edit the properties of a node
- Move a node up or down within the parent's scope so as to change the order
- Delete a node


To insert a node:

1. Open the XML Schema guideline.
2. In the Guideline pane, right-click the node to where you want to add the new node. The following context menu is displayed.
3. Select Insert Node or Insert Child Node, and then click the type of node you want to insert.


To edit the properties of a node:

1. Open the XML Schema guideline.
2. Select the node to edit.
3. In the Details pane, click the Properties tab.
4. Edit the properties.


The XML Schema Editor assists you in the following ways when editing properties:

- Properties that are not applicable within the context of the node are grayed out by the Editor - for example, minOccurs, or maxOccurs for a global <element> node.
- Properties that are required are shown in red.
- Properties that refer to other nodes, such as the "type" of an <element>, the "base" property of <restriction>, or the "ref" property of an <element>, are provided as combo boxes with all available values included in the drop-down list. You can also add a new value to the list that you can define later in the guideline.


See [XML Schema Editing Assistance](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/XML_Schema_Editing_Assistance.htm) for more details.


To change the order of children of a node:

1. Open the XML Schema guideline.
2. Select the node to move.
3. Use the Ctrl+Up or Ctrl+Down key combinations to move the node up or down within the scope of its parent.


Note You may also use the Guideline pane toolbar buttons for moving the node up or down.


To delete a node:

1. Open the XML Schema guideline.
2. Select the node to delete.
3. Use the Delete key or right-click the node and choose Delete.

---

<a id="xml-schema-editing-assistance"></a>

#### XML Schema Editing Assistance

> 📄 Source: [XML_Schema_Editing_Assistance.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/XML_Schema_Editing_Assistance.htm)

##### XML Schema Editing Assistance


During editing, the [XML Schema Editor](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/XML_Schema_Properties_Pane_in_Technical_Perspective.htm) provides assistance to the user through a rules-based engine. This improves user productivity and reduces errors when editing an XML Schema guideline.


The following rules assist the user:

- Formatting and Syntax Rules Assistance: Unlike other products on the market, SpecBuilder provides a structured approach to defining the XML Schema that enables you to focus on the semantics of your schema. Therefore, the user does not have to worry about the syntax rules when writing an XML Schema. Saving or exporting the guideline to an .xsd format automatically writes the schema in the correct XML Schema syntax.
- Context Sensitive Parent-Child Relationships Rules Assistance: When adding a node to the XML Schema, the Editor provides only those options that are appropriate within the context of the current node, taking into account the parent-child relationship rules. This significantly reduces the errors typically encountered in XML Schema editing and improves the user's productivity. Note that this assistance is also provided during Cut/Copy/Paste operations ensuring that you do not paste an incorrect child node within a node.
- Changing namespace prefix: When you change the prefix of a namespace, the new prefix is automatically applied to the types used in the guideline from that namespace. For example, if you change the XMLSchema namespace prefix from xsd to xs, all occurrences of xsd:string are automatically replaced with xs:string throughout your guideline.
- Changing a type's name: A common scenario encountered by schema authors is the need to rename a user-defined type. When you change the name of a type (complexType or a simpleType), the XML Schema Editor searches for all occurrences of the type within the guideline and changes the referenced name accordingly. This works better than a Search/Replace in a text editor because the XML Schema Editor takes into account the defined structural relationships rather than simply matching textual patterns.
- Changing order of elements within a <sequence>: Often XML Schema authors refine their guideline by re-ordering the elements within data structures defined within a sequence. The XML Schema Editor provides a MoveUp/MoveDown editing paradigm to perform this action easily without causing fragmented moves that happen with text-editing approaches.

---

<a id="validate-an-xml-schema-guideline"></a>

#### Validate an XML Schema Guideline

> 📄 Source: [Validate_an_XML_Schema_Guideline.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Validate_an_XML_Schema_Guideline.htm)

##### Validate an XML Schema Guideline


The XML Schema guidelines you create must conform to the rules provided in the W3C XML Schema Specifications.


SpecBuilder provides assistance in conforming to formatting rules because the Save to XSD file operation automatically serializes your schema with correct XML Schema syntax. Validation operation also checks that the structure of the guideline nodes follows the W3C defined rules for XML Schemas.


To validate an XML Schema:

- On the Home tab, click Validate.


Any errors encountered during validation are displayed in the Guideline Errors tab. If no errors are encountered, a message box is displayed depending on the option settings (see [Set Global Options](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Set_Global_Options.htm)).


If errors are displayed, you can click them to be directed to the node which caused the error. You may correct the error and revalidate your guideline.


If the error message is long you can double-click the message to display it in an error message box.

---

<a id="add-external-references-to-xml-schema"></a>

#### Add External References to XML Schema

> 📄 Source: [Add_External_References_to_XML_Schema.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_External_References_to_XML_Schema.htm)

##### Add External References to XML Schema


References to external files from an XML Schema are established by using import, include, or redefine options. W3C XML Schema only allows XML Schema files to be mentioned as a part of these nodes’ declarations, but the SpecBuilder XML Schema guideline allows a high level of flexibility. Not only can W3C XML Schema files be referenced, but schema nodes within the same .ecs file and schema nodes in the external .ecs file can be referenced.


To add a reference:

1. Open an XML Schema guideline.
2. Right-click a schema node, and then click Insert Child.
3. Choose one of the following:
4. Click one of the following:
5. Click Use absolute path for schema location if you want an absolute rather than a relative path.
6. Click Next and do one of the following:


Currently, you can only select the schema node; however, all underlying content is shown to help in making the proper decision about what schema you are looking for.


When an XML Schema is chosen as a source, the node is created, establishing the proper reference.


Note The schemaLocation box contains either an absolute or relative path to the schema when referencing an external .ecs or .xsd file or an internal ID prefixed with a colon (:) when referencing a schema in the same guideline.

---

<a id="show-or-hide-element-nodes-in-business-perspective"></a>

#### Show or Hide Element Nodes in Business Perspective

> 📄 Source: [Show_or_Hide_Element_Nodes_in_Business_Perspective.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Show_or_Hide_Element_Nodes_in_Business_Perspective.htm)

##### Show or Hide Element Nodes in Business Perspective


When working with XML guidelines you may choose to display your guideline in the Business Perspective. It highlights the logical structure of the guideline and can be useful for documentation and review. In this view, it may not be useful to view all the root-level element nodes. Using this function, you can decide which root-level element nodes to display or hide.


To show or hide element nodes:

1. On the toolbar, click Show/Hide elements in Business Perspective (). Show/Hide Elements in Business Perspective dialog box is displayed.
2. Do one of the following:
3. Click OK to return to the guideline view.

2. The pattern and the code list options are
