<a id="xml-schema-reference-guide"></a>

#### XML Schema Reference Guide

> 📄 Source: [Book_XML_Schema_Reference_Guide.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_XML_Schema_Reference_Guide.htm)

##### XML Schema Reference Guide


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="xml-schema-quick-reference"></a>

##### XML Schema Quick Reference

> 📄 Source: [XML_Schema_Quick_Reference.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/XML_Schema_Quick_Reference.htm)

###### XML Schema Quick Reference


This section contains information about XML Schema guidelines and the node types used in SpecBuilder.


Descriptions of the W3C XML schemas in this reference were written by using information from "XML Schema" by Eric van der Vlist and the [*W3C website*](http://www.w3.org/).

---

<a id="all-node"></a>

##### all Node

> 📄 Source: [All_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/All_Node.htm)

###### all Node


An all node is used to describe an unordered group of elements. The number of occurrences can be zero or one. It needs to be used by itself and cannot be included in another compositor.

---

<a id="annotation"></a>

##### Annotation

> 📄 Source: [Annotation.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Annotation.htm)

###### Annotation


Annotation is a container for additional information for users (documentation) or for applications (appinfo). Annotation can be the first element in almost any other XML Schema item. It can also be included directly under schema.

---

<a id="anyattribute-node"></a>

##### anyAttribute Node

> 📄 Source: [AnyAttribute_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/AnyAttribute_Node.htm)

###### anyAttribute Node


An anyAttribute node is a wildcard that allows the insertion of any attribute belonging to a list of namespaces. This particle can be used wherever an "attribute" local declaration of reference can be used - for example, within "complexType" or "attributeGroup" definitions.


The list of permitted namespaces is specified by the "namespace" attribute. The "namespace" attribute expects a list of namespace URIs. In this list, two values have special meanings:

- "##targetNamespace" - target namespace.
- "##local" - local elements without namespaces.


These values can be used along with regular namespaces URIs.


The whole list may also be replaced by two other special values:

- ##any - any namespace; (the default value for the "namespace" attribute).
- ##other -any namespace other than the target namespace.


When ##other is used in a schema without a target namespace, all namespaces are allowed and only attributes without namespaces are prohibited.


The special values ##targetNamespace and ##other come from the target namespace (or lack of target namespace) of the schema where the "anyAttribute" wildcard is found. This also applies when one schema is imported into another.


The "processContent" attribute controls what attributes are allowed as shown later:

- When "processContent" is strict (the default value), the schema processors must validate these "attributes" against their schemas and report an error if they are not valid or if it cannot fetch the schemas for their namespaces.
- When "processContent" is skip, the processors do not attempt to validate these attributes.
- When "processContent" is lax, the validator validates the attributes if it can find a definition for them, and skips them if it cannot.
- When "processContent" is skip (or when it is lax and the schema processor is not able to locate a schema for an attribute), the processor skips any further validation of the accepted attributes that have any datatype.
- When "processContent" is strict (or when it is lax and the schema validator found a schema), the attributes that are accepted for the wildcard need to be defined as global attributes in the schema for this namespace.

---

<a id="any-node"></a>

##### any Node

> 📄 Source: [Any_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Any_Node.htm)

###### any Node


An any node is a wildcard used to insert any element that belongs to a list of namespaces. This particle can be used within choices and "sequences". The number of occurrences is controlled by the "minOccurs" and "maxOccurs" attributes.


The list of permitted namespaces is specified by the "namespace" attribute. The "namespace" attribute expects a list of namespace URIs. In this list, two values have special meanings:

- "##targetNamespace" - target namespace.
- "##local" - local elements (without namespaces).


These values can be used along with regular namespaces URIs.


The whole list may also be replaced by two other special values:

- ##any - any namespace; (the default value for the "namespace" attribute).
- ##other -any namespace other than the target namespace.


When ##other is used in a schema without a target namespace, all namespaces are allowed and only elements without namespaces are prohibited.


The special values ##targetNamespace and ##other come from the target namespace (or lack of target namespace) of the schema where the "any" wildcard is found. This also applies when one schema is imported into another. The "processContent" attribute controls what elements are allowed as shown later:

- When "processContent" is strict (the default value), the schema processors must validate these "elements" against their schemas and report an error if they are not valid or if it cannot fetch the schemas for their namespaces.
- When "processContent" is skip, the processors do not attempt to validate these elements.
- When "processContent" is lax, the validator validates the elements if it can find a definition for them, and skips them if it cannot.
- When "processContent" is skip (or when it is lax and the schema processor is not able to locate a schema for an element), the processor skips any further validation of the accepted elements that may have any content type. The processor includes attributes and sub elements of any namespace regardless of the value of the "namespace" attribute.
- When "processContent" is strict (or when it is lax and the schema validator found a schema), the elements that are accepted for the wildcard need to be defined as global elements in the schema for this namespace or have a valid datatype associated in the instance document through the "xsi:type" attribute.

---

<a id="appinfo"></a>

##### appinfo

> 📄 Source: [Appinfo.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Appinfo.htm)

###### appinfo


An appinfo is a container for structured information that can be used by applications. It can accept any element from any namespace with a lax validation; however, W3C XML Schema elements must be valid. "appinfo" can include any kind of information - for example, metadata, processing directives, and code snippets.


Its content is similar to "documentation" except for an xml:lang attribute that is not allowed in "appinfo".

---

<a id="attributegroup-node"></a>

##### attributeGroup Node

> 📄 Source: [AttributeGroup_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/AttributeGroup_Node.htm)

###### attributeGroup Node


An attributeGroup node is a global container that combines attributes that are often used together. It also provides global access to attributes that cannot be defined globally because they are unqualified or because several definitions exist in a single schema.


Attribute references or local definitions, attribute wildcards, and attributeGroup references may be included within attributeGroup definitions.


An attributeGroup can be redefined through "redefine" elements.


This node type can be used as a reference. It can be included in the following:

- attributeGroup (global definition)
- complexType (local definition)
- complexType (global definition)
- extension (complex content)
- extension (simple content)
- restriction (complex content)
- restriction (simple content)

---

<a id="attributegroup-reference"></a>

##### attributeGroup Reference

> 📄 Source: [AttributeGroup_Reference.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/AttributeGroup_Reference.htm)

###### attributeGroup Reference


An attribute reference or local definition, attribute wildcards, and attributeGroup references may be included within attributeGroup definitions.


For a description of the attributeGroup node, see [attributeGroup Node](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/AttributeGroup_Node.htm).

---

<a id="attribute-node"></a>

##### attribute Node

> 📄 Source: [Attribute_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Attribute_Node.htm)

###### attribute Node


All the attributes defined at the top level of a schema (included directly under the "schema" document item) are considered globally defined.


Globally-defined attributes have a global scope: they can be referenced through their qualified name everywhere in the "schema" in which they are defined as well as in any schema that imports or includes this schema.


An attribute is identified by its qualified name, but the local name is expected in the name attribute.


The definition is done by assigning a simple datatype to the item. This assignment can be done either by reference, by using the type attribute to refer to a simple or complex datatype by its qualified name, or inline, by embedding the definition of the simple ("simpleType") item or complex ("complexType") item within the "element" item.


Local definition of an attribute is also possible. This item serves two different purposes and has two different content models for these two purposes: it can either be a reference to a globally defined "attribute" or it can be a local "attribute" definition. These options are mutually exclusive.


When used as a local definition, the definition is done by assigning a simple datatype to the attribute. This assignment can be done either by reference, by using the type attribute to refer to a simple datatype by its qualified name, or inline, by embedding the definition of the simpleType ("simpleType" item) within the "attribute" item.

---

<a id="attribute-reference"></a>

##### attribute Reference

> 📄 Source: [Attribute_Reference.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Attribute_Reference.htm)

###### attribute reference


When used as a reference to an attribute, the "ref" attribute must contain the qualified name of the attribute (with its namespace prefix).


For a description of the attribute node, see [attribute Node](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Attribute_Node.htm).

---

<a id="choice-node"></a>

##### choice Node

> 📄 Source: [Choice_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Choice_Node.htm)

###### choice Node


The choice node is a compositor that declares a group of items, only one of which can be used in the document. The "minOccurs" and "maxOccurs" attributes of "choice" control the number of occurrences of "choice" and the "minOccurs" and "maxOccurs" attributes of each particle control the number of occurrences of the particle.


You can use "choice" to define a content model where an unlimited number of elements can be found in any order, avoiding the limitations of "all". Substitution groups can be used when the particle used in "choice" is an "element".

---

<a id="complexcontent"></a>

##### complexContent

> 📄 Source: [ComplexContent.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/ComplexContent.htm)

###### complexContent


This node allows you to define extensions or restrictions for another "complexType". Whether it represents a restriction or an extension is controlled by a child element of "restriction" or "extension".


A complexContent node can define whether the content model is mixed or not, but this can also be defined in the "complexType" parent element.


The derivation method and the base type determine the mixed characteristic. If the base type is mixed, it can be set to false (restricted to "elements" only); otherwise the mixed nature of the base type cannot be changed.

---

<a id="complextype-node"></a>

##### complexType Node

> 📄 Source: [ComplexType_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/ComplexType_Node.htm)

###### complexType Node


A complexType node is used to create global and local complexTypes by describing their content model or they can be derived from simpleTypes or other complexTypes.


A new complexType is created by a compositor ("sequence", "choice", or "all") describing the child elements, followed by a list of attributes (or attribute groups or attribute wildcards).


To derive a complexType from existing simple or complexTypes, include a "simpleContent" (for simple contents) or a "complexContent" (for complex contents) item. Use the "mixed" attribute to define whether the content model allows both text and element nodes(true) or not (false).


Other attributes that can be declared are:

- abstract - Specifies whether the complexType can be used in a document instance.
- block - Prevents a specific derivation type of complexType from being substituted for this complexType.
- final - Prevents a certain type of derivation of this complexType element.


A complexType describes all the constraints on the "element", character, and "attribute" nodes that can be included within an item except those described by "unique", "key", and "keyref", which are defined directly under the "element" definitions.

---

<a id="documentation"></a>

##### Documentation

> 📄 Source: [Documentation.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Documentation.htm)

###### Documentation


Documentation is a container for human-readable documentation in plain text or structured formats. It can accept any element from any namespace with a lax; however, W3C XML Schema elements must be valid. "Documentation" can include any kind of information.


Its content is similar to "appinfo", except that it has the "xml:lang" attribute that is not allowed in "appinfo".

---

<a id="element-node"></a>

##### element Node

> 📄 Source: [Element_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Element_Node.htm)

###### element Node


All the elements defined at the top level of a schema – for example, an "element" that is included directly under the "schema" document item - are considered globally defined.


Globally defined elements can be referenced using their qualified name everywhere in the schema in which they are defined, as well as in any schema that imports or includes this schema. They can also be used as document elements in instance documents.


An element is identified by its qualified name, but the local name is expected in the name attribute. The type definition is performed by assigning a simple or complex datatype to the element. This assignment can be done either by reference (by using the type attribute to refer to a simple or complex datatype by its qualified name) or inline, by embedding the definition of the simple ("simpleType") item or complex ("complexType") item within the "element" item.


A default value can be defined by using the default attribute.


Note Creating default values for elements is different from creating default values for attributes. An element is not created if it doesn’t exist in the instance document. The default value is applied only if the element is present and empty.


Several control attributes are included in element definitions (abstract, blocked, final, fixed, and nillable).


Substitution groups are defined by the substitutionGroup attribute that refers to the head of the substitution group. Any global element that is not final can be chosen as the head of a substitution group. Any reference to the head of a substitution group can be replaced in the instance documents by any of the members of the substitution group (assuming these substitutions have not been blocked through block attributes). The relation is transitive, and if the head of a substitution "A" is a member of another substitution group "B", the members of "A" are also considered members of "B".


When used as a local element, definition is done as detailed above for global elements.

---

<a id="element-reference"></a>

##### element Reference

> 📄 Source: [Element_Reference.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Element_Reference.htm)

###### element Reference


An element can be a reference either to a globally-defined element or to a local element definition. These options are mutually exclusive.


When used as a reference to an element, the "ref" attribute must contain the qualified name of the element (with its namespace prefix). 


For a description of the element node, see element Node.

---

<a id="extension-node"></a>

##### extension Node

> 📄 Source: [Extension_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Extension_Node.htm)

###### extension Node


An extension is used to extend the definition of a "simpleType" or a simple content "complexType" into a simple content "complexType". The type to extend is specified in the "base" attribute, and the list of the attributes to be added to the content model can be attributes, attribute Groups, or an "anyAttribute" included in the "extension" element.


The only way to create a "simple content" "complexType" content model is to extend a "simpleType".


The extension method can be applied to another "complex content" "complexType" base type, allowing the addition of elements and attributes to this base type. The additional elements are added after the compositor used in the base type.


When new "elements" are added by using an "extension", the resulting content model is equivalent to creating a "sequence" that contains the compositor used to define the base type and the one included in the "extension" element.

---

<a id="field-node"></a>

##### field Node

> 📄 Source: [Field_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Field_Node.htm)

###### field Node


Note The SpecBuilder navigation has changed and a ribbon now provides better workflow. Please see Ribbon Overview for more information about the ribbon and the resulting improvements in SpecBuilder.


A field defines the location for attributes or elements that are checked for uniqueness or a reference.


Fields are identified by relative XPath expressions checked against nodes selected by the "selector" element.


Concatenated keys can define multiple fields under a "unique", "key", or "keyref" element.

---

<a id="group-node"></a>

##### Group Node

> 📄 Source: [Group_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Group_Node.htm)

###### Group Node


Groups of elements can be globally defined and used as containers that can be referenced elsewhere in the current schema or in other schemas. These containers are very flexible and can be used as building blocks for the definition of local or global complexTypes.


As global components, they can also encapsulate "element" definitions that cannot be made global, such as unqualified elements in a schema with target namespaces, multiple elements with the same name in a schema, elements that should not be used as document elements, or elements that should be redefined through "redefine" in other "schemas".


Element groups can be redefined through "redefine". The redefinition can be either a "restriction" (similar to a complexType derivation by restriction) or an "extension". This is more flexible than a complexType extension since the location where the base group is included in the new group can be chosen, while the new elements are always located after the base type during a derivation by extension.


The three compositors -"all", "sequence", or "choice" - can be used to create an element group.


When a group is being redefined (through "redefine"), a self reference results in the original content of the group being included at this location.

---

<a id="group-reference"></a>

##### Group Reference

> 📄 Source: [Group_Reference.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Group_Reference.htm)

###### Group Reference


A "group" can be used in a local context as a reference to an element group. The effect is a logical replacement of the group reference by the group's content.


For a description of the group node, see group Node.

---

<a id="import-node"></a>

##### import Node

> 📄 Source: [Import_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Import_Node.htm)

###### import Node


An import node allows you to add schemas located in namespaces other than the target namespace of the current schema to a document.


All the global definitions (elements, attributes, element and attribute groups, simple and complexTypes) and "unique" and "key" constraints of the imported schemas can be referenced by using a namespace prefix defined for the corresponding namespace URI.


An import can also be used to import components with no target namespaces into schemas with target namespaces.

---

<a id="include-node"></a>

##### include Node

> 📄 Source: [Include_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Include_Node.htm)

###### include Node


An include allows you to include a schema within another schema with the same target namespace. It is also possible to include a schema that doesn't specify a target namespace. The included schema is treated as adopting the target namespace of the schema in which it is included. This is called "chameleon design", allowing the reuse of "transparent" schemas in the context of different namespaces.


The effect of a schema inclusion is a merge between the two schemas that are consolidated into a global schema. The included schema does not need to be a complete schema.

---

<a id="keyref-node"></a>

##### keyref Node

> 📄 Source: [Keyref_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Keyref_Node.htm)

###### keyref Node


A keyref specifies a reference to a simple key, compound key, or a unique constraint.


The syntax of "keyref" is very similar to the syntax of "key" or "unique". The only difference is the "refer" attribute that must contain the name of the referred "key" or "unique" constraint.


The root element for the "keyref" is the element where the "key" is defined. The "keyref" is often defined with the same root element as its referred "key" or "unique" constraint.

---

<a id="key-node"></a>

##### key Node

> 📄 Source: [Key_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Key_Node.htm)

###### key Node


A key is used to identify certain attributes or element values as simple or compound keys. A key is very similar to "unique". Like the constraints defined with "unique", keys are unique in the scope of their root element and may be referenced by "keyref". The difference between "key" and "unique" is that keys must be defined for each of the elements in the selection list, while unique identifiers may be undefined for elements in the selection list.


The root element for the key is the element where the key is defined. The location of the root element must be chosen carefully since the unity of the key is checked only within the node elements that are the key's children. Defining a root element that has multiple occurrences within a document leaves the possibility of defining local keys that are unique only in the scope of each occurrence of a document. When a key is global to a document, you should define it by using the document element as a root to avoid potential problems.

---

<a id="list-node"></a>

##### list Node

> 📄 Source: [List_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/List_Node.htm)

###### List Node


A list provides a derivation process that transforms a simple datatype (named the item type) into a whitespace-separated list of values from this datatype.


The item type can be defined inline by adding "simpleType" as a child "element" of the list "element", or by reference, by using the "itemType" attribute. Using both causes an error.


The semantic and list of facets for the item type are lost and the new datatype inherits a list of facets.


After derivation by list, the type of the resulting datatype is a list of whatever the item type was ("atomic" or union).


It is possible to define lists of atomic datatypes that allow whitespaces such as xsd:string. In this case, the whitespaces are considered to be separators.

---

<a id="notation-node"></a>

##### notation Node

> 📄 Source: [Notation_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Notation_Node.htm)

###### notation Node


A notation node is used to declare a notation just like the NOTATION declarations in DTDs, with one main difference: W3C XML Schema notations are namespace-aware and can be imported between schemas. When these declarations are used, the notations are used in enumeration facets to create a simpleType.

---

<a id="redefine-node"></a>

##### redefine Node

> 📄 Source: [Redefine_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Redefine_Node.htm)

###### Redefine Node


A redefine node includes a schema into another schema with the same target namespace. It is similar to "include" with one exception: the definitions of "simpleType", "complexType", "attribute", and "group" can be changed. Changes to these components must create components that are valid "restrictions" or "extensions" of the original ones.


A "simpleType" and "complexType" are redefined by using the usual mechanism of derivation: the new types must be defined as derivations by "restriction" or "extension" of their previous definition, which is considered to be the base type.


An "element" and "attributeGroup" are redefined by adding a new definition, and may refer to themselves, indicating that the content of their original definition must be included. In this case, this self-reference is not considered a recursive definition (forbidden in "element" and "attribute groups"), but an extension of the original group.


When "redefine" is used and no redefinitions are specified, its effect is the same as "include".

---

<a id="restriction-node"></a>

##### restriction Node

> 📄 Source: [Restriction_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Restriction_Node.htm)

###### Restriction Node


A restriction is an operation that defines a new datatype by adding constraints (called facets) on a base datatype to restrict its lexical and value spaces. It can be used for "simpleType", "complexContent", or "simpleContent".


The base datatype can be defined by reference by using the "base" attribute or inline, by using a "simpleType" element defining a local datatype.


Each facet has its own meaning and, depending on the facet, acts on the lexical space, the value space, or the whitespace transformation performed between the lexical and value spaces. Derivations by restriction can be applied to any type of datatype (atomic, list, or union), and don't change the type of the datatype.


The restriction of simple content "complexType" is an operation that allows the addition of new constraints to both the attributes and the text node allowed in "simpleContent" elements. The restriction of the text node is done by using the same facets that are available for restricting a "simpleType"; the restriction of the attributes is done by providing a list of modified "attribute" definitions or references. The "attribute" types used in this list must be identical or explicit derivations by restriction of the types used during the definition of the base type. The controls applied to the "attributes" must result in a restriction of their definition (for instance, a value may be fixed, an "attribute" that was optional may become either required or prohibited, and so on).

---

<a id="schema-node"></a>

##### schema Node

> 📄 Source: [Schema_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Schema_Node.htm)

###### schema Node


The schema node is the document (root) element of any W3C XML Schema. It is a container for declarations and definitions and a place holder for any default values expressed as attributes. The compositors embedded directly under "xs:schema" are considered global items and can be accessed elsewhere.


A schema node can contain the following children nodes:

- Type definitions - Definitions for all the "simpleType" and "complexType" items, if any, plus any included or imported definitions.
- Attribute declarations - Top-level declarations for all the "attribute" items, if any.
- Element declarations - Top-level declarations for all the "element" items, if any.
- Attribute Group definitions - Definitions for all the "attributeGroup" items, if any.
- Model Group definitions - Definitions for all the "group" items, if any.
- Notation declarations - Declarations for all the "notation" items, if any.
- Identity-constraint definitions - Definitions corresponding to all the "key", "keyref" and "unique" items, if any.
- External references - Nodes corresponding to all the "import", "include", "redefine" items, if any.

---

<a id="selector-node"></a>

##### selector Node

> 📄 Source: [Selector_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Selector_Node.htm)

###### selector Node


A selector is identified by a relative XPath expression and defines an element on which a uniqueness constraint or reference is checked (it cannot be an attribute, since attributes are unique per XML 1.0). The constraint is checked while looping on the XPath expression.

---

<a id="sequence-node"></a>

##### Sequence Node

> 📄 Source: [Sequence_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Sequence_Node.htm)

###### Sequence Node


A sequence is used to define an ordered list or set of elements. The "minOccurs" and "maxOccurs" attributes define the number of occurrences for the whole sequence.

---

<a id="simplecontent-element"></a>

##### simpleContent Element

> 📄 Source: [SimpleContent.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/SimpleContent.htm)

###### simpleContent Element


This node allows the definition of a "simple content" model by derivation of a "simpleType" or of another simpleContent "complexType". It is not a component by itself ("simple contents" are not named) but rather a declaration of the intention to define a simpleContent model by derivation.


The derivation method is defined by the child element of the "simpleContent" element - "restriction" for a derivation by restriction, or "extension" for a derivation by extension.

---

<a id="simpletype-node"></a>

##### simpleType Node

> 📄 Source: [SimpleType_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/SimpleType_Node.htm)

###### simpleType Node


A simpleType can be either global or local. Global user-defined simple datatypes are defined directly under the "schema" node or redefined by using a "redefine" outside the scope of a specific content model. They can be referenced through their qualified name in the same schema or by any other schema that has included or imported this schema.


Like local simple datatypes, global simple datatypes are defined by derivation from other simple datatypes (either predefined by W3C XML Schema or other user-defined datatypes) by using one of the three derivation methods available for simpleTypes:

- restriction - adds new constraints to a datatype
- list - defines lists of values
- union - performs the union of the lexical spaces of several datatypes


The "name" attribute is unqualified since the datatype is considered to belong to the target namespace of the schema in which it is defined. During a reference, the prefix defined for this namespace needs to be added.


Although simple datatypes are considered to belong to the target namespace of schema in which they are defined, they can be referenced to create new datatypes, attributes, or elements for any namespace.


Further derivations (by list, union, restriction, or all) can be blocked by using the final attribute.


Local simple datatypes are defined directly where needed (during "element", "attribute", or other "simpleType" definitions) for local anonymous usage, and cannot be referenced as such in other places of a schema.

---

<a id="union-node"></a>

##### Union Node

> 📄 Source: [Union_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Union_Node.htm)

###### Union Node


Union is an operation that merges the lexical spaces of several simple datatypes (called member types) to create a new simple datatype. The resulting datatype has a type of union.


The member types can be defined either by reference (through the "memberTypes" attribute) or embedded as simple datatype local definitions in the "union" element. Both styles can be mixed.


The semantic of the member datatypes is lost with regard to validation. The only facets that can be further applied to the resulting datatype are pattern and enumeration.

---

<a id="unique-node"></a>

##### Unique Node

> 📄 Source: [Unique_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Unique_Node.htm)

###### Unique Node


Unique is used to define simple or compound uniqueness constraints that unambiguously identify each element in which they are present from a selected list of sub-elements within the scope of a root "element".


Unique is very similar to ["key"](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Key_Node.htm). Like the constraints defined with "key", constraints defined with "unique" are unique in the scope of their root element and may be referenced by ["keyref"](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Keyref_Node.htm). The only difference between "unique" and "key" is that "unique" identifiers can be undefined in any of the elements in the selection list, while "key" identifiers must be defined for all the elements in the selection list.


The root element for the key is the element where the key is defined. The location of the root element must be chosen carefully since the unity of the key is checked only within the node elements that are the key's children. Defining a root element that has multiple occurrences within a document leaves the possibility of defining local keys that are unique only in the scope of each occurrence of a document. When a key is global to a document, you should define it by using the document element as a root to avoid potential problems.

---

<a id="rosettanet-messages"></a>

### RosettaNet Messages

> 📄 Source: [Book_RosettaNet_Messages.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_RosettaNet_Messages.htm)

#### RosettaNet Messages


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="rosettanet-messages-overview"></a>

#### RosettaNet Messages Overview

> 📄 Source: [RosettaNet_Messages_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/RosettaNet_Messages_Overview.htm)

##### RosettaNet Messages Overview


RosettaNet is a non-profit consortium aimed at establishing standard processes for the sharing of business information (B2B). RosettaNet is a consortium of major computer and consumer electronics, electronic components, semiconductor manufacturing, telecommunications, and logistics companies working to create and implement industry-wide open e-business process standards. These standards form a common e-business language, aligning processes between supply chain partners on a global basis.


The RosettaNet standard is based on XML and defines message guidelines, business processes interface, and implementation frameworks for interactions between companies. The focus is to supply chain area, but manufacturing, product and material data, and service processes are also in scope.


RosettaNet Messages consist of the following core components:

- Headers:
- Preamble Header
- Delivery Header
- Service Header
- Payloads

---

<a id="hl7-guidelines"></a>

### HL7 Guidelines

> 📄 Source: [Book_HL7_Guidelines.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_HL7_Guidelines.htm)

#### HL7 Guidelines


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="hl7-overview"></a>

#### HL7 Overview

> 📄 Source: [HL7_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/HL7_Overview.htm)

##### HL7 Overview


Health Level Seven (HL7) is one of several American National Standards Institute (ANSI)-accredited Standards Developing Organizations (SDOs) operating in the healthcare arena. Most SDOs produce standards (sometimes called specifications or protocols) for a particular healthcare domain such as pharmacy, medical devices, imaging or insurance (claims processing) transactions. The domain of HL7 is clinical and administrative data.


For more information about HL7 standards, visit [www.hl7.org](http://www.hl7.org/).

---

<a id="hl7-version-3-overview"></a>

#### HL7 Version 3 Overview

> 📄 Source: [HL7v3_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/HL7v3_Overview.htm)

##### HL7 Version 3 Overview


HL7 version 3 is a messaging standard for health care applications to exchange key sets of clinical and administrative data. Even though it is called HL7, this version is totally different from the previous version of HL7 in format and data models. Version 3 is not compatible with previous versions of the standard.


HL7 version 3 is an attempt to improve on previous versions. Version 3 provides significantly more flexibility that earlier versions. A new data model is used for this version (RIM). This provides a method for expressing the semantic and lexical relationships between HL7 messages.  


For more information about HL7 standards, visit [www.hl7.org](http://www.hl7.org/).


The HL7 version 3 guideline is converted to the XML schema guideline while opening. You are able to work with the ordinary XML schema guideline which has the additional HL7 version 3 section in the Properties pane.


Note All fields in the HL7 version 3 section in the Properties pane are read-only.


For more information about XML Schema guidelines, see [XML Schema Guideline Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/XML_Schema_Guideline_Overview.htm).

---

<a id="hl7-select-data-type"></a>

#### HL7 Select Data Type

> 📄 Source: [HL7_Select_Data_Type.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/HL7_Select_Data_Type.htm)

##### HL7 Select Data Type


When working with HL7 guidelines you may want to change the data type for a field node in the guideline. Use the Select Data Type dialog box for this purpose.


To launch the Select Data Type dialog box:

- On the [Guideline pane](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/HL7_Guideline_View_in_Technical_Perspective.htm), click a field node you want to edit.
- Click Select Data Type () next to the Data Type box.


Note Information about data types is available in the read-only mode.


You can view data type definition by clicking View data type definition next to the Data Type field on the [Properties Pane](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Properties_Pane_Overview.htm).

---

<a id="json-guidelines"></a>

### JSON Guidelines

> 📄 Source: [Book_JSON_Guidelines.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_JSON_Guidelines.htm)

#### JSON Guidelines


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="json-overview"></a>

#### JSON Overview

> 📄 Source: [JSON_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/JSON_Overview.htm)

##### JSON Overview


JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data-interchange format. It is a human-readable collection of data that can be accessed in a logical manner. JSON is language independent; it uses JavaScript syntax, but like XML, the JSON format is text only. Any programming language can read the text and use it as a data format. The JSON file name extension is .json.


There are two structures that JSON is built on:

- A set of name/value pairs. This is realized as an object, record, struct, dictionary, hash table, keyed list, or an associative array.
- An ordered set of values. This is realized as an array, vector, list, or sequence.


These data structures are universal and almost all modern programming languages support them. It is convenient if a data format that is interchangeable with programming languages also be based on these structures.

---

<a id="json-guideline-overview"></a>

#### JSON Guideline Overview

> 📄 Source: [JSON_Guideline_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/JSON_Guideline_Overview.htm)

##### JSON Guideline Overview


The JSON guideline was developed by Edifecs as the proprietary format that defines the structure of JSON data similarly to a JSON schema. The JSON guideline describes the structure that is built when processing JSON data files. The following is an example of the JSON guideline:



A JSON guideline can have an object or a simple type as a child node. An object can be defined by simple types or by other objects. An object can have a property or/and extension as a child. If a property type is not specified, any type is considered valid and the node is skipped during validation. The property type can also be an object.


Note By default, extra properties that are not listed in the properties themselves would be considered invalid. If you expect any extra properties in data, select the allow additional properties check box in the Properties pane of the object selected in the guideline.


The following property settings are available in the Properties pane:

- Name: Displays the name of the property.
- Title: Displays your custom title of the property.
- Description: Describes the function of the property.
- Type: Determines the property type. The available types are described below.
- Use: Determines if the property is optional, prohibited, or required. By default, the value is optional.
- Nillable: Determines if the property can take null value. By default, the value is false.


The following simple types are available:


| Node | Description | Property | Property Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| any | Any value is considered to be valid. | Title | Use this field to enter your custom title for the node, if required. |
| Description | Use this field to describe the function of the node, if required. | | |
| Note The Title and Description fields are applicable for all nodes. These fields are not displayed if the property or definition parent node already has them. | | | |
| string | A simple type string. You can restrict the string type by entering format and/or pattern used for regular expressions in the Properties pane. | Fixed | Determines a fixed valid value. |
| Format | Select a particular format from the drop-down list to restrict the string value to the specific format. The following formats are available: date, date-time, email, hostname, ipv4, ipv6, time, uri. | | |
| Min length | Determines the minimum and/or the maximum possible length of the string value. | | |
| Max length | | | |
| Pattern | Determines the pattern used for regular expressions to restrict the string value. | | |
|  number |   A simple type number. | | |
| Fixed | Determines a fixed valid value. For example, if 45 is specified, 45 is the only possible value. | | |
| Multiple of | Determines the multiplicity. For example, if 3 is specified, the possible data values are 3,6,9, and so on. 8 is an invalid value since 8 is not a multiple of 3. | | |
| Minimum | Determines the minimum possible value. | | |
| Exclusive minimum | If true, the minimum value is excluded. For example, if the minimum is 0 and the exclusive minimum is true, the value 0 is excluded or considered invalid. By default, the exclusive minimum is false. | | |
| Maximum | Determines the maximum possible value. | | |
| Exclusive maximum | If true, the maximum value is excluded. For example, if the maximum is 100, 100 cannot be used, while any value less than 100 is valid. By default, the exclusive maximum is false. | | |
| integer | A simple type integer. Includes whole numbers and their negative counterparts. | Has the same properties as number. | |
| boolean | A simple type boolean. | Fixed | Determines a fixed valid value. |
|  enum | An enumerator that restricts the value to a fixed set of values. For example, [“monday”, “wednesday”, “friday”]. | No additional fields. | |
|  null |   Null. | No additional fields. | |
| object | AdditionalProperties (Boolean) Json object. Object is defined by simple types and/or other objects. | Allow additional properties | If enabled, any additional properties are valid. |
|  array | The JSON array. An element of the array can be of any type including array, object, and so on. For example, [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] or [1, “two” , {“key”:”value”}]. | | |
| Min items | Restrict an array by entering the minimum and/or the maximum number of elements. | | |
| Max items | | | |
| Unique items only | If enabled, indicates that array elements must be unique. For example, [1,1,2] fails validation since 1 is a repeated value. | | |
|  choice | The selection of multiple possible values where only one can match. | No additional fields. | |
|  {definitionName} | The [definitions](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Work_with_Definitions_and_Extensions.htm) that you can create to reuse complex recurrent structures. | No additional fields. | |



You can define a property as the definition, which can be an object that defines a complex structure with multiple objects, simple types, and so on. Add a definition as a child node of a JSON guideline to be able to create definition.


You can extend objects with other properties by using extensions. For example, if you use a definition for a particular node property but need to extend an object used in definition with other properties. You can also create extended definitions. For more information on how to use definitions and extensions, see [Work with Definitions and Extensions](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Work_with_Definitions_and_Extensions.htm).


By default, the JSON guideline is displayed in a business perspective. To switch the perspective, on the Home tab of the ribbon, in Perspective, click Business Perspective to enable or disable the business view. In the business perspective, the guideline tree view displays the actual guideline structure and only displays the property names. It resolves definitions and extensions as part of the structure and does not display them as separate units.


The following is an example of a JSON guideline in the business perspective:



Note that when the business perspective is enabled, the JSON guideline diagram is displayed in the business perspective too. For more information, see [JSON Guideline Diagram](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/JSON_Guideline_Diagram.htm).

---

<a id="json-guideline-diagram"></a>

#### JSON Guideline Diagram

> 📄 Source: [JSON_Guideline_Diagram.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/JSON_Guideline_Diagram.htm)

##### JSON Guideline Diagram


The JSON guideline diagram is displayed in the Diagram pane. To view the diagram, select the Diagram check box in the View tab of the ribbon. The following is an example of the JSON guideline diagram:


*[Image: JSON Guideline Diagram]*


The JSON guideline diagram displays the structure of the JSON guideline and definitions. Definitions used in the guideline are highlighted with yellow. By using the diagram, you can edit a guideline and do the following:

- add children and siblings to the guideline nodes
- include and exclude guideline nodes
- copy, paste, delete, and cut guideline nodes
- move guideline nodes up and down.


You can view the JSON guideline diagram in a business perspective. For this, on the Home tab of the ribbon, in Perspective, click Business Perspective. In the business perspective, the diagram displays the actual guideline structure and only displays the property names. The same way as for a guideline in the business perspective, it resolves definitions and extensions as part of the structure and does not display them as separate units. The following is an example of the JSON guideline diagram in the business perspective:


*[Image: JSON diagram in business perspective]*

---

<a id="work-with-definitions-and-extensions"></a>

#### Work with Definitions and Extensions

> 📄 Source: [Work_with_Definitions_and_Extensions.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Work_with_Definitions_and_Extensions.htm)

##### Work with Definitions and Extensions


With definitions, you can create complex recurrent structures once and reuse them in the JSON guideline. The definition can be an object that defines a structure with multiple objects, simple types, and so on.


To create a definition:

1. In the Guideline tree view, right-click JSON Guideline, click Insert Child, and then click definitions.
2. In the Guideline tree view, right-click definitions, click Insert Child, and then click definition.
3. In the Properties pane, enter a name for a definition, its title and description.
4. In the Guideline tree view, add a structure you want to reuse by adding children to the newly created definition node.


In the following example, the definition is created for a mailing address:


*[Image: JSON Guideline Sample Definition]*


To reuse the created definition, provide its name in the Properties pane of the property. In the following example, the Address definition is used for the ProviderAddress property.


*[Image: JSON Guideline Sample]*


With extensions, you can add new properties to an object.


To create an extension:

1. In the Guideline tree view, right-click a property or definition you want to add an extension to, click Insert Child, and then click object.
2. In the Guideline tree view, right-click object, click Insert Child, and then click extension.
3. In the Properties pane, in definition, select the definition you want to extend from the drop-down list.
4. In the Properties pane, in description, enter a name for an extension.
5. In the Guideline tree view, create properties you want to add as siblings to the newly created extension node.


In the following example, extensions are added in the property PatientAddress and definition Contacts.


*[Image: Sample JSON Guideline]*

---

<a id="work-with-json-editor"></a>

#### Work with JSON Editor

> 📄 Source: [Work_with_JSON_Editor.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Work_with_JSON_Editor.htm)

##### Work with JSON Editor


On the Analyzer/Data tab, you can view the structure of your [JSON file](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/JSON_Overview.htm) and edit its contents in a user-friendly interface, without having to make changes to the code. After you edit your JSON document, you can [validate](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Analyzer_Wizard_Overview.htm) its structure against a guideline to make sure that it complies with the guideline and does not contain errors.


To edit a JSON file:

1. Open a [JSON guideline](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/JSON_Guideline_Overview.htm) or [create a new](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_a_New_Guideline_Document.htm) one.
2. Go to the Analyzer/Data tab, and then follow the [Data Wizard](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_New_Data_Files.htm) steps to upload a JSON file.
3. To edit the elements on the tree, right-click an element, and then select the following options: | Option | Description | | --- | --- | | Type | Select the type for the element. It can be an Array, an Object, or a String. By default, the type is set to Auto and defined automatically when you enter a value. | | Append Child | If the selected element is an object or an array, you can add a child element to it and select its type. | | Insert Before/After | Select the type for a sibling element that you plan to insert before/after the selected element. | | Edit | Select to edit a value for the element. | | Go to Next/Prev Occurrence | Select to jump to the next/previous element of the same type. | | Undo/Redo | Select to undo/redo previous edits. | | Cut/Copy | Select to cut or copy elements within the same or different documents. | | Paste Before/After | Select to add the element from the clipboard above/below the currently selected element. | | Paste As Child | Select to add the element from the clipboard as the first child to the currently selected element. | | Delete | Select to remove the selected element from the tree. | | Go To... | Select to [jump to a specific element](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Jump_to_Specific_Data_Node.htm). | | Find/Replace | Use to specify the text to search for and replace. |
4. Click Save Data As () on the toolbar to save your changes.


When working with a JSON document, on the toolbar of the Analyzer/Data pane, you can alternate between views to choose one that works best for your business needs. By default, your data is represented in a Tree View (), where you can expand/collapse elements and differentiate data types by labels. You can also view JSON data in a [business-friendly form](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Data_Business_Perspective.htm) ().

---

### Data Files

<a id="work-with-data-files-using-analyzer-pane"></a>

### Work with Data Files Using Analyzer Pane

> 📄 Source: [Book_Work_with_Data_Files_Using_Analyzer_Tab.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Book_Work_with_Data_Files_Using_Analyzer_Tab.htm)

#### Work with Data Files Using Analyzer Pane


This section contains the following information:

---

<a id="create-new-data-files"></a>

#### Create New Data Files

> 📄 Source: [Create_New_Data_Files.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Create_New_Data_Files.htm)

##### Create New Data Files


You can create a new data file or load a data file in the data view by using the Data Wizard.


To load a data file:

1. Start SpecBuilder.
2. Open the guideline for which you want to create or load a data file.
3. Click the Analyzer/Data pane. The Welcome to the Data Wizard wizard page is displayed.
4. Select Load an existing Data File option to load the data file, or click Retrieve Data from Database to use the data from a database.
5. Enter the file name or use Browse (...) to select the file.
6. The file is loaded into the data view.


To create a new data file by using the Data Generator:

1. Start SpecBuilder.
2. Open the guideline for which you want to create or load the data file.
3. Click the Analyzer/Data pane. The Welcome to the Data Wizard wizard page is displayed.
4. Select the Generate new Test Data option.
5. Follow the Data Generator Wizard as given in [Data Generator Help](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Data_Generator_Overview.htm).


To create a new data file without using the Data Generator:


Note This option does not use the data dictionary and does not provide much flexibility in creating data.

1. Start SpecBuilder.
2. Open the guideline for which you want to create or load the data file.
3. Click the Analyzer/Data pane. The Welcome to the Data Wizard wizard page is displayed.
4. Select the Create New Data Using Data Editor option. The following dialog box is displayed.
5. Select your option, and then click Finish to create the data and load it into the data view.


For XML and DFF guidelines files that were generated from the database you have an option to pull data from the specified database.


To retrieve data from Database:

1. Start SpecBuilder.
2. Open DFF or XML guideline generated from the database.
3. Click the Analyzer/Data tab. The Welcome to the Data Wizard wizard page is displayed.
4. Select the Retrieve Data from Database option.
5. For the DFF guidelines, provide record parsing information. For more information on how to do it, please see [Record Parsing Information](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Record_Parsing_Information_Translator.htm).
6. Click Next.

---

<a id="add-or-edit-nodes"></a>

#### Add or Edit Nodes

> 📄 Source: [Add_or_Edit_Nodes.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Add_or_Edit_Nodes.htm)

##### Add or Edit Nodes


To edit nodes in the data file, right-click the node to edit. The context menu contains options for modifying the data nodes.


*[Image: Edit nodes]*

- Insert Sibling: This adds the new node immediately after the currently-selected node at the same level. The types of nodes that can be inserted as a sibling depends on the selected node and its parent.
- Append Child: This adds the new node immediately below the current node as a the first child to the selected node. Types of nodes available to be inserted as a child depend on the selected node.
- Update Children Count: Click to update the number of segments, functional groups or transactions.
- Make Automatically Updating: Click to automatically update the number of segments, functional groups or transactions.
- Edit: This option is only available at the element node level. It allows you to change the data in the element.
- Cut and Copy: With these options you can cut or copy nodes from one file to another or within the same file.
- Paste Before/Paste After: These commands add one or more of the cut or copied nodes as siblings either before or after the current node, providing that the node type is valid.


*[Image: Paste nodes]*

- Paste As Child: This command adds the nodes which are in workspace as the first child to the currently selected node, provided the node type is valid child of the selected node.
- Go to: Click to launch Go to dialog box. For more information about this option, see [Jump to Specific Data Node](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Jump_to_Specific_Data_Node.htm).
- Find/Replace: Opens the Find and Replace dialog box. For more information about this option, see [Search and Replace](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Search_and_Replace.htm).
- Edit Delimiters: Opens the [Edit Delimiters](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/DataExplorer_Edit_Delimiters.htm) dialog box to allow you to change the delimiters per interchange.
- Properties: Click to launch the Properties dialog box.

---

<a id="jump-to-specific-data-node"></a>

#### Jump to Specific Data Node

> 📄 Source: [Jump_to_Specific_Data_Node.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Jump_to_Specific_Data_Node.htm)

##### Jump to Specific Data Node


You can move directly to a specific data node in a data file.


To jump to a specific data node:

1. In the Analyzer/Data pane, right-click on a node, and then click Go To ().
2. In the Go to Node dialog box, select the following options:

---

<a id="view-node-properties"></a>

#### View Node Properties

> 📄 Source: [View_Node_Properties.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/View_Node_Properties.htm)

##### View Node Properties


You can view node properties for the root nodes (Transaction, Group, Interchange).


To view node properties:

- Right-click the node, and then click Properties.


Note Node properties are available in the read-only mode.

---

<a id="modify-data-files"></a>

#### Modify Data Files

> 📄 Source: [Analyzer_Modify_Data_Files.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Analyzer_Modify_Data_Files.htm)

##### Modify Data Files


You can modify a data file in the Analyzer/Data pane.


To modify a file, right-click on the node to modify and choose a menu option.


The following menu items are available:  

- Insert Before: Depending on the selected node, this context menu provides option to add items above the currently selected node. Loops are not shown in the context menu, because in EDI data file loop is based on the trigger segment. When data file is re-analyzed then appropriate Loop node icon is displayed based on the guideline.
- Insert After: Depending on the selected node, this context menu provides option to add items below the currently selected node. Loops are not shown in the context menu, because in EDI data file loop is based on the trigger segment. When data file is re-analyzed then appropriate Loop node icon is displayed based on the guideline.
- Append Child: Depending on the selected node, this context menu provides option to add an item as the first child node to the selected node.
- Edit: Enables you to edit the content of the node. If the node contains children nodes then the node subtree is also expanded. If the node's Content Type (see explanation later in the topic) is a macro, the edit operation enables you to change the macro or combine the macros to generate data value.
- Content Type: Use this command to change the internal representation of the node. An element or segment node can be represented internally as either a simple type that contains the data value itself or as a macro that generates the data value when needed. The output to a data file continues to be the data value itself. The macro capability enables the node value to be updated by the application based on user-defined macro combination. E.g. the count of Functional Groups in an Interchange can be made a macro and get updated automatically when the data file is edited. A macro node is represented with a different gray color icon. The Binary Content Type is available only when the element in the guideline is defined as Binary type.
- Go to Next Occurrence: Use this command to jump to the next node of the same type. Same type is defined as data nodes that are linked to the same guideline node. The command runs across transaction and functional group boundaries - for example, if you have a data file that contains 3 functional groups with 4 transactions each and the current node is the first ST segment in the transaction, this command allows you to jump to the first of the 11 ST segments that follow.
- Go to Prev Occurrence: Use this command to jump to the previous occurrence of data linked to the same guideline node.
- Undo and Redo: Use these commands to undo or redo previous edits.
- Cut and Copy: This menu command now allows you to cut, copy, and paste nodes between different documents as well as within the same document.
- Paste Before: Adds the node from the clipboard above the currently selected node.
- Paste After: Adds the node from the clipboard below the currently selected node.
- Paste As Child: Adds the node from the clipboard as the first child to the currently selected node, provided the node type is a valid child of the selected node.
- Delete: Removes the currently selected node from the tree.
- Go To: Use this command to jump to a specific data node. See [Jump to Specific Data Node](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Jump_to_Specific_Data_Node.htm) for more details.
- Find/Replace: Use to specify text to search for and replace.
- Edit Delimiters: Lets you change the delimiters in the data file. Delimiters can be different for each interchange. Segment delimiters are not displayed on the user interface. When the file is saved, it uses the delimiters indicated in the dialog box.

<a id="save-data-files"></a>

##### Save Data Files


You can save the data file from the Analyzer/Data pane.


To save the data:

2. Choose a file name from the list or type a new name for the file in the File name box.
3. Click Save.

<a id="view-generated-xdata"></a>

#### View Generated XData

> 📄 Source: [View_Generated_XData.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/View_Generated_XData.htm)

##### View Generated XData


To view XML and HTML XData:

- Click Analyzer/Data pane.
- On the Options menu (), click Generate XData (XML), and then clickAlways.
- Generate the data.
- On the toolbar, click XData ().


Additional radio button lets you switch between XML and HTML views. With the Report Template (XSLT) drop-down list, you can select from various report templates that have been registered.

<a id="view-xml-and-html-logical-data-reports"></a>

#### View XML and HTML Logical Data Reports

> 📄 Source: [View_XML_and_HTML_Logical_Data_Reports.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/View_XML_and_HTML_Logical_Data_Reports.htm)

##### View XML and HTML Logical Data Reports


To view XML and HTML logical data reports:

- Click Analyzer/Data pane.
- On the Options menu (), select Generate Logical Data Report, and select Always.
- Generate the data and run Analyzer.
- On the toolbar, click LDNS Report ().


Additional radio button lets you switch between XML and HTML views. With the Report Template (XSLT) drop-down list, you can select from various report templates that have been registered. You can also create your own template files by using XSLT or contact support to have a custom template created. See [Data View Options Report StyleSheet Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Data_View_Options_Report_StyleSheet_Tab.htm) for information on registering a custom template file.
